

The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming

This book gives novice coders an understanding of the methods and techniques used in professional games development. Projects are designed to help develop and strengthen problem solving and basic C/C++ skills. This book also helps develop familiarity with Microsoft Visual Studio’s ability to target and use fixed/restricted hardware, which are key skills in console development.
This book allows readers to increase their confidence as game programmers by walk‑ ing them through increasingly involved game concepts, while maintaining the under‑ standing that despite the increased complexity, the core methods remain consistent with the advancement of the technology. It also demonstrates underlying principles and some pitfalls of game coding, in practical, step‑by‑step ways, to increase exposure and confi‑ dence in game coding concepts and technical advances.
This second edition has been fully updated in line with recent hardware changes, with new and updated cross platform code examples hosted at the companion website, which can also be compiled and run on a standard Windows PC, without a target system if the user does not have one readily available.
This book will be useful to all novice coders seeking to learn the fundamentals of C/ C++ game programming.
The companion material for this book is available at www.scratchpadgames.net.

Brian Beuken is a Senior Lecturer at Breda University of Applied Sciences, Netherlands, where he teaches game and console programming. Formerly, he worked as a Programmer/ Project Manager, and Head of Development at Rockpool Games and Managing Director/ Studio Head/Executive Producer at Virtucraft Studios Ltd. He has developed games for multiple companies including Nintendo, EA, Sega, THQ, Codemasters and many more.


The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming Using Target‑based Development

xiv	Contents


Brian Beuken – Who Is He?















Brian Beuken is a veteran games developer, having started in the early 80s writ‑ ing his first games on the venerable Sinclair ZX81. Self‑taught as many were at the time, Brian wrote games in Basic and Assembler, selling them via mail order before branching out to form his own small company specialising in the con‑ version of projects from one popular machine to another. A chance to work for Ocean Software in Manchester England, then one of the largest games compa‑ nies around, saw Brian leave his native Scotland and become a full‑time game programmer, staying in the centre of the tech bubble that was Manchester and working for several companies producing a host of projects in quick succession.
Eventually, the Manchester bubble burst as companies began to merge and move away. Brian became a well‑established freelance coder specialising in Z80 systems and handheld devices from Nintendo and Sega, before again taking a leap into entrepreneurship and forming his own company in nearby Bolton, Virtucraft Ltd., later Virtucraft Studios Ltd.
Virtucraft grew from 3 to 30+people in the space of 4 years, until once again the bubble burst and the company was forced to close. Brian went on to become the Head of Development at an emerging mobile games company which was later sold and became part of the mighty Square Enix. But Brian had left before that happened, unhappy with the distance from development in the management role, he returned to coding and once again entered the freelance market for sev‑ eral years again specialising in handheld consoles.
A chance encounter with a tutor at Breda University of Applied Sciences (BUas), then known as NHTV, in Breda, The Netherlands, resulted in Brian being offered a teaching position at the still new International Games Architecture and Design (IGAD) program they had established to bring game development skills that the industry needed for education. Finding the program offered far more


DOI: 10.1201/9781003527695-1                                                                                                                                 1
than he’d seen in any comparable education, Brian signed up, thinking he’d try it for a year. Seventeen years later he’s still there, still coding and occasionally add‑ ing to his 75+ published titles while also developing an insanely large “aaa” (not quite AAA) project for Raspberry Pi on www.patreon.com/brianbeuken.
But the “1 year” stint has proven to be most rewarding, and Brian continues to enjoy watching his students find the joy of game development which they can take with them to an industry that sorely needs more programmers.
Brian lives in Breda, The Netherlands, almost speaks Dutch, but still can’t pronounce his immigrant great grandfather’s Dutch surname properly, much to the amusement of his Dutch friends. He is married to multilinguist Jirawan who speaks Dutch for him when needed.
Aside from programming, Brian’s hobbies include programming, teaching programming, learning new programming concepts and complaining about new programming concepts. He also plays music with a couple of local bands, playing guitar well and singing very badly. His bandmates appreciate it when he sticks to playing guitar.

“From Hello World to Halo – It’s Just Code!” Who Is This Book For?
Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming, Using Target‑based Development, is quite a mouthful, isn’t it? As a title, it’s also making a few promises that it probably can’t keep, since there are so many definitions of what are the funda‑ mental skills a game programmer needs to have. But it’s my view there are a few things that can get people up and running and develop their hunger for learning, and it’s those things I want to bring to you here. The content, however, is by no means exhaustive that would take several volumes, and regular yearly updates, to keep up with new practices; but it is a selection of highlights of what we need to know to get things going and allow you to see what can be added with a bit more research.
We’re not going to focus on the minutiae of specific games, or hardware capabilities, but we will present overviews of how things can be done and some technical tricks we can use to make things easier or better.
This is for people who want to be game programmers but probably don’t quite know how to do it or feel a little daunted that their coding skills don’t really let them explore their game‑creation skills. Although this is very much a begin‑ ner’s book, it is not really aimed at a total novice who has never programmed before; you should be able to understand at least the basic concepts and syntax of C/C++ programming and be open to learning more from other sources.
There are some excellent beginner’s books I recommend to all my students, such as Michael Dawson’s excellent Beginning C++Through Game Programming, which though light on actual graphic gaming, is a superb foundation for C++. I will give some very simple getting started examples and build on those, so even the most code‑wary beginner should pick things up as they go.
None of the code in this book is complex; indeed, it can be comfortably argued it’s oversimplified, it’s designed as a jumping off point for a novice to expand their knowledge and most of the initial explanations are going to be understandable even for a total beginner. I’ll start slow and explain much at first, but the pace will pick up as we get going. I’ll let the online source code go into more detail as I explain the overall intent of what we are doing rather than the



2	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
specific functions. If you still don’t understand the syntax of the code, you should undertake a beginner’s coding course, there are several online courses. I’m espe‑ cially pleased (and biased, I wrote parts of it) with this one: https://www.3dgep. com/cpp‑fast‑track‑1‑getting‑started/.
Also, we need to explain what is meant by Target Based Development; in this case, it means we’re going to code projects on simple SBCs (single board computers), more specifically, but not exclusively, Raspberry Pi. Despite the title, this isn’t a book solely about using a target but the use of a cheap target system is a means to an end to encourage the reader to limit expectations and work within tight constraints which game programmers, especially console program‑ mers, must work with. I want primarily to focus on gameplay concepts and game structures, which will let us get games up and running quickly.
However, we do have to introduce some increasingly more technical con‑ cepts later, when we’re a bit more comfortable, since most of these technical concepts will have a direct impact on the performance of your games. You will need to know just enough to avoid some pitfalls and get things up and running correctly.
SBCs are usually quite simple systems, so building a working knowledge of the generic hardware to produce graphics, sound and data storage is generally easier to learn on them, than it would be on your up to the minute PC which will shield you from errors by virtue of massive processing performance and near unlimited memory.
Once understood, all the concepts and projects in this book are easily trans‑ ferrable to any development target where the reader can stretch their growing skills on more powerful systems while still being mindful of the need to work within constraints of hardware which are hard to push, as well as personal limits that should always be pushed.
But SBCs are fun to work with, cheap to acquire and present a real sense of achievement when you make them to do more than just act as media servers or control units.
Most importantly, this is not a how to do x, with y kind of book. I want to take you through a journey of discovery, mine as well as yours, and provide sug‑ gestions and working examples on how to do things that games need, and let you decide if the approach I’ve taken is valid. I want to make you question things and hopefully come to different conclusions, using what I supply as a base for debate and expansion rather than a gospel to be followed. When working with begin‑ ners, I don’t believe in imposing “the right way”, I prefer to have faith in, “this works for me, can I make it better?”. The “right way”, for you at least, will come with practice and the joy of achievement.


What Are We Gonna Do?
For the last 17 years, I’ve been teaching beginner‑level game programmers how to write computer games. Not so much the actual languages used in programming but the principles of creating games. Starting with simple space shooter games, scrolling platformers, maze games, character animation games, etc.
Eventually, at some point in their development when they start to move beyond such simple themes, and their confidence is high enough, I then encour‑ age them to take their first steps in writing more complex 3D immersive games on consoles, such as the Nintendo Switch, Microsoft Xbox and Sony PlayStation 5.



Brian Beuken – Who Is He?	3
It sounds like a massive leap to teach beginners how to make a simple shooter game in 2D to then write games on the most powerful consoles on the market, but in fact the progression is really quite simple. Coding is coding … once you have the basics in your head, the rest of it is down to understanding how to get your code to work on different machines and with increasingly larger projects, only the levels of complexity change.
Internally, you could not find more different machines than a PC, a Nintendo Switch and a PlayStation 5. But the basic ideas of getting something to appear on screen, move around under some kind of control and process some form of logic are what the game programmers are really trying to do. That is independent of any machine architecture!
Via various concepts such as Application Programming Interfaces (APIs), Libraries and Engines, a lot of the complexity of how the screen objects appear is hidden from the programmer and accessed via simpler means, though there is always a chance to unhide it, usually causing untold damage as a result. We generally leave the technical side of the machines to tools and graphics coders who make those API’s, Libraries and Engines available to us, so we can focus on the simpler concepts of getting our chomping pizza to move around the maze collecting his trademarked pills.
When you move away from the worry of the technical issues of how to draw things and start to abstract your thinking toward making, a “thing”, do the “thing” you want that “thing” to do, you find yourself able to explain and under‑ stand your game concept far easier. What you understand, you can code!
For some time, I wanted to write a tutorial that could take some of my meth‑ ods of learning as you go/do, taking beginners from their first simple projects to producing fully working games on consoles. But there are some roadblocks I must overcome. Not least of which is while PC coding is easy enough to enter, and I would have no problem converting my first year courses into a simple tuto‑ rial. Game console coding, which I also teach, is essentially a closed shop, open only to industry and a few elite educational institutions.
So I thought, when I wrote the first edition, it might be an interesting jour‑ ney to take a machine I’d never worked on before and document my way through it, re‑creating the out of my depth feel many new programmers have when con‑ fronted by a blank screen, while at the same time giving the reader a chance to understand the basic concepts of game programming I’ve been involved with for over 40 years.
First things first, I needed a target machine…. In my normal game develop‑ ment career, I’ve worked mostly with consoles from Nintendo and more recently Sony. So, my basic techniques of coding are firmly entrenched in the idea of writ‑ ing games on an editing system that you don’t run the code on.
For the vast majority of my career, I’ve used what we call a development machine, more often than not a PC, to write the code, which in turn is transmit‑ ted via some kind of network link to the actual target machine the games will be played on, usually some form of specialist development kit that allows communi‑ cations and debugging to take place. All the main console systems use Dev Kits, and I’m incredibly lucky to have access to them all.
That creates something of a problem. The general public are not actually allowed to purchase these dev kits, also I’m not allowed to talk about them or even show you a photo, having Non‑Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) all properly signed up. So even if I wanted to, I can’t tell you how to write games on a PS5 or Nintendo Switch, but I do very much want to give you a flavour of it.


4	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Looking around at the kind of equipment most beginner programmers have; there are however a few choices. The most obvious are Apple and Android phones. These days almost everyone has one of these, to take selfies or spend a few minutes playing games, so it seems like an ideal choice.
But there are issues with them. Apple devices really work best when using Mac’s and their own idiosyncratic (but by no means bad) Objective C based development tools. These are great tools, but I find them a little too specific for my liking, and since one of the objectives in writing this is to get you, the reader, to be able to work on standard types of machines I feel I should rule them out, perhaps that’s another book.
Android uses Java…I suppose now is a good time to admit I don’t like Java… but the reasons for that will take too long to go into, but I’m certain that in at least one chapter of this book or forum post, I’ll explain and you may feel I’m overreacting…I am of course, but allow me a little bias. I believe Java is a fine lan‑ guage for some things, just not console games. Again, that may be another book.
That said, it’s quite possible to code at a low level on an Android system using the NDK essentially working on the hardware level, however that then raises another issue with Android machines, at the hardware level there is a massive amount of difference from one maker to another, resulting in what are called, abstraction layers between the hardware and Android to maintain compatibility for the Android OS, but less consistency in access methods if you want to deal with hardware direct. Since I want you the reader to have as few issues with the hardware as possible, and not be wrestling with it, it means we cannot be 100% certain the code is going to work on every Android system. That, more than Java, rules out Android for us.
Common devices exist, there are ways to hook them up to the PC but do they provide a good experience that simulates development for consoles?
I don’t think so, Apple comes close and indeed for all basic definitions most Apple devices are small consoles but the Objective C issue and confusing tools make it a bit too specialized. If you want to do apps for Apple devices, I think there are more than enough excellent tutorials out there.
Most industry programming is done in C/C++ so that’s what I want to work with. Yes…before someone pipes up, you CAN do C/C++ on Apple and Android devices, it’s just not as easy as I want it to be, and while I don’t anticipate this journey to be without incident, I prefer to keep to paths I know I can travel.
This book is intended to be a tutorial…So I wanted to find a simple and easy system to work with.
I didn’t find one!
And there’s a good reason for that, pretty much any computer system has its own drawbacks when it comes to getting it setup, often this is software based and that in turn led me to another decision I needed to make before starting. What kind of standard development IDE (Integrated Development Environment) was I going to use…I wanted to use Microsoft Visual Studio.
Why? Well because I like it! I’m used to it, almost every other game devel‑ oper not doing Apple or Android product uses it, and moreover…it’s now free! Free is good. Especially since I am going to encourage you to spend a little bit of money to fund your journey into the joys of game programming,
It’s not everyone’s favourite IDE, in fact I know Pros who will spend hours explaining to you just how bad it is, but truthfully if you’re a beginner and all you want is a simple system to write code, press a deploy button and watch the fireworks…It does just that. If you’re a Pro, you want to totally investigate the


Brian Beuken – Who Is He?	5
code, be able to search for sections that contain information and so on…Visual Studio does that too, it won’t stop me cursing it from time to time, but I never met a programmer who was 100% happy with his tools.
As with most things in life, once you get used to something, you don’t like to change. Mac users love X‑code (mostly) and PC users love Visual Studio (mostly). We’ll just use what works.
So, our development system is chosen, we are going to use a PC or a Mac, run‑ ning Windows and Visual Studio. But we don’t really need to worry about what kind of Windows. During the first edition of this book, I was still using Windows 8.1 on my main system, and occasionally on my laptop running Windows 10. I’d just upgraded to Visual Studio 2015 and I was sure it’s going to annoy the hell out of me for all the things it did different from Visual Studio 2013. That anger passed and I’m now bitterly complaining about Visual Studio 2022. Programmers like to complain about upgrades. Some of the images in this edition are from the first edition’s Visual Studio 2013 set up but unless they show totally different content from the latest 2022 version, I probably won’t change them.
Now for the tricky bit, what’s the target system going to be…As I said before when I started the first edition, I wanted to use a machine I’d never used before so that I could also experience some of the things a new coder will come across. It also needed to be able to hook up to the PC somehow and it needed to be available to the general public.

2π |!2π
Ok, let’s stop beating about the bush, after all I already said and the section head‑ ing gives it away. I chose the Raspberry Pi because it’s cheap, it’s freely available all over the world, it connects to a PC via network cables or wirelessly, it has con‑ sistent hardware, so what works on one is almost sure to work on another even if a few differences in speed happen, and in my opinion, it’s a machine which has largely been ignored by the games development community. So, you’re going to be treading in largely virgin sand, that’s quite exciting.
I need a consistent, fun bit of hardware, with suficient power, reasonable graphic abilities and on‑board memory to create a range of decent little games to learn with, The Raspberry Pi gives me all that, and most of its clones are close enough to also give us some insight into the fragmentation issues, in a small enough scale to cope!
Now it also must be said that once I settled on the Raspberry Pi, I had my eyes opened up to the fact that there are several Raspberry Pi type machines out there, in fact, there is a thriving community of similar small board System on Chip (SoC) machines with their own communities. So, I expanded my remit a little to include as many of the main ones as I could find with a simple limit of cost. I only looked at units I could buy for under $100.
If you have one of these other systems, then it’s only fair that I make sure what we do here is going to work on them too, so long as they run some form of Linux and have OpenGLES 3.1 for their Graphics systems. We “should” be able to get our games to work on them too. I’ll try to give a summary of machines I’ve tried and maintain an update on the support site.
Of course, technology never stands still, and as I was a quarter of the way in writing the first edition of the book the Raspberry Pi Foundation announced a new model, the Raspberry Pi3 and as usual it sold out within hours of its announcement. Not quite as big a leap in performance as the 2 was over the 1,



6	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
but still another boost in performance for the same price is much appreciated. Since that book’s release, we’ve now had both the Raspberry Pi4 and the Pi5. And that has required some rethinking on how to work with the older projects from before.
I guess most of you will now be on model 6 by the time this comes out. But the nice thing about the later Raspberry range is that aside from memory and speed, they all are based on the same hardware principles and are a little more standard than the earlier Pi3. So even though I’m going to continue with my Raspberry Pi Models 4B and 5B for now, I’ll be swapping over to the Model 6B in a year or so when it comes, everything in the book will be checked on the latest models before it goes to the printers and any issues found will be documented on my support site for the book.
At this point, I must confess that despite my expectation that the Pi4 would not be too different from the Pi3, it was. So much of the older edition content is not going to work quite as well on the Pi4. Basically, the GPU setups are different and that does create some conflicts. But there are details on the support website on how to re‑work the setup code and get the older Pi3 code to run on a Pi4. Or just follow this content in this edition with the newer setups.
But from this point on, we assume the Pi4B is the base level model and since its OS is now an almost standard version of Debian Bookworm (at the time of writing) I think it’s a bit safer to say the later models will be compliant. Fingers crossed.
Since a big part of the thinking behind writing this book is to introduce some of the concepts and limitations of working on consoles, I set myself some limita‑ tions. As far as possible I plan not to use any paid or specialist external 3rd party libraries, though consoles do sometimes use external libs (audio for example, commonly uses 3rd party libs even though SDKs usually provide some support). Wherever possible, the only libraries I will add will be on the RaspberryOS/ Linux OS repositories already or freely installable.

You Call This Code?
A word about my coding style in this book. Aside from some quite deliberately poor design1 choices and (semi) deliberate errors, I fully intend to make in order to show you how to improve, the style is, ermm, my own!
I tend to write in a mixture of C and C++, partly because many consoles and embedded toolchains I use still use C, and partly because it’s a style I have found my students pick up quite well before fully adopting C++. I find full Object Oriented Programming (OOP) C++ to be a little unreadable and confusing, espe‑ cially for beginners. Also sticking rigidly to pure OOP can have some impact on performance. I also don’t want to have to spend two or three pages to explain why C++ wants you to do things a certain way, before we can move forward. So, I’ll continue with my C with classes approach until we find it does not fit our needs. But I will be introducing more structured C++ as the book goes on.
The thing about a computer language is this; it is there to make your life easier! If you find it easier to write in mostly C…do so! Never let anyone stop you unless you are in a team doing OOP. But on your own at this stage, I encourage


1  It really is intentional…well most of it…some of it, a few things are clearly done to highlight bad practice, and a few things are just bad practice. If you’re spotting it. Well done!



Brian Beuken – Who Is He?	7
you to simply write code that works, something that makes sense to you, at least until you are 100% sure you know what you are doing, then you can explore the majesty, or lunacy, of coding standards. With practice you will naturally start to see patterns in your code that will lead to improvement and understanding of the more advanced quirks of any language, once you start to see faults in your own code and that becomes 2nd nature to you, you’re a coder.
But my C with classes style using OOP when needed is functional, my aim here is to make things clear, to give you room to expand and improve on the basic systems. I’m also not a hardware expert, so much of the information I am going to pass on is taken from a basic user’s viewpoint, based on what I have found the machine can do within the project I am producing. I am quite sure a technical graphic coder could get and give a lot more info on the tech side of things but that’s not really what I want to focus on here.
That’s not to say we should not learn new things, I always aim to improve, but I’m usually just too busy making games! It’s supposed to be fun! This means a lot of the early samples in the book will be mostly C based to tie in with the traditional starter projects found in most textbooks. I’ll introduce some classes and then hopefully some more recognisable standard C++ as we progress. I don’t use a lot of C++ 17 usually, but I will introduce some more useful and up‑to‑date C++17 concepts when we need them, as we go through the projects. I hear that there is now a C++23… meh, mañana!
If you want to write tidy sharable code for the team, I suggest a CS (Computer Science) course somewhere. If you just want to gain confidence in coding, make something work and improve your understanding of how to make games. Read on, and feel free to rework my code when you are confident enough to do it. Also take note, most of the technical stuff we have to deal with, in the 3D sections in particular, can ALL be done better! I’m giving you functional, but deliberately not optimal systems, it leaves you open to play with and improve things and I will suggest some ways for you to explore and research, so you get a bigger confidence boost from doing it yourself.

Uggh It’s All So Old
One other thing you need to also consider is that some of the libraries, file and data formats used in the book are older concepts that don’t have too much traction any more in modern game dev. So why present them here? Simple, we are using target machines that do not have the horsepower of a modern computer system in fact they are about 10–15 years behind the current power level of even a modest PC. A great many cutting‑edge concepts need a lot of horsepower or large data stores to function, we just don’t have those resources available to us.
So it’s quite appropriate that we are going to use some techniques from the good old days, which are still perfectly functional and once grasped will make updating these methods a journey you will want to take with foundation knowl‑ edge to help you.
Coding and using things that work will give you a sense of achievement and quick visible results, rather than a sense of frustration that tends to cause new coders to give up. We will enhance some of the older ideas with our more mod‑ ern systems and methods where viable, so you are always free and encouraged to try to use more modern concepts as soon as you feel you are capable of writing



8	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
them. If your target can handle them! But I am deliberately presenting relatively simple methods which are compact, fast and effective even on the lowliest of tar‑ get systems.
Finally, though most of the current range of SBCs are multi‑core and get a significant boost when using parallel processing, I’m not going to explore that in too much detail in the projects presented here, though I will explain the concepts and some of the projects should be suitable for parallel processing if the reader feels they want to tinker. I do this because parallel coding works best if the proj‑ ects are built with this in mind, but as this book is targeting beginners, I want to avoid potentially confusing technical concepts until such time as the begin‑ ner starts to understand what they are doing. There’s no rush, when you “get it”, you’re free to do anything you want to the code and enjoy the achievement of improvement for yourself.

What Do We Need to Get Started?
As with every new technical challenge, the first thing you need is some cash, you’re going to have to spend a bit of money. Not much, trust me, as a Scot, with Dutch ancestry, spending money is as painful to me as it is to you, so we’ll limit it to what we need.
Throughout the book I am going to refer to our target machine as the Target or Raspberry Pi, which due purely to its massive market lead, I am going to assume you will be using. But with very few exceptions, any SBC with on‑board/ integrated GPU (Graphics Processing Unit), keyboard and mouse connections and a display output can be used. So long as it’s running some form of Linux, which we only need for file and I/O handling, and most important is using and has drivers for OpenGLES 3.1 or higher as a graphic API (Application Programming Interface). This will all be explained in more detail later.
I don’t have the foggiest idea how Linux itself works, so I’m only going to use what I need to use to make the code work. On other SBCs, I tend to use versions of Debian, since that’s basically what the Raspberry Pi uses, but a few machines prefer Ubuntu. As I say, once we are running our code we don’t care about the OS. I’ll limit my usage to getting the IP address, installing some libraries, basic I/O and making sure it has a compiler and required drivers on it, some things may need to be downloaded, but are all available for free!
There are some issues with the different SBCs and Linux methods of setting up rendering windows, but for the most part all these machines provide drivers that make that possible, usually in some sample OpenGLES 3.1 code you can find in the maker’s downloads.
Some of the most popular SBCs are detailed on the support site and I will have personally tried all the samples on them so can give good feedback on any setup and execution issues you might come across.
Once individual drivers are set up and installed, after any specific initialising systems are called the code samples in here should work on any valid target with few issues. However, computers are interesting things and even two machines of the same model from the same maker but with different production runs may display differences or have different user setups, so it would be naive of me to suggest everything is going to work on every machine on a first attempt, but as far as possible, it should. Any significant differences in machine setup or installation will be covered as much as I am able to on the support site.



Brian Beuken – Who Is He?	9
The Target
We need an SBC of course; and as I say, for the most part I will assume Raspberry Pi, 60 million+ users would indicate that most of you are using that. So that’s your first purchase, if you haven’t done so already, you need to do this now. At the time of writing, I’m using the current primary model, a Raspberry Pi 400, but there is also now a Raspberry Pi 5B and 500 which will dominate by the time you read this, aside from being (very much) faster, it only has one minor kink in its rendering systems you need to care about. I also have picked up quite a few of the so‑called Raspberry Pi “beaters” that are on the market, such as Nano Pi, Banana Pi, Orange Pi, Pine etc. which also use a Linux OS. One awesome thing about these little SBCs is most are pocket money cheap so adding to my collection is proving to be a fun hobby. I’ll document any issues I find on the support site. I do know that some of the others are faster or have more impressive graphic systems and a few have SATA and USB3 to make disk access a factor, but I strongly sug‑ gest we all stay with the Raspberry Pi’s for learning and then transition to others if that’s what you want to use.
Buying an SBC on its own is a bit useless though, it usually needs an SD card, to act as its boot drive and storage medium, ideally preformatted with some form of Linux, which on the Raspberry Pi is called RaspberryOS (there are other options, but RaspberryOS is easy to use). It also needs some means to power it, ideally a 5v, 4–5Amp, wall wart.
Internet access for your target is needed to install libraries and updates. Wi‑Fi is usually standard now but a wired network connection to your PC is pre‑ ferred for faster communication between the target and dev system.
I’d recommend a case to keep the Raspberry Pi safe and tidy. These are minor extra costs and usually most sellers will offer you a bundle.
Remember that the Raspberry Pi is a computer…therefore, it will also need a display, a keyboard and a mouse to get the most out of it, I tend to have a few broken and bashed keyboards lying around, which met their fate during some horror bug hunting session, but any simple cheap keyboard and mouse will work. We will be doing our coding on our own PCs so the Raspberry Pi’s keyboard and mouse only need to be functional, and you can pick up really cheap combination keyboard/mousepads.
As noted, your target also needs to have a display, it may be you have a monitor on your PC that has dual HDMI inputs or you want to use a KVM system; this will work, but will not be effective when you want to debug on one screen and see the project running on another. Display switching between the two images will not be a satisfactory experience. A small monitor with your Raspberry Pi hooked up beside your main monitor is ideal. There are also some very serviceable low‑cost LCD panels you can hook up to the boards directly but don’t get less than 7”, our early games are mostly going to run in 1024´640 pixel resolution which we’ll scale to the screen’s resolution most of the time and the small sub 7” screens can’t really handle even that low‑res making scaling look odd.
If you plan to use a target system you already had for a while, and it currently is set up with lots of apps which are set to run in the background, it would be wise to create another project based SD to boot clean, so that we make sure our target machine is not using resources on other applications when we are developing. Throughout the book, I am assuming a total clean fresh install of RaspberryOS or your usual flavour of Linux, with all default settings.



10	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
The Development Machine
Any desktop or laptop‑style PC can be used; we are only interested in its use as an editor, so a keyboard, mouse and display are all you really need. The only hard‑ ware consideration is that it needs to be able to network in some way, via standard cables or wirelessly.
Next, you’ll need a copy of Visual Studio. This pre‑supposes you are running on a computer with a Windows OS. Some models of Macs can run Windows, so pop off to Microsoft’s site and see if you can download a version, it can sometimes be obtained for free or on discount for students… Visual Studio itself is free in its rich featured Community version from www.visualstudio.com.
I must take a moment to thank Microsoft for this; it really is a great gesture to make such professional tools free to everyone.
Visual Studio can handle all our connections to our target, so it’s pretty much all the software we need on our PC to start with but you will find a need for some graphic editing and simpler editing tools as you progress.

Why Can’t I Use Linux for Everything?
You can, be my guest I don’t mind at all, the problem is I don’t have a clue about Linux, I don’t really want to have a clue about Linux, and you will discover as you read this that I’m never going to have a clue about Linux. I’m totally not qualified to give you any real insight or advice on using Linux. Which means you’re on your own? This is kind of the way most Linux coders seem to work!
We use Linux in some form on our target machines, simply because it pro‑ vides an easy use of Secure Shell connections (SSH) via a network, as well as a means to create a graphical interface to let our projects run and access some input and output functions.
I’m not anti‑Linux in any way; it’s a fine OS, especially as it’s basically free. It’s just not one I personally have ever really wanted to use or found easy to keep up to date with. I know only a few basic features which are used in the book, any‑ thing I don’t know I’m going to Google for, just like everyone else.

Support Website
Almost all the code in the book and some other things that didn’t make it in the final edit will be available online at my website www.scratchpadgames.net. Most of the “missing” parts are things I want you to enter yourself for the practice. I’ll also maintain an errata and update on systems or tools I use, colour versions of all screenshots, as well as final and much more complete versions of all the demos in the book for you to download, review and try out.
For brevity, the listings in the book are sometimes incomplete or have had formatting altered to fit on a page. Later in the book when you should be more proficient, I’m not even going to provide the code as a listing, you can review the downloaded source code itself which will be commented and tie in with the text. I’ll provide suggestions on how to deal with a problem and some out‑ lines, confident that you already have the requirements from previous examples. Downloading the demo base code will allow you to get your systems set up ready to add your own additions as the book outlines.
But there will be “final” versions of the demos available so you can compare your efforts with mine. I only ask that you please please please, don’t just look



Brian Beuken – Who Is He?	11
at the final versions and cut and paste the code, you really will not benefit from doing that. Work with the support site, in conjunction with the book.
It’s quite probable that I will clean up, tinker with, or fix bugs in the source code after the book goes to print. But don’t worry I will make as few changes to the base online code as possible, and the source code will have descriptive com‑ ments, especially if varies from the printed versions.
Let’s get started now, but remember as you go through the book, have fun, experiment and tinker with everything and develop your own style and be will‑ ing to push for more.

That Difficult Second Edition
Well, no one is more surprised than I, that these mindless scribbles actually found an interested audience, despite a lot of mistakes – both from me and some ques‑ tionable typesetting, which I’ll try to be more careful to check this time round. The book found the very niche audience I was hoping was out there, and slowly, a small community of mad crazy people who loved making their single board computers (SBCs) do graphics has grown.
I honestly wasn’t sure if there was enough interest in coding for fun on sys‑ tems that just don’t have the power to do much. But over eight years later, the response to the book has been incredibly uplifting. There is still a demand to code for the sake of it – and to improve your skills by messing about with cheap systems that let you develop a mindset that isn’t obsessed by what “engine” to use. But here we are: a second edition. It’s going to be much like the first, with – hopefully – all of the errors removed. I am so sorry to the Raspberry Foundation for calling Raspbian, “Rasbian” over and over again in the last edi‑ tion; global spell checking, and dyslexia are a curse. I have immediately fixed that. It’s now called RaspberryOS, so hopefully I won’t be misspelling it this time. The first edition was very much an introduction to some common concepts
of game programming, and we deliberately focused on small SBCs because they are cheap and provide a good simulation of target‑based development, which is common among game console programmers.
That focus allowed us to do some low‑end tech content, like using OpenGLES 2.0 for graphics. We only scratched the surface of what OpenGLES 3.0 could do, as it was still new on SBCs. This time, I’ll try to go into a bit more detail, but still, as before, only as it relates to cool things we can use in our example games. It still



DOI: 10.1201/9781003527695-2                                                                                                                               13
rests with the reader to pick up a proper OpenGL/GLES book to get the most out of their graphics processing units (GPUs). Don’t worry, you really don’t need to know everything a GPU can do. But if, in the course of a project you think, hmm I wonder if I can use compressed textures on this to save some memory, you’ll know where to check for that feature.
This time around, we’re going to drastically reduce our use of OpenGLES 2.0 and take more interest in the rise of OpenGLES 3.x capable machines which are now the go‑to systems for SBCs, especially if you want to do games. And for those of you who don’t have an SBC, all the code will also be available on PC builds for Windows.
Desktop Linux builds as targets, are also simple, since the Pi4/5 is basi‑ cally a Linux system. I will provide some setup instructions on the support site. However, be aware that the overwhelming increase in power of an Intel/AMD PC will hide some of the issues with poor coding practices I try to bring to your attention here.
Also, a small caution if you insist on using your PC as a target, whatever OS you use. We won’t be using full‑fat OpenGL 4.6 on your PC. Since my prime tar‑ get is still the Raspberry 4B, we will stick with OpenGLES 3.1. Modern PCs with reasonably modern GPUs are quite happy using that version of OpenGL, which we will set up using the popular OpenGL library, GLFW.1 If you want to do some research to max out your performance on a bigger machine – and use the maxi‑ mum version of OpenGL available, you can do that after working through this book, using the content here as a starting point for higher levels.
I’ve kept and reworked a few early original OpenGLES 2.0 examples from the first edition, but we will move to OpenGLES 3.x to make things easier when we do 3D. Because the Pi 1/2/3 was a little esoteric in the way it handled some of its start‑up in the first edition, we’ll clean that up and make things a little more generic (hence why we can do more PC stuff this time).
I’ve added content on using the GPU a little more effectively, and additional text and UI rendering topics are also covered and expanded.
There will also be some online content, where I will also set out some exam‑ ples of optimisation, both of our memory and our performance. We’ll take a couple of our unoptimised book projects which just about work and show how we can vastly improve performance by slightly re‑thinking the setup of our projects to avoid bottlenecks and waste. You will find some videos and website content on these topics via the support site or from the eBook’s links.
The new generation of SBC’s extra power allows us to try more modern model loading and animation ideas, letting us move away from the outdated MD2/3 systems used last time. It’s time to put a toe in the water of more complex rigged systems, which are supported by more modern standards of graphics such as FBX and DAE.
There are some new updated code bases to work on this time, though we’ll keep the early starter projects, despite the start with OpenGLES 2.0, to ease the shock of using new hardware. The sooner we get up to speed on what OpenGLES 3.1 brings to the party, the sooner we can start with some new project ideas that can show us how to give our games a bit more visual excitement and take some stress off the CPU.
Much of the software used in the first edition has updated too, OS’s for example; Raspbian Jessie, became Raspbian Stretch, then Raspbian Buster, then

1  https://www.glfw.org/.



14	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
RaspberryOS using Bullseye and more recently Bookworm. I’m hoping I can get this out before the next version, but any issues that crop up will be reported on the support site.
Mainstream Ubuntu and Debian used on other targets were also updated. But the updates to the OS were generally not massive and most were documented on the Scratchpadgames.net support site. A general move away from Xorg to Wayland display systems did, however, make some of the original code a little more challenging to compile and run. So, the book will be expecting you to use Xorg/X11 you can set this in your Raspberry raspi‑config settings. I will work on getting Wayland more friendly to use though, so check on the support site for updates.
Our general target systems are going to continue to be Raspberry Pi, this time the base will be Raspberry Pi 4B/400 for all basic projects, and of course we will also run on the much more powerful Raspberry Pi5. There is no significant difference in usage between the Pi4 and Pi5, though the Pi5 is considerably faster. They both have the advantage of being standard Linux systems, unlike the Pi3 which had a few quirks, especially with its graphics. That is why the Pi3 code dep‑ recated so quickly after the Pi4’s release. The Pi4 doesn’t need to jump through as many hoops to be a viable graphic system and should retain compatibility in its future iterations.
There’s a host of nice new boards out there now, which give great performance for OpenGLES 3.2. They nearly all have graphic drivers but not all are hardware accelerated. Note that I don’t favour one board over another, if it runs OpenGLES and has hardware acceleration, I’m happy to port games to it. But, if it takes me a week to discover it can’t do something fast enough because the driver is emulat‑ ing, I’m much less happy. That and that alone is the reason I use Raspberries. There are much faster more high‑spec systems out there. But if the graphics systems are not better than the Pi, I’d rather not spend a lot of time on them.
As with the first edition though, as long as you have drivers for the correct version of OpenGLES we use here, you can run these demos on pretty much any compliant SBC or Linux PC with very little effort, and it’s good head‑scratching practice for you to try to figure out what’s going wrong if it can’t build and run.

New Tools and Projects?
I covered the use of plug‑in tools in the last book and very much supported using VisualGDB (www.visualGDB) as it was the best way to work when I started writ‑ ing the book. Today, VisualGDB is still my weapon of choice and, personally, gives me the options I expect. But Microsoft has also greatly improved its Visual Studio C++ support for Linux in Visual Studio, from 2017 onward. So much so, that it’s hard to find a lot of differences between the two systems other than cross‑platform toolchains and some unlikely to be used build options, which VisualGDB supports.
So, for this book I’m going to use Visual Studio for Linux (VS4L); it’s free and does the job well; additionally, it will also help us build a standard Windows ver‑ sion of our projects. Two project options for you to use, choose your best weapon. After the explanations, how you build the code is up to you, but I would avoid trying to do more than one version until you grasp the basics of how the build cycle works.
Of course, with Linux and Windows/Visual Studio updates over the years, some required libraries updated and I’ve noted this in my forum updates, and


Getting Started	15
there were some issues with the maths library GLM which forced us to move to C++17 for compiling for a while, before being fixed, but as before I still don’t really want to make much use of any advanced C++ features. This book focuses on core concepts of C++ and only delves into C++17 or higher features that make life a bit easier. You can discover your inner C++ guru in more advanced books on the subject once you are comfortable with the level presented in this book.
The support site at www.scratchpadgames.net has been split into old and new. They share a lot of content of course, but due to code deprecation the older content is no longer available, alternatives are provided though.  As always, the forums will provide you with any help you need or for you to report new errors that are bound to crop in.
If you are brand new to C++ coding, I still recommend Michael Dawson’s book, Beginning C++ Through Game Programming, as an excellent “start here”, kind of book, which is required reading for my university students as their first pre‑lessons textbook. It’s quite light on graphical games but the coding concepts are very relevant to the content presented here. Many other books are available though and on‑line courses; find one that clicks with you. You only need a mini‑ mum understanding of C++ to follow this book, and I do explain most of the beginning code, but the aim here is to help you use the language to make games rather than being a study in the language itself.

New Games?
Am I adding new games? No, there’s not really much point. The projects from the first edition remain but have been updated or changed, mainly to take advantage of OpenGLES 3.1 and a bit of optimisation, but we’re going to keep the same base projects. Some new ideas will be documented on the support site, and we’ll cover some new game ideas there, to keep the book size down a little. Also, unlike the first book where I was feeling my way around an unfamiliar system, after 8+ years I’ve learned a lot more about how far I can push a Raspberry Pi and still get a nice game out of it. It honestly has surprised me how much we can do on it and my view on its limits has changed a lot over the years. If you don’t do anything crazy, the Pi and its SBC cousins can make some amazing games.
We’re covering a few different styles and genres which are common in the games market or if you want to do a different genre it can be deduced from the content provided here. Of course, you won’t be able to sell anything, no one buys Linux software but you can claim bragging rights which are also usable to pay for rent, mortgage, food and… ermm wait… I may have got something wrong somewhere.

Mine Looks Different?
It’s in the nature of commercial software to update from time to time, and even as I write this, I’ll have to deal with software updates of at least three of my key tools which are known to update regularly. When I finish writing this, I will go back through it and change as many old images as I need to, so as to be as up‑to‑date as possible, even then, by the time you read it there are likely to be many subtle dif‑ ferences in the layout and format, even sequences of some of the tools, especially visually. I also use at least two development machines, home and work laptop, with different versions of the main tools and multiple targets, so I fully expect a


16	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
lot of images to be different to your basic first time setup. Also, some of the code has changed a little over time, especially some of the keyboard handling, and that does tend to get tinkered with a lot! Even though I was happy with the code when the book went to press, I will continue to tinker! Always check with the download code to be sure you have the latest version.
However! Don’t Panic! We can do that later when we get into the tricky stuff! It’s unlikely any of the functionality will change massively in successive
updates, so if the screenshots and code snippets presented in the book don’t look exactly the same, consider them as guides only, and take note of what that image/snippet and the text around it is telling you to add/change/remove and don’t worry if it looks different, be adaptable. Any updates/changes that have real functional issues for the book code, I will document, and maintain fixes on the support site, where there is a forum for you to ask for help.

First Steps
It’s a common rule that programmers must never assume anything, so I’m immediately going to break that rule and assume you know how to install Visual Studio, set up a Cross Platform Linux Project in Visual Studio and/or install and use VisualGDB and get your Target, in this case, a Raspberry Pi, setup to go. No? Ok well, let’s do the simple things first.
Setup the Raspberry Pi. This is relatively easy, especially if you opted for a preformatted SD card when you purchased it. If so, insert the card into the Pi, hook up your power, keyboard, mouse and display and fire it up.
If you didn’t opt for the preformatted card, you have a bit more work to do, but it’s always best to go to the Raspberry Pi website and follow the latest instruc‑ tions: https://www.raspberrypi.com/documentation/.
Install Visual Studio, which also should be simple, Microsoft downloads tend to be painless, if a little slow due to their size. Installation can take a little while but there’s not a lot of input required from you, so once you’ve started it and ticked all the right boxes, you can go make a few cups of your favourite beverage and come back when it’s done. You can download the free community edition of Visual Studio, and at the time of writing that would be the 2022 version from https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/vs/community/.
When installing you are given some options of features you want to include, please make sure you install “Desktop development with C++” and “Linux and embedded development with C++”. Also, anything else you feel might be useful (you can always add or remove things later). If you’ve already downloaded Visual Studio in the past, please fire up the Visual Studio Installer and make sure these two options are available, you can check by choosing, “modify”, and looking at the settings.

Building on PC
If you don’t have a Raspberry or other SBC target system, then in this edition, you are in luck. The downloaded content does have the ability to create PC builds using GLFW as a simple method of setting up graphics and i/o. Though GLFW is itself a very nice library to write games with a lot of cool features, we’re not going to do anything more than setting up a working environment to allow our Pi code to work on PC. But feel free to use some of the features of GLFW if you understand them.


Getting Started	17
You can choose which version to run by selecting a project and setting it as your start‑up project (right‑click on the solution name) in the Solution view pane. Select Windows or Rasp4Project, and then choose x64 config for the Windows build or ARM64 for the Raspberry build. We’re only going to build Debug builds in these tutorials.
Your luck only runs so far though, since I’m still going to focus on the Pi build (after we set things up), even though our PC can do a lot more, we are not going to exceed the limits of the Pi. The PC is set up to emulate OpenGLES 3.1, and in nearly all cases your PC can do that, in a few cases the code will fall back to a different version, which may have problems with some of the Pi‑based con‑ tent. Sadly, I can’t anticipate what problems, or give definitive fixes here, but if you report the issue on the support site, we can try to fix it as a community. But, that said, a PC is not our target, it’s just a convenience for you to work without a Raspberry to hand. Priority on code errors will always be for the Pi code.

Setting Things Up
Sadly, we still have a few confusing steps to go through to write our first Raspberry Pi program so let’s start by introducing ourselves to Visual Studio. If you’ve already used it and know some C/C++, you can skip to the section titled “Setting up the Raspberry Pi and other targets”.
Those of you still with me, prepare to be amazed and scared, mostly scared, since Visual Studio is indeed an incredibly scary thing when you first fire it up but we’re only going to use some of the most basic features to start, so let’s just play with those first before we even think about the scary topic of connecting to the target machine.
Depending on your version of Windows you should have a link somewhere on your start menu or taskbar for the version of Visual Studio you just installed. I prefer to keep it on my taskbar at the bottom so it’s always accessible.
Now this is the point where we need to go to the support site. My commis‑ sioning editor specifically asked me to ofload some code and graphic content to the site to keep the page count, and by extension, the price of the book down, which is a good idea. So, let’s save a few pages by visiting the site and following the setup and first Hello World project there. Once you’ve worked through that, come back for Chapter 2.
