2


Getting Our Target Ready















Welcome back, I hope you enjoyed your first visit to the website. We should now have a nice Visual Studio setup for programming things on our PC. But of course, this book is mainly about programming on a target system. So, we must make sure that is now set up. We don’t need to ofload that content as it’s not too much, so we’ll keep it here.
If you only plan to use the PC build version, none of this is relevant, when you select the Windows Build in the downloaded content as your default project, then you’re already good to go. But if you have a Linux/Raspberry target con‑ nected in some way to your Windows PC, there are a few things to consider. We need to make sure our target is communicating, has compilers on board and the drivers and libraries we need are accessible and up to date. For ease of reference, I will consider every Linux reference or Target to be a Raspberry Pi 4B or better.
We want the OS to be as clean as possible, so for this book, it’s best to use a new fresh and clean SD, or boot directory for your Target.
If you bought a preformatted SD card or managed to install it following the instructions on the support site, our target should be quite happily running its OS now, with SSH interfacing active and annoying screen blanking switched off, but ironically, we’re not going to spend a massive amount of time using it.
It is really wise though, to spend just a little bit of time reading the manuals or wiki sites that your maker recommends just to familiarise yourself with your target machine.
I’m using Raspberry Pi 400 as my main target, though I am quite comfort‑ able with how this works now, it’s a little different from how it worked 6 months ago due to small updates/changes to tools, so minor variations will have to be expected and dealt with by you using a tiny bit of imagination or a quick visit to the support site and forum.


DOI: 10.1201/9781003527695-3                                                                                                                               19
We’re not here to talk about the joys of navigation on a Linux‑based system, and I never touched Linux before I got my first Raspberry Pi, so I’ve been on my search engines to find out the key things I need to know. It turned out I needed to know a lot! But probably the first and most important thing you need to know is how to make sure your version of Raspberry OS (or another Linux) is up to date.
That part is simple, assuming your target has internet access (it should) in your Linux console, type:

sudo apt update

then

sudo apt upgrade

RaspberryOS now also gives you a nice little icon on the panel to indicate updates are available, so you can also just update from the GUI.
I wish I could spend some time explaining to anyone not using a Raspberry machine how to get it set up and running but in truth, there are just too many of them out there, and I really don’t want this to be a book about Single Board Computers and their idiosyncrasies, it’s meant to be about programming.
Throughout the book, I am only going to show general code which works for my intended targets. Raspberry and PC, but fear not, the only significant1 differ‑ ences relate to the graphic setup and whether it works in a window or full screen.
Most versions of Linux keep the library and include files we need in the same places, but reality can bite sometimes, and the fact is a few systems will have dif‑ ferent locations of those files. I will record as many as I can on the support site but really, it’s up to you to find them as I won’t have every single machine available to test and check.
It is wise to know that OS updates from time to time as do several standard apps that come with your OS and these are not all updated at the same time. Makers will post new builds for you to create new SDs but be aware that every time you make a new fresh SD you lose any package libraries (libs) or projects that you installed on the previous version. So rather than continually burning SDs every time a new build comes, and potentially losing your useful tools and libs, use the update/upgrade rou‑ tine described above. PC updates are usually automatic and background.
From our viewpoint as programmers, the target is just a system to run code, whether it runs in its console text mode or a GUI, we’re going to make it do other things and the only two things we need to know are the hostname which we can control and/or the IP address of our target which we can’t. On a Pi, we can easily set our own hostname; you can navigate to Preference‑>Raspberry Pi Configuration menu and set our own name. Or if you are on a different system, go into a terminal and type hostname to ask what it is. The IP address is the actual current network address of your system, but the hostname is a simpler more flexible way to address it. Generally, we will use the hostname to speak to our target, but if there are connection issues, some PCs and virus checkers can be a little sensitive about connecting to strange machines, and the IP address will usually overcome most issues. The standard hostname is raspberrypi.
Getting the IP address is simple, once we’re all connected correctly, we need to ID our target on our network. If you don’t have the graphical interface or have


1  There is one other significant difference, which relates to shaders, but so far, I only find this to be a problem on our PC builds, they don’t like lowp or medium values, only highp; full info is on the support site.

20	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
opened a terminal window and have a console screen, then we can simply type ifconfig and press enter, we’ll get a slightly confusing set of numbers but look for the eth0: section which has a four value inet number something like 192.168.2.20.
The actual number will depend on your network, but once you find it, take a note of it, that’s your Target’s ID, the IP address, on the network. On a Raspberry and other modern OSs, you can also get this number by hovering over the up/ down pair of arrows that indicate a wired connection. If you are not plugged in with a cable, but on the Wi‑Fi network, look for wlan0: and you can also hover over the wireless icon which will replace the up/down arrows.
Ok we have the hostname which we use and the IP address, so now we move on.

Oh Wait…Did We Plug In?
Having problems? Of course, we must assume that either it is hooked to the Dev PC via a cable, or plugs into a router on the Dev PC’s network, or it is connected to the same Wi‑Fi network as the Dev PC. The key point is your target, and your development PC needs to be on the same network, and they need to be con‑ nected. This needs to be a network connection, not Bluetooth or USB, which is not effective.
That really should be all there is to it, if you are hooked up via Wi‑Fi or cable on the same network or peer to peer (directly connected with a cable), you will be able to get a connection to your target. A small caveat here, at the time of writing (November ‘24) if you are using Bookworm as your OS on Raspberry, it has been known to be a little fussy connecting directly to another PC, it’s sometimes a little less fussy connecting to a general router or Wi‑Fi. There are a few solutions on the Raspberrypi.com forums. Usually, though it takes time to negotiate a con‑ nection but gets there after a minute or 2 (which is absurd, but out of my hands).

Let Visual Studio Know
Visual Studio for Linux asks you to do some setup, so it knows what you are connected to (that IP address or hostname) and can make a copy of some of the directories and include files you will be compiling on the target. For the most part, once I’ve got you started and ready, you don’t need to do too much. Though if you add system content to your target, or OS updates on your target, it’s a good idea to update your Visual Studio’s directories. This process is explained and demonstrated on the support site. But briefly, open Debug→Options→Cross Platform→Connection Manager from your Visual Studio menu bar.

Getting the Machines to Talk
There are two basic ways that our Dev PC can talk to our Raspberry Pi; via a hardware cable, which is generally going to be the fastest, or via Wi‑Fi which assumes both machines are on the same network.
Though you can hook the Dev PC up directly to the Raspberry Pi, most peo‑ ple with multiple computers use some form of router, it doesn’t make a lot of dif‑ ference, so long as both machines are on the same internal/home/ofice network and therefore can “ping” each other to create a network communication. It’s pos‑ sible to even connect to them over the internet…but really I can’t see the point.
Wi‑Fi basically works the same way as a cable but is slower. Basic wired net speeds are rated around 1000 Mb/s, which is the max our Raspberry Pi can handle.

Getting Our Target Ready	21
Wi‑Fi tends to be under 500 Mb/s, though as with all tech, they are getting faster, and 500 Mb/s is certainly fast enough to work with, but more speed is always better, so try to use a wire. Don’t be too alarmed if you find that the actual per‑ formance of your equipment does not match the stated performance on the box.
All  this  technical  talk  makes  it  sound  like  we  have  to  perform  some kind of black magic rituals, but in reality, hooking our machines up is pretty straightforward.


Sending Our First Program
Download and fire up the StartHere project from the website and follow the online instructions to connect or select the PC build. If we get things to connect ok, we should see a very simple main.cpp file in our solution which will look very famil‑ iar; it’s basically the same as our first Visual Studio PC project. Hit F5 and build it. You should get a blank screen for a while, followed hopefully by a Linux
console window displaying, Hello, from XXX where XXX is your project name. Run it again… This time it might be a bit quicker to fire up. That’s because it’s
not sending data or collecting data via the connection the system is smart enough to know that the content is already there and has not changed, also since it has not changed it’s not re‑building the content. It’s probably still a bit slow, but we can make that better as we go.
Our two systems have slightly different options for setting how your project builds, and these are dictated by the properties option.
You can set values in your VS4L project Properties by right‑clicking on the Project name in the solution, which will give you a menu with many options that look like this. Take note we’re setting small changes to the Debug config for an ARM64 system. Most of the main changes are going to be on General, Debugging and some of the C/C++ options. There’s a video link on the support site to help you set this up, please look if you are not too sure what to do here, it can be a bit daunting (Figure 2.1).




















Figure 2.1  Setting properties.


22	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
You also need to be sure you are building the correct config for our target. Most Raspberry Pis now use a 64bit OS with their 64bit CPUs, so you should be building ARM64. Code will build in all the configs, but it may not work quite as you expect or as quickly, so be sure to focus for now on the Debug build of an ARM64 and always check that’s the configuration you alter. Once you have it set up though, you’re unlikely to make many changes other than adding some libraries and preprocessor commands to control your project’s optional content. If like me, you are lucky enough to have a small Linux PC as a target, then of course you want to use the ´64, or less appealing ´86 options. ARM is standard 32bit, which is still viable on a Pi with a 64bit system, but it will throw little warn‑ ings of incompatibility at you, more as a complaint than a showstopper though (Figure 2.2).


















Figure 2.2 The first run.


Also notice here that when you build and finish your projects run through, your output window will show a lot of content. It’s all perfectly correct and accu‑ rate, but also a bit technical, not all of which really makes sense, for now that’s ok. When we get real errors, it will be clear to us what’s going on. When we are a bit more experienced, we’ll understand what it is telling us and it may point us to some actual errors, for now though, we don’t need to worry about it. For clarity though, the info is basically telling us; our project was sent to the PC, the validity of the files sent was confirmed by checking with the ones referenced in the solu‑ tion and it was compiled, linked, ran and exited.
So far so good!
There are only two things we need to know here: Assuming we are using a target.
First and most important, our code is not running on our PC, it’s running on our target. Our PC is talking to it and getting info that allows it to know where it is in the program cycle so that the debugger can correctly display info. Visual Studio is acting as a dumb terminal to your target.
Second, the Visual Studio Linux Console output on our Visual Studio win‑ dow is actually pretending to be our target’s console window, when our target sends to its console it will appear there. It’s a process called routing.


Getting Our Target Ready	23
We could open up a console window on the target if we wanted and run the executable from its directory like this (Figure 2.3):


















Figure 2.3  Running on a Pi.

Or even direct the output to the target console if one is visible. But really, we don’t want to go to so much effort, consoles are really for information or direct access only and having them on our development machine is much better than having them on the target which we want to be a graphic display when we write our games. The less we directly touch the target the better.
Congratulations. You’ve just had your first project run on your target. And it really wasn’t very hard at all. Feel free to change it, add silly names and extra output, as we did before with the PC project. Get comfortable with this, we have taken an important first step in target‑based development.

We Don’t Need to Repeat This
Aside from making the occasional edit and addition to our properties, we don’t really need to make a new project from the start of the book. The start points for each project in the book are downloadable from the support site. As long as you are aware of the edits you need to make, like the target IP/hostname and maybe some directories where you keep libs. The next time you need to make a fresh new project will be when you start your own from scratch project.
There are some different examples of content on the support site to demon‑ strate how to start up a new target project.












24	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
3Using the Target














Ready to Rock and Ermm Indeed Roll!
So far so good, we have a dev system on our PC, which gives us a professional IDE and a means to send code and other data to the Raspberry Pi for compiling, then running and debugging. If you want to only run the PC build, you can just set the default project to the PC version, but you’re missing out on the good stuff. Currently, we are using a remote‑based compiling system, this is effective
and simple to set up, but has some drawbacks, mainly on the compilation speed. Our target machine has only a fraction of the power of our PC, so it is going to compile our code much slower than our PC could do it, so it makes more sense to do the compilation of our ARM code on our PC.
That’s something we could do, but that kind of cross platform development has one or two minor pitfalls I want to avoid till we’re more comfortable with our setup. If we just use the compiler on the target, which was already there or we installed it, there is basically no doubt that it works on that target. Running a compiler on our PC to output code is 99.9% of the time going to be fine if you have the correct toolchain (compiler/linker), but I want you to get a sense of how long compilation can take, so that when we do make a switch to PC‑based com‑ piling, you will know the benefits.
For now, we’ve achieved something, getting code from our keyboard onto our PC, and then running on the target, that’s worth a pat on the back and a celebratory swig of strong programmer coffee.
But in Chapter 4, it’s time to get serious after a bit of explanation and some proper setting up!





DOI: 10.1201/9781003527695-4                                                                                                                               25
Graphics Explained!
So far, we’ve managed to get a bit of code going and print some text, however, to write games we need nice pretty graphics in glorious colour with lovely ani‑ mations. To do that, we need to ask the video system in our target to draw our graphics. And there lies a problem for us as novice coders. Asking a very complex hardware chip to do even the simplest things is hard work, and we generally don’t have access to all the relevant registers and access protocols the chip wants…How then do we do graphics?
Back in the (good?) old days, screen displays were generally memory mapped in some way, so if you wrote a byte to the memory area representing the screen, a pixel would appear.
Sadly, those days are gone, with the advent of ever more powerful graphic chips whose sole purpose is to produce 3D objects in virtual space on a flat panel, drawing flat memory graphics directly has fallen out of favour (though it can still be done on some machines).
The preferred method these days is to ask a graphics processing unit (GPU) to draw things for us, usually triangles. Those most beloved of all coder graphic primitives.
But getting our GPU to draw even simple triangles means a lot of very low‑level requests to hardware to set up registers, send data, confirm data, attach data and so on… it’s a pain.
To relieve that pain, hardware manufacturers make their systems compli‑ ant with graphic APIs (Application Programming Interfaces), or more accurately graphic APIs are produced for hardware systems, but given there is no sense in limiting your market, hardware makers generally introduce new features to their hardware slowly, to allow the APIs a chance to incorporate new features.
There are two dominating APIs in the games field. DirectX from Microsoft, which is used by PCs and most other Windows‑based systems, and Xbox con‑ soles from Microsoft. It’s a vast, frequently updated and powerful API which on recent versions of Windows has been rolled into the OS itself so is no longer an additional download.
This is very much a workhorse API for any PC, and its constant develop‑ ment over the years has created a standard where its market dominance was so powerful that hardware was forced to comply with it allowing for widespread standardisation of desktop GPUs which in turn allowed the hardware makers to focus more on performance of the base standards, ahead of fancy new graphic gimmicks which most users could not access. This also had the benefit of allow‑ ing coders to specialise and drive the graphics tech forward to the levels we see today.
New hardware features do come along but under controlled release condi‑ tions and almost always with the API’s updated and ready to use them when they come to market.
The other giant is OpenGL, which is available for almost every computer‑based system imaginable. It is a more open and community‑friendly API than DirectX, but it does have a standards body, The Khronos Group, who maintain it, update when needed and enhance performance, maintaining the reference materials that users and hardware makers can use to develop new software and hardware.
It also has a very popular subbranch called OpenGLES the ES stands for embedded systems, and is considered a low overhead high‑performance version,




26	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
for use in machines which need low power consumption and do not have mas‑ sively powerful chips or memory. It does have some limitations and a lot of previ‑ ously deprecated but still usable features of full OpenGL have been removed to keep it slim. But, it is a popular API due to its use in mobile phones and other small devices. Including, our Raspberry Pi and almost all other Single Board Computers on the market.
There are also proprietary APIs from Nintendo and Sony, which are unique but familiar in their approach to things, taking the best of OpenGL and DirectX and adapting them for their specific needs. Because they are only available on the maker’s own machines, these APIs are highly specialised and optimised for known hardware configurations and are generally very high performance.
Also, sadly they are 100% confidential and not to be discussed in this book. However, it’s fair to say once you understand how to use one API you should be able to adapt to the others since they all generally try to do the same things in similar ways at least from the user’s point of view. What happens under the hood should probably stay under the hood unless or until you are an expert in 3D maths and hardware coding.

So, it be OpenGLES ye be Wanting Arrgghhh!
Is it Talk like a Pirate Day (September 19th)? No, oh well every book should have at least one talk like a pirate comment, that’s mine… it if is Talk like a Pirate Day, add an arghhh at the end of every sentence in this section, maybe an occasional, me hearty, too.
We’re almost ready to start some proper tech coding; we are going to enter the world of graphics by doing some 2D games, let’s stick to a few very simple concepts and add to our knowledge of how to use OpenGLES as we go.
This is one point where our SBC target as a console concept lets us down a little. Consoles, of course, have those very powerful graphic hardware features we all love and also have those specialised APIs I mentioned before. What these do is allow for a higher level of manipulation of all the machine’s graphic systems, which in turn forms the basis of their Software Development Kits (SDKs). A col‑ lection of enhancements to the API, and various software packages to control graphics, assets, memory, sound, etc.
We don’t have an SDK… and writing a fully functioning SDK is way beyond the scope of this book. But we can identify the key components we will need to write basic game concepts and put together our own very basic project layout as we go. We’ll talk about that more after we’ve done our project work. To begin with our collection of routines and function we will put together through the book is more of a framework than a fully formed SDK, but we will add to it as we need to, until we have something we can be happy to share with others. When you do eventually manage to get your hands on a proper console, this experience of what goes into the framework will be a massive boost to you. Programmers never like to re‑invent a nice round wheel, but they do like to know how to make them should they ever find themselves having to work with square ones.
As we go along with each game, we’ll try to add various components to our framework and make things easier for the games that follow. To start with we need to use the most basic Graphic functions we have available, which in our case is… drumroll, OpenGLES… arrghh me hearty!



Using the Target	27
Now it’s fair to ask, why use OpenGLES rather than full fat OpenGL, espe‑ cially if you are working on a PC Linux target system, or only doing a Windows project, then OpenGL is available to you. But in this book, I’m going to limit you to OpenGLES. To allow us to keep common code between systems, we need to work to the lowest system, which is going to be the Pi’s OpenGLES, so we’re going to just keep the PC builds using the same features that OpenGLES has, you can experiment later with the extra features that full OpenGL4.6 has when you get your head around everything in this book. OpenGLES gives us baby steps to start with.

Where Is Open GLES2.x on My Target?
Now here’s where we will start to set up some of the major differences between the editions of this book. When I wrote the first edition, I was targeting the then quite new Raspberry Pi 3B, which has now been succeeded by more powerful Pi 4B and Pi 5B versions with even more powerful graphics. The Pi 3B used OpenGLES 2.0, at the time an API that was starting to get a bit old, but such was the popularity of the Raspberry and the fact it was unable to do OpenGLES 3.0 on hardware, it made sense to work with that in the first edition, and leave the extra features of OpenGLES 3.0 to appendices for those readers who were using slightly more exotic systems.
But, the Raspberry Pi4 came out with much more advanced GPU capabili‑ ties, and supports OpenGLES3.1, with just a few hints of some OpenGLES 3.2 ability.
So, for this book we are going to use OpenGLES 3.1 but we will start as we did before, using the very simple OpenGLES 2.0 systems just to get us up and running, since I think it’s a bit easier to explain OpenGLES 3.1 when you’ve done some OpenGLES 2.0.
Fortunately, OpenGLES 3.1 comes built into RaspberryOS, so we don’t have to install anything new, but we do make a point to update it, and OpenGLES 3.1 is fully compatible with OpenGLES 2.0 so our first test projects in OpenGLES 2.0 don’t need any hoops to be jumped through to work, we’ll bring the hoops in later.
Now if you are not using a Raspberry Pi4B or 5B as a target SBC but you are planning on having another brand of SBC as a target, you will need to make sure you have the relevant graphic libs, and a few other libs installed for future use. You can find out how to do this on the support site.

A Nice New Project with Graphics
Our old console program has served its purpose, it’s time for us to produce a proper game project so let’s start a new one, which has OpenGLES already setup and ready to go, but we’ll hold off on doing anything too dramatic.
This is the point in most books where you are told to go download a project from a website and work on it, and though I made people type code in the first edition, this time you’ll find the GameProject1 on the support website, but if you are new to coding, it really is a good idea to get some practice of just enter‑ ing code into your IDE, and if you are a beginner, I strongly suggest you enter

the program listed on the site by hand. You’ll make a tonne of typing errors, but that’s good practice for finding real bugs later. We’re going to go through the whole process of starting a project and building up the small arsenal of files we’re planning to use throughout this book to create a framework for writing all manner of simple games.
At this point, it might be wise to locate some good online resources for OpenGLES 2.0. Googling for OpenGL will bring up a plethora of main OpenGL sites and some of those are good until they get deeper into full OpenGL which we can’t really do or are too focused on the older OpenGL1.x which is no longer in general use.
The Khronos Groups’ page’s main focus is on documenting the feature set of OpenGLES 2.0 so there are not a lot of easy‑to‑use tutorials but it is the go‑to place for information on how to gain access to all the features of OpenGLES 2.0 (and other versions).
The main documentation can be found here: https://www.khronos.org/opengles/sdk/docs/man/

https://www.khronos.org/opengles/sdk/docs/reference_cards/OpenGL‑ ES‑2_0‑Reference‑card.pdf
Again, bookmark this on your browser bar or better yet, print it out and put it on the wall next to where you work.
Since we’re going to leave OpenGLES 2.0 behind us later in this edition of the book, I don’t recommend you find a copy of the standard reference book for it. I recommend instead you get yourself a copy of the OpenGLES 3.0 guide, by Dan Ginsburg and Budirijanto Purnomo. A copy of this book is considered essential to keep by your side when working on OpenGLES 3.0. There isn’t an update for OpenGLES 3.1 or 3.2 but you can follow the reference guides for the extra fea‑ tures and there are lots of tutorials.
https://www.khronos.org/files/opengles32‑quick‑reference‑card.pdf.
And  the  online  content  is  here:  https://registry.khronos.org/OpenGL‑ Refpages/es3/
Let’s begin now, as we did in the first edition with some openGLES2.0 code.

Let the Download Take the Strain
One of the “problems” with OpenGL in all its different formats is it needs some technical setup; it needs to create “contexts”, and “buffers” and “Windows” and “display” spaces that can interface with the display of your machine. To make things a bit more complex, the setting up is different for Linux and Windows so you need two different methods.
In the first book focused on Raspberry, we typed in the Raspberry setup, but this time round we’ve got our dual system startup projects ready for us, so we’ll let it do the setups and move on. If you’re interested though, most of the setting up is in the XWindows.cpp file in the Raspberry Project. The PC is using GLFW and GLAD to make a GLES 3.1 display, or, in some rare cases due to incompat‑ ibility it will set up an OpenGL context with a warning.





Using the Target	29
Our First Graphics Project!
Ready? First, we should set up a working directory; ideally, it should be just off your main drive on Windows that’s usually the C: drive. It’s important later, to make sure you do not have spaces in your directory path or file names, Linux hates spaces and Pis use Linux, so let’s avoid any future hair pulling by creating a new folder in your C: drive called Work and extract it there. We will extract all our projects to that folder to keep them together. Once extracted, load the appropriate GameProject1.sln and we will start with a Pi build which should be the default project setting, you know how to set up the target settings, so if you are not using hostname raspberrypi400.local1 you should set up for that. Of course, if you don’t have a Pi, or SBC target, set the default project to the Windows project, the code will still work as long as the startup project and config are set correctly.
First test your project can compile and run ok, no typos or strange errors? It’s not actually doing much but we need to be sure it runs before we add to it.
All good? If not check the site for help, if yes, we are ready to start. This proj‑ ect just sets up our machine, and it initialised OpenGLES for us and goes into a very simple empty loop, waiting for us to add a bit of code to do something.
For the next couple of projects, this is all we will need, but later we will add more system libs, as we need extra features that we know are available in the libraries that come supplied with the Raspberry Pi or are supplied by 3rd Parties. I’ll go through this when needed.

HOUSTON, We Have a Triangle
Let’s explain now what the project has done for you. When we normally start up a graphic project we have a lot of things we need to set up, until that’s done, we can’t get anything on screen, so we need to first create what’s called a render surface, which is basically the image we want to display on our screen, using something called EGL, which is a low‑level API that sits above the core OpenGLES code and acts as a collection of helper routines to get things going and provides the direct link to your video hardware.
We also must do something about our shaders, even though we have no idea at the moment what they actually do, OpenGLES insists we have some. Talk about carts before horses?
Next, we must actually do the creation and drawing (rendering) of our triangle.
Then make it visible!
These steps all need to be set up before we can even begin to see something. That is just so much work! But that’s just how it is, once we’ve got these ideas worked out and coded, we don’t really have to think about them again, so let’s bite the bullet and make a start.
Though I’ve bowed to the publisher to ofload content to online sources, at this point I really do want you to try to type this upcoming part in and get some practice handling some strange‑looking code to let you get used to Visual Studio and understand the compile process. Please review the HelloTriangle Project from the support site and make sure it runs. It will default to the PC build if all is

well, and you have a Pi connected, please set the start‑up project to Rasp4Project and send the config to ARM64.
You’re almost certain to make typos and omissions, so this small program is a good start to help you learn where to find the errors and fix them.
All our project contents are going to be located in the “OurProject” project in Visual Studio, which is a “shared” project, a special type of project. Our Windows and Rasp4Project projects are just set up and get going projects to jump to the Game.cpp project, which is shared by both platforms and starts our own code. Behold the Triangle Code!
Now, let’s enter the code. In the Game.cpp, you should recognise that Game.cpp and Game.h contain class methods that our Windows and Raspberry instan‑ tiate and then call the start. We’ll do this a little haphazardly to begin with just as a direct in file function, but this way lets us type in one function, the DrawTriangle function, which at the moment is an empty shell. Expand it by typing in this code;

int DrawTriangle() {
Graphics GraphicFunctions; static GLuint PO =  ‑1;
if (PO ==  ‑1) // only do it once
{ // take care to use the \n to end the line GLbyte vShaderStr[] =
"precision highp float;\n" // we use highp
since PC's dont' like using medium or low
"attribute vec3 a_position;\n" "void main()\n"
"{ \n"
" gl_Position=vec4(a_position,1.0);  \n" "}\n";
GLbyte fShaderStr[] =
"precision highp float;\n"
"void main()\n"
"{\n"
"gl_FragColor =  vec4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0);\n" "}\n";
// Load and compile the vertex/fragment shaders GLuint vShader =
GraphicFunctions.LoadShader(GL_VERTEX_SHADER, (const char*)
vShaderStr);
GLuint fShader = GraphicFunctions.LoadShader(GL_FRAGMENT_SHADER, (const char*)
fShaderStr);

// Create the program object
PO =  glCreateProgram(); // save the PO and remove the ‑1 init value
// now we have the V and F shaders attach them to the program object
glAttachShader(PO, vShader); glAttachShader(PO, fShader); // Link the program glLinkProgram(PO);
// Check the link status



Using the Target	31
GLint AreTheylinked;
glGetProgramiv(PO, GL_LINK_STATUS, &AreTheylinked); if (!AreTheylinked)
{//they should link fine but lets report if there was an
error
GLint RetinfoLen =  0;
// check and report any errors
glGetProgramiv(PO, GL_INFO_LOG_LENGTH,
&RetinfoLen);
if (RetinfoLen >  1) {
GLchar* infoLog =  (GLchar*)malloc(sizeof(char) * RetinfoLen);
glGetProgramInfoLog(PO, RetinfoLen, NULL, infoLog); fprintf(stderr, "Error linking program:\n%s\n",
infoLog);
free(infoLog); }
glDeleteProgram(PO);
return FALSE; //something bad happened }
}
//get ready to draw
GLfloat TriVertices[] = {
0.0f ,   0.5f, 0.0f, ‑0.5f,  ‑0.5f, 0.0f, 0.5f ,  ‑0.5f, 0.0f
}; glUseProgram(PO);
glVertexAttribPointer(0, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 0, TriVertices);
glEnableVertexAttribArray(0);
glDrawArrays(GL_TRIANGLES, 0, 3); if (glGetError() !=  GL_NO_ERROR) {
printf("Oh bugger"); // we use this so we can lock a breakpoint if needed
return FALSE; }
return TRUE; }

Sorry for making you type that, but you’ll thank me one day. You will probably make a few errors, and it won’t work first time. Even if it seems to compile and run, but not do what you expect, it will have errors. That’s normal, you need to develop some practice in finding mistakes, usually small ones, such as a missing line, or a mistyped symbol, but whatever you do, do not cling to the dogma, that you typed it in exactly… because if it does not work, you didn’t! Be cool with that. Learning to accept you will make errors, often, oh so often, and taking responsi‑ bility for those errors so you can find and fix them, is vitally important to being a responsible programmer.
There’s not really a lot to this code, but it is confusing that you must do so much to do so little. At this point, I’m not going to explain everything in here; the comments can do that for you. But this will serve the purpose of making sure you can compile and run an OpenGLES project.
Compile and run, and all being well, lo and behold, we have a triangle (Figure 3.1):




32	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming















Figure 3.1 Hello triangle.


Now all of this is fine and dandy, and you can take some pride in getting this up and running if you typed it all in yourself. But I find most entry‑level programmers who are trying to write games, come across this or similar little start‑up programs and then realise there is a massive disconnection between put‑ ting a triangle or square on screen and their latest, poly‑laden 60 GB game on the consoles. Sadly, this often stops them in their tracks because it’s just too big a disconnection for them to see a way forward.
And they are right, there is a massive disconnect, it’s totally confusing. But it’s mostly a case of scale. That 60 GB game is simply moving a lot of triangles around, and it’s using code that allows the programmer to manage how many of these triangles are drawn at any given time and in any given way and in any given colour. The problem we have is that we now know how to draw a triangle, but we don’t really have any idea how to create games with it. That management of the way we draw things is much more important to us than the actual mechanisms that draw all the pixels on screen.
There are not many games that you can do with one static, coloured but rather uninteresting triangle, but this is mainly a test, we know it compiles, transfers and runs on our target so we should be confident that everything works, the key point here is we have four basic game program features in place that we coded ourselves, and can now expand on.

◾   An Initialisation system

◾   A Processing system, which will/can include user input or counters and will later include Enemies/AI

◾   A Display system, which loops back to the processing until we decide to end creating a main loop

◾   An exit





Using the Target	33
These four things are vital to almost any game program, the concept of a main loop is especially important, and though there are variations on how it’s done, almost all games have these. But it’s almost time now, to make something cool happens.

Why Are We Working in a Window?
One thing we might have noticed is that our triangle display isn’t filling the screen, it’s living in a small window…that’s ok, we can work with that, and there will certainly be situations when it is desirable but for games, really, it’s better to get the system to fill the screen. I can just change the line;

init_ogl(p_state, 1024, 720);

To 1920, 1080, and I’d get it filling up the screen, but some might consider this a big mistake. Let me explain:
On most HDMI display targets, the physical pixel size of our screen is fixed, that’s probably going to be 1920´1080 but notice I said probably. In fact, there’s a lot of variation in screen sizes and supported monitors. So, if we were to set a hard number size, it may not work on other people’s systems.
To do a full screen properly, we need to interrogate the system to find out the proper size and use those values. Sadly, there is no direct easy way in Linux to interrogate the system; we in fact have to use one of a couple of resolution librar‑ ies that are available. For now, and only for now, we’ll use the hard numbers but when we get a bit more comfortable, we’ll use a lib to control resolution and add it to our framework.

Setup.sh
Before we move on, if you downloaded the hello triangle code, you would also have downloaded a small script file called setup.sh. This is a bash script that will download all the libraries and files we need to use in later projects. We don’t need it all immediately, but we might as well do it now. PC users, all libs will be included in the project, so you have nothing to install.
For Pi, you need to copy the file from your project folder into your home/ xxx directory and once it’s in there and your Pi is online, we just need to execute it by opening a terminal making sure it’s in your home/xxx dir and entering.

sudo ./setup.sh

If it does not work, check you are connected, if so, there may be some issues with permissions and the ability to execute. Make sure to have permission set to executable by anyone (Figure 3.2).











34	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming















Figure 3.2  Changing permissions.

If it still won’t run, try entering:

sudo dos2unix setup.sh

It’s possible the file may have been edited on the PC making Linux a bit fussy about running it as a script.
Now we will have all the libraries and files installed for later projects. Though I won’t add them to the project build functions yet.
All projects will have a copy of this script, you only need to run it once though, it will install all the libraries used in the book to your Pi, don’t worry that some things do a fair amount of code building (like Freetype), it’s not install‑ ing any malware on your Pi, it’s just building the code from source files. Setup.sh takes a few minutes to complete and may ask you to confirm some things with a Y input. Please do.

2D
We are going to start our game programming journey with some 2D games, I know some of you want to leap right into your grand, photo quality Hi Def, 3D, Massively Multiuser Online Cloud processing realistic AI with real lasers, game. But trust me, getting the basics right is very important and a load of fun!
Many if not all the concepts used in 2D games are directly transferred to 3D games and allow you the chance to properly visualise what you are doing and measure it against what you were expecting.
I often try to explain to my students with my tongue loosely in my cheek, how Space Invaders and Halo are basically the same game. You control a char‑ acter; you can move around and shoot things, while at the same time trying to avoid being shot by those same things.
They are essentially the same game, only the visualisation, level of complex‑ ity and amounts of data being shoved onto our screen are variable.






Using the Target	35
It’s quite possible to break any shooting style game down to the basics of moving around, and shooting while avoiding being shot. If we simply consider that as being what we have to code, we can add complexity and additional eye candy as we go.
Of course, there are many other types of 2D games, puzzle games, platform games, racing games, the list is probably endless, and trying to cover them all, even in a book as big as this, is going to extremes, but you will be able to employ the concepts explained here in any kind of game, and more importantly you’ll be able to expand and develop the concepts to suit any new game genre you are lucky enough to think of.











































36	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming


4


Putting It All Together















Expanding Our First Graphics Program
So far, we’ve not done anything that has needed actual “graphics”, our triangle and possibly other shapes you might have dabbled with are mathematical con‑ cepts rather than images as we recognise images, we can alter their colour and size but there’s no actual image or texture yet and that will severely limit the games we can produce.
This is mainly because displaying actual graphics that look like chomping yellow pizza, or avenging hammer‑wielding plumbers, is a whole new set of prob‑ lems we have to introduce and get over. But if we want to be game programmers, we need to have the ability to display images, which are either created by the pro‑ gram (procedural graphics) or produced by someone or something else (assets) for incorporation into our program.

Loading Graphics or Other Assets
It’s not impossible to enter graphics into our project as typed data, but it’s extremely impractical for anything more than a small monochrome image. Modern games have gigabytes of graphic images and converting them into source data would be a monumental and unwelcome task. So instead, our assets are usually stored in a usable file format on our storage system for loading into the program and set up to use in the game.
So now we’ve reached the point where we need to load something from the computer’s storage system which ideally we have made or downloaded from our PC. Since we are doing remote building, we are going to take advantage of the transfer of data that VS4L handles for us automatically.



DOI: 10.1201/9781003527695-5                                                                                                                               37
Anything that is not actually compiled but is instead used by your program is called a Resource or an Asset, the terms are often interchangeable, but I prefer “Assets” when talking about graphics, or sound data that are directly used in the game. I use the term “Resources” to describe other files that may provide infor‑ mation to or be used in our project, like a script or list file of some kind. I may be wrong with this terminology, but no CS graduate has ever told me differently.
But assets are what we need to get access to, at this point, we need to load some simple images, and then find a way to display them.
Loading files is a simple thing in C/C++, the Standard Template Library gives us loading and saving abilities, but graphics are not just files, they are data files, data which is actually encrypted into a particular format, and there are many formats.
We simply don’t have the time or energy while learning to write a decryption system to turn graphic files into simple pixel data that we can actually draw on screen, so we need some way to do that.
Lucky for us the world is full of programming geeks, who like to write graphic systems that we can use freely, and there is a perfect system called SOIL (Simple OpenGL Image Library), it is free, available for Linux‑based systems and quite easy to get hold of.
But SOIL is very outdated, in fact many of its features rely on a very old ver‑ sion of OpenGL to function so are not really that useful and probably won’t work on many modern systems; however, one thing it does have and which works across many versions of Windows and Linux is its Image Loader, called stb_image.
SOIL itself is really just a wrapper package for a collection of image (and sound) manipulation programs including stb_image, which is a widely sup‑ ported and reasonably easy to use, set of routines to load various common for‑ mats. It’s all we really want from SOIL. Since it works, let’s work just with that. It’s simple enough to get hold of. We can install it directly onto our Raspberry, or even have it as files in our project, since we really only need the stb_image.h file. I prefer to install it onto our Raspberry since it is likely to be reused often
and it will install the latest version which may have some updates to its list of compatible files, but to be brutally honest, it’s rare to see an update. There are better image processing systems around, but for a beginner, this is all we need, when you outgrow it, you’ll understand better how it works and can probably write your own.
You can install on the Raspberry with git clone using this command in your console:

git clone https://github.com/nothings/stb.git

This will install all of SOIL to the home directory of your Pi,1 which by default on my Pi is

../home/pi

You may have a different name though, so you’ll have../home/XXX where XXX is your chosen user name. But whatever it’s called an additional stb directory will be added after you clone, so you will have

../home/XXX/stb

1  Review support site to add on PC if not already in place.



38	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Remember that address you will need to let your compiler know about this. Let’s set about adding stb_image to our project.
I won’t ask you to type in another long initialisation system, we can use the Hello Triangle for now, we’re just experimenting with a file loader.
Remember that directory we had to look for, now we need to add stb_image.h to our project, and the simplest way to do that is to right‑click on the header files “filter” which looks like a folder on your solution view and choose Add>then “Existing Item”.
Navigate to your stb folder, click to add stb_image.h and…we’re done.
All we need now is some code to let us use it properly. So now I also need you to add two new files to this project, a header and a cpp file.
Let’s call the header MyFiles.h and the cpp, MyFiles.cpp.
Try adding them to the correct solution “filter”, i.e. Headers, and Source, using the Add >New Item. You will be prompted for a type of file so choose Header file.h and C++ file. You can also select the directory where you want them to live but for now just accept the default project directory, later when you have a larger project you may decide to have clearer directories for different types of files.
While the directory isn’t too important yet, do make sure you give them the correct names, not the default Source.cpp and Header.h names. Use MyFiles.cpp and MyFiles.h.
These new files, of course, will be mostly empty, but we will put some code into them to make them valuable new utility functions.
Open up MyFiles.h and add this code

#pragma once class MyFiles {
public: MyFiles();
~MyFiles(); int height; int width; int comp;
char* Load(char const *filename,int*,int*); };

That’s, it’s pretty easy, we’re creating a small wrapper class that will allow us to load a graphic file and convert it into normal raw memory.
The actual code for this now goes into the MyFiles.cpp file, so open up that file and add this

#include "MyFiles.h"
#define STB_IMAGE_IMPLEMENTATION #include "stb_image.h"

MyFiles::MyFiles() {} MyFiles::~MyFiles() {}
char* MyFiles::Load(char const *filename,int* width, int* height)
{
unsigned char *data =  stbi_load(filename, width, height, &comp, 4); // we are always going to ask for 4 components for RGBA
return (char*) data; }



Putting It All Together	39
Now we have two very useful files…which will allow any program that includes the header to be able to load some standard image2 formats and make them avail‑ able at the location held in the pointer variable data.
Try compiling this, it won’t do much but there should be something to take note of! Did you notice that adding that file means that our build process has become a lot slower? Pi5 and PC users probably won’t notice, but there will be a distinct time taken to compile.
Try compiling again. It was very fast that time.
The first slow build is because this header stb_image.h header file is quite large and includes many other files. Also, because it’s a header file, if we included it in our main project it’s going to get compiled EVERY time we alter the file that includes the headers… that’s a bit of a pain. Because we put the stb_image.h include in the Myfiles.cpp, it’s only likely to get built one time, and then the resulting code that’s made from that is reused over and over again. As long as we know how to create and load our files, which we do by including the MyFiles.h we won’t need to compile the header file on every build.
We can use that for all subsequent 2D graphic loading needs in our games. Just make sure we have an instance of a MyFiles object somewhere and include the MyFiles file in the other classes that want to use it.
For now, we should have managed to get our project to build and run, but of course we don’t have any graphics to actually load, and since we are remote compiling. We need to get them onto the Pi at compile time…

Adding Assets to the Build Chain
There’s an important step in the way VS4L handles assets, but it’s quite logical. VS4L needs to send files held on your PC down to your target to compile there, and it therefore needs some means of knowing what you as a user want to send down. It’s quite simple for VS4L, since we list all our files in the solution, if the file you want is included in your solution, it will usually be sent down to the Pi whenever it detects the file is new or has changed since it was last sent.
This does mean we need to keep slightly sensible directories on our PC, usu‑ ally just off the main project directory, and when files are sent the Pi will dupli‑ cate those directories. This makes sense when you consider that our project runs on the Pi, so it needs to access its files on the Pi, and we don’t want to be unable to locate them.
It is important to note though that some files are marked as being content but not part of the project build. Some file types such as .OBJ files (a format for 3D model files) can get confused for code.obj files (intermediate compiled files) and that’s a headache we don’t need. For graphic files or other data type resources, we usually want the project to load them in when running not to (try to) compile them.
We can add graphic files to our solution, in a nice new filter folder we can call Graphics, and on our PC and Pi make sure we have a nice directory, which can also be called Graphics where we will keep all our graphics, perhaps with a few sub‑directories for different forms of graphics.
Adding existing items to your solution in the Graphics (or any other) filter makes them known to the build system and they will be sent down to the Pi next


2  Standard images usually include png, bmp, tga and jpg, but do be aware there are some variations in the formats, and you need to be sure that stb_image can handle your image.


40	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
time you build along with the directories that hold them. But we do need to stop Visual Studio compiling some types of files it thinks are source files, such a.obj and some shader extensions.
You can right‑click on the offending files’ filename in your solution explorer, select properties and mark it as excluded from the build, but it is content, and it is a resource or some other type of asset to your project. Visual Studio will now not try to be helpful and build that file on the PC but just send it to the target.
Doing this for every graphic can be a bit tedious, but you tend to only add one or two graphics at a time so it’s not too heavy a task if you keep on top of it, which you probably will. Adding a graphic and testing it, fixing any settings as you go. If you get a confusing, “can’t find compiler” or “don’t know how to compile”, error, it’s usually trying to compile a graphic or shader file by mistake (Figure 4.1).





















Figure 4.1  Confirm content.


Keeping Things Tidy
It’s not a good idea to just dump all your graphics scattered around in the project folder, we really need a proper folder structure, so I’m going to add an Assets folder where we will mainly keep graphics, but later sound/speech/models, etc.
I think it’s also a good point now to add header and source folders and from now on add our header files to the header folder and our cpp files to the source folder.
Folder management is one of those never‑ending debates about how your project should be arranged and there are good arguments for having different file types in different folders, but really just try to keep code and data and other resources clean and separate and it’ll all look good.
For now, let’s add the assets folder on our PC and also add a filter in our Visual Studio Solution to keep it looking tidy, the Pi will echo those dirs when they are sent down.


Putting It All Together	41
Visual Studio allows you to add new filters to the solution view frame, but even though it uses the traditional folder style Icon, it does not actually create new folders in your projects directory of your PC. This is both a blessing and a curse, because it allows us to keep all the files with.h or.cpp endings in one view on the solution view, but it does mean that we may have added those files from any number of different folders in our directory.
That’s the curse part, our system of transferring data down to the target really relies on us having a project root folder, where our project is stored and then transferring all the files we need from that root and its sub‑folders to the target to allow our assets to be accessible, Since we are allowing compiling on PC, we need to force things a bit to ensure our assets folder lives just the project root. When we compile our games, the systems on both machines will create extra folders for debug and release builds. And we can step back one folder and then into our assets folder with simple filenames which have this format:

“../Assets/filename.ext”

If we don’t keep our folders and files under control, it can get quite messy, quite quick. It’s very tempting to put all our files in the same root folder and just use filters, I’ve seen this done often but it’s not a good practice, even though we’re doing it now we are going to change later, it will all depend mostly on where we decide to put files when we add them to the project. But folders containing assets are best located in the project root directory (Figure 4.2).
















Figure 4.2  Directory layout.

You should see now that if our executable is in a Debug folder then “../Assets/ Sprites/fname” takes us down one level../ to root, then switches to Assets then Sprites so we can get our file from that folder.
Then when the time comes to send files over, the system checks the headers in the root, and all its sub‑folders, sending the required files to the target and making new sub‑directories on the target when needed.
This base structure works fine for now, but we’re going to mix it up a bit later when we do some proper cross‑platform work. To make our life a little easier, we are going to set a specific folder for code to live in called bin and we will also do this to our PC builds and it will ensure that whichever target build we run our executable file will be in a known directory that is cross‑platform friendly, more on that a bit later.

Add Some Code
Let’s try to load and display an image, nothing fancy, just find a decent‑sized image, smaller than your screen size, but big enough to be seen, something from 512´512 up to 1024´1024, that will fit on screen and be visible. When you have one save it to your assets folder.
Ok we now have an image, let’s make a small program to display it.
If you don’t have any suitable images on your PC, have a hunt on the old interwebaroonies for an image that you would like to use, ideally png, jpg or tga, for this. Copy/download it into your Assets Folder and add to the Assets filter and an existing object, as content for VS4L.
You can add any number of images and alter the file name to load them.
So far so good, we have been able to load an image, store it in its raw data format in memory somewhere, and it’s now ready for display.
Our loading and graphic conversion systems are now in place, but we are still only able currently to draw a triangle, which isn’t ideal, images are rectangular usually, so we should alter the draw routine to draw two triangles and make a square, that’s ok, but we are also going to turn whatever we load into data that can be displayed on the square in the form of a texture.
Two things now are needed. The ability to create a texture and the ability to display that texture using a different form of draw.
Add this code to your project, near the top, just before, LoadShader, its self‑contained, though at the moment pretty non‑functional as it needs data to work with. Data we have to load in. Even then, though it can create a texture, at the moment there’s no way to draw it

/*  Create a texture with width and height */
GLuint CreateTexture2D(int width, int height, char* TheData) {
// Texture handle GLuint textureId;
// Set the alignment glPixelStorei(GL_UNPACK_ALIGNMENT, 1); // Generate a texture object glGenTextures(1, &textureId);
// Bind the texture object glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, textureId); // set it up glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D,
0, GL_RGBA, width, height, 0, GL_RGBA,
GL_UNSIGNED_BYTE, TheData);

if (glGetError() !=  GL_NO_ERROR) printf("Oh bugger"); // Set the filtering mode glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER,
GL_NEAREST);
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MAG_FILTER, GL_NEAREST);
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_S, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE);
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_T, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE);

if (glGetError() ==  GL_NO_ERROR)	return textureId; printf("Oh bugger");
return textureId; }

We need some data, I’ve added a few nice pics from my photo album, in the assets listed on the support site. For this first attempt at doing graphics, let’s use a pic of my lovely grandkids (in Scottish, “The_Weans”), choosing pumpkins for Halloween (Figure 4.3).






















Figure 4.3 The weans.

Our task now is to load this in, convert it to a texture and then display it. We have the load part, though we must first make sure the image is located in our asset directory.
Let’s load it up and convert it. After the FileHandler add this code;

MyFiles FileHandler; // now go do the graphic loop
int Width, Height;
char* OurRawData =  FileHandler.Load((char*)"../Assets/ The_Weans.jpg", &Width, &Height);
if (OurRawData ==  NULL) printf("We failed to load\n");

Note that I purposefully put a small test to report a failed load. Because at the moment we’re not actually able to display this, so we’ll never know if it loaded. Error traps like this are invaluable to make sure you don’t write buggy code while blind and assume later that it’s the draw routines that have failed. Try mistyping The_Weans and compile and run to make sure you get a notice telling you that it failed.
Loading is done, we’re also pretty sure (blind faith though) that converting to texture code is done, so time to worry about drawing.



44	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Remember I told you OpenGLES 2.0 used shaders? Well, there’s a first clue as to what will need changing, our current triangle fragment shader, the part that does the actual drawing of pixels, does very little, it simply draws a red pixel, we now need a shader that will take a pixel from a texture and draw it.
Look at the shader code in Init again. Check out this line

"{gl_FragColor=vec4 (1.0,0.0,0.0,1.0);}\n";

This is the main line that needs to be changed so that it can do more than just write a red pixel
You may have noticed the shader code itself, references other variables. a_position, a_texCoord, v_texcoord, etc.
We never actually did anything to set up those variables, because for the most part, they were ignored as we are just dumping a hard pixel to screen.
They need to come into play now and be part of our program somewhere as these provide a means to access particular pixels for any modifications we need. I don’t want to get into this too much as I am going to assume you’re still finding your feet with coding so explaining how this shader works could be confusing, so for the moment all I’m going to do is set it. Explanations will come later when we really need them.
Here’s what your shaders should now look like. The gl_FragColor is now going to take a value from a texture coordinate.

GLbyte vShaderStr[] =
"attribute vec4 a_position;\n" "attribute vec2 a_texCoord;\n" "varying vec2 v_texCoord;\n"
"void main()\n" "{gl_Position=a_position;\n"
" v_texCoord =  a_texCoord;}\n"; GLbyte fShaderStr[] =
"precision mediump float;\n"
"varying vec2 v_texCoord;\n" "uniform sampler2D s_texture;\n" "void main()\n"
"{\n"
"gl_FragColor =  texture2D( s_texture, v_texCoord );\n" "}\n";

Without going into massive confusing detail, we can see that we are now using a few extra values in this shader, and our code needs somehow to know about this, which needs some additions, we have to tell our code about a_position, a_texCoord and s_texture, and store handles to them in our structure so we can set them up for our shader to work correctly.
Now go back to the top of your cpp code, alter the UserData Structure so it looks like this

typedef struct {
// save a Handle to a program object GLuint programObject;
// Attribute locations GLint  positionLoc;
GLint  texCoordLoc;
// Sampler location GLint samplerLoc;
// Texture handle GLuint textureId;
} UserData;


Putting It All Together	45
Our structure now has values we can use to store the shaders attribute and sampler locations, so let’s add some code to our Init routine to do that, at the end of Init, you can see we are loading a ProgramObject value (which is actually a duplication of a redundant ProgramObject variable you can remove) …continue with the next three lines with save our location information so that we can set up the shader.

// Store the program object p_state‑>user_data‑>programObject =  programObject;

// Get the attribute locations p_state‑>user_data‑>positionLoc =  glGetAttribLocation
(p_state‑>user_data‑>programObject, "a_position"); p_state‑>user_data‑>texCoordLoc =  glGetAttribLocation
(p_state‑>user_data‑>programObject, "a_texCoord");

// Get the sampler location p_state‑>user_data‑>samplerLoc =  glGetUniformLocation
(p_state‑>user_data‑>programObject, "s_texture");

Now that they exist and they can be used, our draw routine needs quite a bit of modification, so rather than talk through it, replace it all with this.

/*****************************************
Draw a Rectangle with texture this is a hard coded draw which is only good for the Rectangle ******************************************/
int DrawRectangle() {

GLfloat RectVertices[] =  {
‑1.0f,1.0f,0, // data has to be position then texture 0.0f,0.0	// TexCoord 0
‑1.0f,‑1.0f, 0.0f,	// Position 1 0.0f,1.0f,	// TexCoord 1 1.0f,‑1.0f, 0.0f,     // Position 2 1.0f,  1.0f,    // TexCoord 2 1.0f,  1.0f,  0.0f,  // Position 3 1.0f,  0.0f       // TexCoord 3
};

GLushort indices[] =  { 0, 1, 2, 0, 2, 3 };


if (PO ==  ‑1) {
GLbyte vShaderStr[] =
"attribute vec4 a_position;\n"
"attribute vec2 a_texCoord;\n" "varying vec2 v_texCoord;\n"
"void main()\n" "{gl_Position=a_position;\n" " v_texCoord=a_texCoord;}\n";

GLbyte fShaderStr[] =
"precision mediump float;\n" "varying vec2 v_texCoord;\n" "uniform sampler2D s_texture;\n" "void main()\n"
"{\n"
"gl_FragColor =  texture2D( s_texture, v_ texCoord );\n"
"}\n";


46	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
// Load and compile the vertex/fragment shaders GLuint vShader =  GraphicFunctions.LoadShader(GL_
VERTEX_SHADER, (const char*)vShaderStr);
GLuint fShader =  GraphicFunctions.LoadShader(GL_ FRAGMENT_SHADER, (const char*)fShaderStr);

// Create the program object PO =  glCreateProgram();

// now we have the V and F shaders attach them to the program object
glAttachShader(PO, vShader); glAttachShader(PO, fShader);

// Link the program glLinkProgram(PO);
// Check the link status GLint AreTheylinked;

glGetProgramiv(PO, GL_LINK_STATUS, &AreTheylinked); if (!AreTheylinked)
{ // they should link fine but just in case, lets report if
there was an error
GLint RetinfoLen =  0;
// check and report any errors
glGetProgramiv(PO, GL_INFO_LOG_LENGTH,
&RetinfoLen);
if (RetinfoLen >  1) {
GLchar* infoLog =  (GLchar*) malloc(sizeof(char) * RetinfoLen);
glGetProgramInfoLog(PO, RetinfoLen, NULL, infoLog);
fprintf(stderr, "Error linking program:\ n%s\n", infoLog);
free(infoLog); }
glDeleteProgram(PO);
return FALSE; //something bad happened }

}

glUseProgram(PO);
// Load the vertex position glVertexAttribPointer(0,
3,GL_FLOAT,GL_FALSE,  5 * sizeof(GLfloat), RectVertices);
// Load the texture coordinate glVertexAttribPointer(1, 2,GL_FLOAT,GL_FALSE,5 * sizeof(GLfloat),
&RectVertices[3]);

glEnableVertexAttribArray(0); glEnableVertexAttribArray(1); // Bind the texture glActiveTexture(GL_TEXTURE0);
glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, textureId);
//actually draw the rect as 2 sets of 3 vertices (2 tris make a rect)
glDrawElements(GL_TRIANGLES, 6, GL_UNSIGNED_SHORT, indices);

}



Putting It All Together	47
It’s not too different, but you can see now there’s a bit more to it, we’re setting up more vertices, since a rectangle has six due to it being made from two triangles, and it’s also having to deal with the texture points as well as the setting up the Attribute Pointers so that the data in our p_state is used by the shader.
Compile and run, and you will still get a blank rectangle window on screen. There’s one last important part to add to this.
Go back to the main loop, which in this case is the while (!quitting) loop. After we check if our data is valid, with this

if (OurRawData ==  NULL) printf("We failed to load\n");

add this line

textureId =  GraphicFunctions.CreateSimpleTexture2D(Width, Height, OurRawData);

This final bit of the puzzle will tell our code it can now use the texture ID supplied by the CreateSimpleTexture2D routine.
Compile and run, and we should see this lovely screen (Figure 4.4)

Well done, we can now display images. That’s a very important step. Of course, this code is just horrible, it’s a mashup of HelloTriangle and a few bits of things we added. It’s very rigid, and only good for one rectangle on screen at a time. It’s been hacked together to do one thing. But it works; now we must make it into something more usable and a lot tidier. It also helps demonstrate that the shader code we need to use is a little confusing, which can sometimes be the second hurdle after the triangle disconnection that holds new programmers up and scares them off.
But not to worry, we’re going to try to get past that confusion, trust me for a while so I can set some code up that will allow us to largely ignore the hardware and get some actual game coding done.

Displaying More Images

It’s not a complete project, I do want you to do some work	but code wise it is all there, what you need to do is go add those nice pictures of your own, put them in the assets folder and then include them as existing items in the Assets filter so you can visualise them in the editor.
