So, the simplicity of OBJ will eventually prove to be a problem when we want to do a lot of models or have a model with a massive number of frames.
An OBJ isn’t always composed of a single mesh though, one good thing in its favour is the fact that an OBJ can have multiple component meshes stored which make up the whole. If you look at our current render systems code, we do actually render multiple shapes inside a loop. These shapes are named; therefore, there are ways to isolate individual shape components and run them through a different MVP, creating some degree of motion inside an OBJ, like rotation of, or bouncing of wheels which you might want to play with later. But actual animation in the sense of moving from one pose to another isn’t in the OBJ bag of tricks.




Recognisable Environments	345
We can fake animation though, for example as suggested a few pages back, in the same way we did with our 2D images, where we replaced one graphic image for another, to create an apparent stop frame movement. If we have eight slightly different model meshes, representing eight frames of a walk, we can do a walk animation by simply replacing one model for another. Unlike sprite animations where we show a different graphic, all we need for model animations is a different set of vertices, pointing to the same textures.
But such a load and play for 3D animation is quite clunky and it also needs us to load multiple OBJ files to keep track of the frames, as well as make sure we have enough frames to make the animation smooth. It’s all possible, but really it would be a hack and looks quite bad. So, I am going to say this is a developmental dead end and leave it as a curiosity to try if you want to.
OBJs then are best suited to items that don’t animate, require some addi‑ tional detail or content and are preferably only loaded once. They are ideal for, but not restricted to, fixed terrains, unanimated detail objects and basically any‑ thing that is non‑dynamic in a game.
So, what can we use for animation? We clearly need to have animated mod‑ els, if OBJs are not really up to it, what other options are there?
We could of course write our own proprietary model format, if you think you’re ready for that feel free. But I’m not a massive fan of reinventing the wheel. There is a plethora of model formats around which are used mostly for anima‑ tion, and indeed for our level of processing power I think I have the perfect sys‑ tem to start us off.

Let’s Get That New Animation Done
MD2 animation was my goto system on the Pi3, since the Pi3 had quite a few lim‑ its on its OpenGLES 2.0 shaders that made what we’re about to describe, nearly impossible. What made it cool though was it was based on the fact that each key‑ frame consisted of a mesh of vertices, not unlike the multiple OBJ concept, and between two keyframes, there were vertices that had a direct one‑to‑one relation‑ ship. The vertices however may have been at different points in model space, and to animate you worked out a line between the start and end point for each vertex (frame 1 and frame 2) and then lerped along that line over time, this has the effect of altering the mesh to look like it is animating.
That should be simple to grasp, and we can see in old demos on the site it was effective, but there are a lot of meshes being stored, one for each keyframe, and the keyframes themselves are pretty unchangeable requiring an artist to work their magic on each of them to get any kind of new sequence. This made it hard to get a wide range of moves and motions, also switching between totally different sequences could be problematic as there are no intermediate keyframes to use. Oh, and let’s not forget that mesh sizes now compared to 10 years ago are much greater, so we would need massive buffers and time to process them using a mod‑ ern model with 100K plus vertices rather than 1K’s of old.
Now, in a rigged system, we also have common vertices, but they are located on a frame or rig or maybe easier to understand a skeleton. In MD2, all the frames, or meshes that make up the frames, have an equal number of vertices, on a rigged system we have one skeleton and one skin, which is a mesh or sequence of meshes, that saves a lot of memory (Figure 8.2).





346	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming


























Figure 8.2 Bald Vetruvian man by Pim Bos.


What’s cool is it’s the bones we animate, not the skin, at least not directly, that’s handled as we render. We deform the skin depending on how the bones are posed and moving over time to their next pos.
The vertices that make up the skin are connected to their closest bones at different points along the bone, or bones, and when the bones move, we calcu‑ late the new vertex position based on how much the bones it is attached to have moved between frames; in MD2, we lerp the vertices, now we lerp the bone. Move the bones, and we move the skin. Notice I said bones, plural, generally a vertex can be influenced by more than one bone, and we provide a value called a weight to each connection. This will get clearer soon.
So, with rrigged systems, we usually have one skin, that’s a saving, but we might have a large number of bone frame movement. But since a rig generally has a small number of bones, we can do a lot more with much less data allow‑ ing you to store really a large number of keyframe positions. It’s also possible to procedurally move bones to different positions allowing a R\rigged system to interact more appropriately with an uneven terrain, for example, or indeed to allow changes of sequences to feel more natural.






Recognisable Environments	347
Unlike a human skeleton, a rig is considerably simplified and usually based on two bones per joint, the number of joints being determined by how complex you need your motion to be. For a simple biped, we have our ankles, knees, pelvis, spine, shoulders, neck, elbows and wrists as a minimum. We can bend and rotate most of those joints and our flesh goes with it.
A very simple biped will look something like this (Figure 8.3):





























Figure 8.3  Focus on the rig by Pim Bos.


If we want more complexity and more flexibility, we can add more joints for a super flexible spine, or fingers and toes. But more complexity does mean more work… so keep it simple, if you don’t need individual fingers don’t add them.
To animate, we move the bones, the same idea as any stop frame animation, and we can pose our rig in an almost infinite number of ways. We can keep key‑ frames of these poses and “lerp”1 the movement from one frame to another, even if the next frame is procedurally generated (Figures 8.4 and 8.5).





1  Since we are also doing rotations, we will in fact use slerp, more later.


348	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming





















Figure 8.4 Hands up by Pim Bos.



























Figure 8.5 Hands up by Pim Bos.



Recognisable Environments	349
Connect the Mesh
Now that we understand the basics of how the rig moves, we need next to con‑ sider how the mesh attaches to it, since we never actually render the bones (except perhaps to debug) we want to see our models’ arms move, and if the upper arm moves then the lower arm moves with it, it may also (as with our arms) rotate as it moves. This gives us some idea into the flexibility of bone‑based movement but also hints at some of the complexity. Bones are connected, but the movement of one bone has a limited direction impact on bones above it, but a significant impact on bones below it. This is a hierarchy of systems and if we think of the joints as being positions and rotations of the bone… we might start to see that this is matrix maths again... That’s useful (Figure 8.6).







































Figure 8.6 Connect the skin by Pim Bos.


350	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
The actual rigging and animation of a model is still best done by an artist, one who specialises in rigging because it’s quite important that the vertices are correctly attached to the bones and that any movement of a joint does not over‑ stretch the mesh. But for the most part, a model from the programmer’s view‑ point is a good old T‑Pose and the vertices, rather than having offsets from a centre, have an offset from their nearest bones. I think that I’ve wafled enough; it’s time to get to work.
We need a few models to work with, since producing them is a bit of a chore, we can look for some free examples.
There are some lovely free models and animations available on Mixamo.com, you can use to experiment with. Looking through the characters there’s a nice Paladin character we can use. He’s probably a little bit vertex heavy for a full game on a low‑end system but for learning he’ll do nicely. Go to Mixamo.com and have a play around it’s free. And check out my video on the support site where I load a basic OBJ model into it and produce an animated rigged model with it. Being able to con‑ vert model formats and turn them into rigged systems gives you a lot of options to find decent online models for free to use in a project, or even to make your own with basic modelling skills and not having to worry much about the rigging (Figure 8.7).














Figure 8.7  Screenshot 1 from Mixamo.com.

As nice as the model is, the main thing we should care about is the rig, which looks like this (Figure 8.8).














Figure 8.8  Screenshot 2 from Mixamo.com.


Recognisable Environments	351
Using Maximo’s own chars is cool, and it allows us to produce a range of sequences and characters we can use in our upcoming demo.
Now, we have a basic method to create animated models (or we can down‑ load them) and we need a means to load models which have these skeletal rigs in place and a skin which we can deform, which you can imagine isn’t in OBJ. So, we need to use some new model formats and, in this case, a new model loader. Time to meet AssImp.

AssImp
AssImp, an abbreviation of Asset Importer, is a universal model loader that can load almost any type of model (57 at the time of writing) and turns the result‑ ing data into an intermediate structure called an aiscene. Very much like TinyOBJLoader, AssImp creates primary data of meshes and materials that define the basic shape and wrapping of a model, but also more importantly it can contain animation and skeletal data and bone information that allows us to load most of the common rigged model formats such as FBX or DAE (Collada) (both of which Mixamo exports).
All these concepts get stored in the aiscene structure and if we are not using them, they are set to nullptr. If you load an OBJ into an aiscene, it clearly has no animation or skeleton but will contain meshes and materials for each mesh. But an aiscene can also contain secondary information on lights, cameras, tex‑ tures, bones and animations. This is useful as FBX and DAE formats can come fully equipped with such info and being able to extract it with one load is very useful. Assimp is also constantly updating so new features are added as are new model formats.
When our render systems are set up to use an aiscene, it won’t matter if the original data was OBJ, STL, GLtf 1 or 2, FBX or DAE or any of the other common formats. If what we need is there, it will become accessible via the aiscene. Let’s set it up and demonstrate.
Assimp library is provided in the setup.sh libs, so all we need to do is include the libname in our linker and then we can access the headers like this;

#include "assimp/Importer.hpp" #include "assimp/scene.h" #include "assimp/postprocess.h"

We can see we have an importer, which is what does the actual work of loading models, a scene file to define the struct and methods to modify an aiscene, and a utility header to process the data into more useful content once we load it.
A simple model load looks like this.

void Model::LoadModel(std::string path)
{ // define some basic load flags for Mixamo style models #define ASSIMP_FLAGS (ai_Process_Triangulate | aiProcess_FlipUVs)
Assimp::Importer importer; // Assimps main read system is the importer
const aiScene* scene = importer.ReadFile(path, ASSIMP_ FLAGS); // we have several options for minor tweaks to content.
if (!scene || scene‑>mFlags & AI_SCENE_FLAGS_INCOMPLETE || !scene‑>mRootNode)
{
std::string errorString = "Embuggerance, Error Reported::";



352	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
errorString = errorString + importer. GetErrorString();
errorString += "\n"; printf(errorString.c_str()); // print it out return;
}
ProcessScene(scene‑>mRootNode, scene); // make the data }

ProcessScene needs a little explanation. Since our model can itself consist of several other models or subobjects within the “scene”, each of those needs to be extracted into usable data like bones and meshes which in turn is processed to produce simple vertices. You can look at the code in the demo to follow the details of this; what matters is at the end of this processing, our model will have some useful vectors of meshes, indices for element buffers, textures, perhaps ani‑ mation, and of course bones (if they exist) which we can then use to draw.

Rendering a Rigged Model
Animating and rendering a Rigged model are usually done in different but linked steps, animation focusing on the movement of the rig itself, accounting for any procedural restrictions or transitions as well as the more complex connection of bones that move causing other bones to move (Forward Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics). So, let’s focus first on rendering, so we can see a result when we start to animate.
The model is stored in what is technically called a Bind Pose, but for bipeds that essentially is always a T‑pose (well sometimes an A‑pose, but that’s just a very tired T‑pose), as always the skin or vertex data is a soup of vertices with attached bone ID’s and weights, in a model space, but when we render it, our nodes base transform provides an offset from position of the Bind Pose and cal‑ culate new vertex positions based on the bones it is attached to.
Once loaded, we can think about rendering, the process isn’t much different from an OBJ model render and should really be our new generic rendering sys‑ tem. TinyOBJLoader has served its purpose well and can still be useful since it is easier to edit some of its output to our needs.
Now to the shaders… this is where the bone attachment comes into play, we need our shaders to have information on the bones and matrices to transform them.
Generally, on a Pi we would try to keep the bone count to a minimum, but if we’re going to be using downloaded models, we’re just going to have to accept that our bone counts may be high. But that’s ok, OpenGLES 3.1 on Pi has enough uniform matrices to cope with a lot. We’ll assume (never assume, but always do a test to avoid overloading) a max count of 64 bones in total, and a max of 4 pos‑ sible bones being used by a vertex, in practice most of the vertices will attach to 1 bone, a few to 2 and a very few to 3. It’s unlikely, but never impossible to have more than 4 but… 4 just happens to be an easy value for us to work with since a vec4 has… 4 values. (In fact, some systems can have 32 bones vertex… that’s extreme.)

const int MAX_BONES = 64; // probably overkill
const int MAX_BONES_PER_VERTEX = 4; // probably only 1 or 2

uniform mat4 MVP; // model view projection provided per model uniform mat4 BonesMatrices[MAX_BONES]; //up to 64 is good



Recognisable Environments	353
Our CPU will provide these matrices after completing of an animation step and a draw setup, which comes next, so to start with these will all be default matrices. We can now turn our attention to our input/attributes. We’re comfortable using position, normal and texture coords, but for animate we will have a few more values in our mesh that we want to get into our system, we are going to include a few things we don’t currently care about though as they will be part of
the mesh.

precision highp float;
layout (location = 0) in vec3 aPos; layout (location = 1) in vec2 aTexCoords;
layout (location = 2) in ivec4 aBoneIDs; // use ints as indices. layout (location = 3) in vec4 aWeights;

As before, we need a position which in this case is a base position and our stan‑ dard texture co‑ordinates. We will later add some lighting attributes, but for now, let’s focus on the animation.
The two most interesting for animation are aBoneIDs and aWeights… these two values have four components each, and we can index them aBoneID[0–3], aWeights[0–3] and of course can use a variable to loop.. So… we can get the bone, and the amount of influence it has on the vertex and combine them in a simple loop

void main()
{	// Animation steps through the 4 possible bones
vec4 totalPosition = vec4(0.0);
for (int i = 0; i <  MAX_BONES_PER_VERTEX; i++)//4 possible bones
{
if (aBoneIDs[i] == ‑1) continue; // don’t consider this if (aBoneIDs[i] >= MAX_BONES)
{// unlikely to happen but this avoids errors totalPosition = vec4(aPos, 1.0);
break; // stop the loop and use the base }
// we can use the bone value to index to the correct matrix vec4 localPos = BonesMatrices [aBoneIDs[i]] * vec4(aPos, 1.0);
totalPosition += localPos * aWeights[i]; }
// the position has been moved/influenced by the new bone position, so now what is the final vertex using the traditional mvp * pos
gl_Position = MVP * totalPosition; // get final position }

Most of our work on animation is done here in the vertex shader, so we don’t have to do massive amounts of different content for our fragment shader, so let’s get these models loaded and rendered.
We need a draw system that processes the usual list of meshes but also feeds the bone matrices to the shader, so let’s set that up. Clearly, we need to pass our usual things, like MVP, but also a matrix for the orientation and positions of each bone

void Model::Draw(glm::mat4& MVP, std::vector<glm::mat4>& finalBoneMatrices,GLuint shaderID)
{ // send MVP then end the all bone uniforms we have glUniformMatrix4fv(glGetUniformLocation(shaderID, "MVP"), 1, GL_
FALSE, &MVP[0][0]);
for (unsigned int i = 0; i <  BoneMatrices.size(); i++)



354	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
{
std::string name = "BonesMatrices[" + std::to_string(i) + "]"; // name of matrix
glUniformMatrix4fv(glGetUniformLocation(shaderID, name.c_str()), 1, GL_FALSE, &BoneMatrices[i][0][0]);
}
// now do the meshes itself
for (int i = 0; i <  meshes.size(); i++) {
meshes[i].Draw(); }
}

In the demo, we’ve set up texture and VAOs 1st, but we do still have to send the uniforms each time we draw a set of meshes that form a model, we’d probably also send lighting info and a few other things when we have a better understand‑ ing of things.
And now we have loaded a model that isn’t an OBJ, and it displays and renders fine in its default T‑pose… Feel free to find and load other models in different for‑ mats, don’t be surprised though if some models you download get a bit messed up, even within formats, some variation occurs, and we usually have to make small fixes with an art package like Blender, to make them work, but once fixed the models are usable, also try exporting in DEA instead of FBX. I find DEA to be a bit friendlier, but FBX is also nearly always going to be good. AssImp won’t care (Figure 8.9).









Figure 8.9 Let’s use this guy.

Let’s go to the next step the animation itself.

Let’s Dance
Or we can just walk on the spot, but we need to have a model and animation data. Mixamo allows us to export models with or without skin. That means we can load a skin in, check out a base sequence, like IDLE, and save it out with the skin, we can then decide on our next sequence, e.g. RUN. Load one of those on Mixamo and review it, then save it out, but this time without skin. That will make your 2nd and subsequent exports only have animation data. Be careful of this, sometimes an FBX/DEA isn’t a complete model, and it may indeed only be the animation data, designed to work with a base model that contains the rig. Try saving out a few animations of your own to add to the demo later, there are indeed some dance moves you might find fun to try.

An Animator Class
We need to be able to do certain obvious things, like setting a sequence, then animating through it, and then deciding if it is done, or should loop or even move to a new sequence that we can queue up.


Recognisable Environments	355
For now, we’re only doing the IDLE, so that’s going to be a simple looped sequence. The primary purpose of this class is to take our current keyframe, interpolate the motion and rotation between the current and next frames and then produce the required sequence of transformation matrices which can go to the shader.
We will need to define a few things straight away, like a vector for the matri‑ ces and what the current animation is, we also need to clarify that an animation depends on context, data for animation, a name for the result of using that data and a data container class that holds the specific time‑based data to make the sequence appear to animate, clear as mud eh... but context will make it clearer.
Our 1st pass at the class will look something like this;

class Animator {
public:
Animator(Animation* animation, std::string animName); ~Animator();

void UpdateAnim(float deltaTime); // process the current anim void SetAnim(std::string animName); //what sequence to do void AddAnim(Animation* animation, std::string animName); //map
to the list
void GatherBones(Animation* animation, Model& model);// void CalculateTransforms(const AnimNode* node, glm::mat4
parentTransform);
Animation* currentAnimation = nullptr; // keep track std::map<std::string, Animation*> animations; // a nice list float currentTime; // current offset time is important std::vector<glm::mat4> BoneMatrices; // the bones

};

That AnimNode will get clearer, soon!
Ok so far, but what do we work on, oh year. Bones… So, we need to provide a concept of what a bone is, this is fairly universal idea and again it’s a class con‑ taining some structs. We can produce bones as objects that mainly keep track of their position and rotation, as well as its possible scale, even though that may not change much in normal biped motion it can change a lot in other types of deform, so we’ll build it in.
Since we work on keyframes it does mean that we can have the same bone in a great many positions, so we’ll have to know how many keyframes we have when we load the animation, but since we don’t know till we load we will use vectors, but vectors of what? First, let’s define a couple of structs.

struct BonePosition {
glm::vec3 position; };

struct BoneRotation {
glm::quat orientation; };

Good so far? But one more minor tweak is needed. Interpolation requires change over time… It’s not impossible for the transform to start interpolating at differ‑ ent points along a timeline, this is why I didn’t define it as a single vec3 or quat. It needs to be paired with a timeframe, or time stamp that indicates how far along


356	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
an animation timeline we are. We can add time to each struct and later we can add other things if we feel they are relevant to bones. Since we use a vector of these structs, adding to them is simple.

struct BonePosition {
glm::vec3 position; float TimeStamp;
};

Our class for a bone will start to shape up now, like this

class Bone {
glm::mat4 m_LocalTransform = mat4(1); // the bone needs its
start transform
std::vector<BonePosition> m_Positions; std::vector<BoneRotation> m_Rotations;
std::vector<BoneScale> m_Scales; //generally bipeds don’t scale but not impossible

// we need methods to update and control but they are coming

}

The constructor and other methods can be found in the downloaded code, what’s important is that we can construct a bone, and its core data is able to load up all the keyframes position, rotation and scale from an animation sequence we load from an AnimNode, which I can explain now, is what the animation data provides us about particular movement of a particular bone.
All semi‑sensible so far, but we do need to clarify what an animation class is, aside from having the means to actually load the animation data, it needs to store it and create those animation nodes so that we can pass information to the final bones.
These bones themselves will get collected into a Model class which rather than the old OBJModel, will focus on the rig, while also containing VAO/VBO and shader/texture data.
We will need a data structure to store our collection of bones and some methods to extract, build and store them. Since bones are (usually) named with a string, we can use a std::map structure, which keeps track with its name as a key and its general ID and some info on its transform from model space to bone space…(another space…don’t worry too much about that, it is provided). We can get quite a lot of info from the aiscene and more info from the animation data when we start to consider that.
That node data is key to this; basically, every bone can have several other bones attached to it, so a single bone is a parent to other bones, who singly in turn can have other child bones. This is why we have bone space, so that we can transform the children of our bones.
A Node defines the bone and stores the name and initial transform but also gives access to the children of that node. It’s usually a hierarchical system, so that means we need a root node of some kind. But 1st define what a node is for us.

struct AnimNodeData {
std::string name; // bones usually have a text name, usually Bone0, 1 2 etc


Recognisable Environments	357
glm::mat4 transformation; // the transform that will get passed std::vector<AnimNodeData> children; // keeps track of its
immediate offspring
int NumberOfChildren = 0;//we can get this from .size(); but this is quicker
};

We define this in our animation class when we start as a Root node, and as we load animations from aiscene, we build on this. The importer lets us load the scene as always then we get to the meat of the animation nodes.

const aiScene* scene = importer.ReadFile(Filename, aiProcess_Triangulate);

// pull the important info from the scene
Animation* animation = scene‑>mAnimations[0]; // scene provides animation
duration = (float) animation‑>mDuration; ticksPerSecond = (float)animation‑>mTicksPerSecond;

We can see that aiscene has some of our content ready and some important stuff like duration of the animation and the frame rate as ticks per second.
We will need to now go through all the animation data we just loaded, make new nodes and fill the data up. This usually gets done with a standard function

SetHierarchyData(rootNode, scene‑>mRootNode);

There’s no need for me to repeat the whole function here, it’s in the code, but you can imagine it simply reads the root node from the data loaded and recursively creates a series of nodes and subnodes and subnodes of subnodes, etc., until every node is loaded and contained in this Animation.
Now when an animation is happening, the animation data itself only moves the bones it needs to, and it references them all by name, and there may indeed be quite some number. But until we animate, we don’t have a complete list of all the names of the bones in the rig. So, whenever we load an animation, we have to check all the bones that will be used and assign an index value to it which we can keep in the Animation structure. Once we have loaded from the aiscene, we can gather the bones needed and if any are new to the Models bone map we add them. The animation will build up the bone name list, as new animations are loaded.

void GatherUnknownBones(aiAnimation* animation, Model& model);

And finally (at last) we can update the animation once it’s all set up with a simple call to
void Animator::UpdateAnimation(float dt)

{
if (CurrentAnimation) // make sure we have one { // work on the animators time values
m_CurrentTime += m_CurrentAnimation‑>GetTicksPerSecond() * dt;
CalculateTransforms(CurrentAnimation‑>GetRootNode(), glm::mat4(1.0f));
} }

We simply check if we have an animation, if so, we increment time and then starting at the root node to calculate the impact of the animation values on each matrix from a standard I matrix start point. Since this function is recursive, it

358	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
will call itself with new matrix data as it travels down the node tree of the rig, resulting in all the bones updating and storing their data.
The code itself will make this a bit easier to see how it’s all working, but once we apply the correct interpolation to each node, the bones move, and we already know that has an impact on how the vertices display and we have animation.
Run the demo and we should see IDLE working fine. We can expand this now, adding lighting and more animation frames to really complete this section (Figures 8.10 and 8.11).



























Figure 8.10 IDLE.














Figure 8.11 Getting your bad self down on the dance floor.


Recognisable Environments	359
Now We Control Things
It’s going to be up to our main game code to control which sequences we play, and how long we play them for. For this simple demo, we just want to do walking, idle and we can press a key for dancing or any other sequence we choose. We’re not going to get into too much detail here as we are just making a demo, not a game here which would require a lot of actions to be triggered. But it’s good to under‑ stand the process of triggering and stopping animation and to note what happens when we want to transition sequences.

Controlling the Sequences
Ok so in principle at least, we understand how to render and play our animations, but we do still need some kind of control and a means to produce those impor‑ tant transform bone matrices. We need to use some kind of animation data that can be applied to our rigged systems.
Using Maximo, I have produced a number of sequences that we can use and are included in the ModelAnimation Demo on the support site.
Since only one of my FBX files has a mesh and a rig, the main one, PaladinIdle. fbx we load that as our starting point, we’ll load the others once we know we can control the IDLE.
I’ve given you a simple animation system, which assumes only a few states which trigger sequences, you can add more yourself by selecting something from Maximo that meets your needs and adding it to the list of animations to load and play. Make sure you use the Paladin character. Now’s your chance to add that cool Dance sequence.

Combining Animations
One nice feature of the old MD2/3 system was that all your animation sequences were contained in a single model and only needed 1 load. That is not directly possible with Mixamo models, as a rule (currently) you can only export a single character and a single animation (or no character but a single animation).
This is another situation where your tame artist can come to help you, but it’s not the hardest thing in the world to combine them, if you are familiar with the free 3D modelling software, Blender.
It’s possible to load multiple separate animations into Blender and…well, blend them together. And then export your model with its single mesh, texture and rig but with all your animation sequences named and stored in the file in a format of your choosing. That will save you having to do the multiple load we have now, which is a bit of a time killer.
That, however, is more of an art package tutorial than a coding tutorial, so I suggest you to have a look on YouTube for one of the many online walk‑throughs on how to do this. Coders should have some basic skills with art packages for simple tasks like this…but don’t be tempted by the dark side.

Blending Sequences
Now all our animations work just fine, and we can choose which sequence we want in our control code, but you might have noticed that the swap or transi‑ tion from one sequence to another is kind of off. It snaps from one sequence to another, which isn’t very realistic.



360	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Animation sequences have a known start point and that start point usu‑ ally isn’t going to be the same or anywhere close to the keyframe of the current sequence as it moves along the timeline, also a lerp from the current keyframe to a new 1st keyframe may look odd, but can be valid for some transitions, like lying down to standing up.
Because we have bones in our animations it’s possible to transition between two animation sequences by blending the two sequences, using a simple blend factor, which decides how much of one sequence (the new one) gets to influence the other (the old one), which we increase over a variable timeframe, until the 1st sequence is phased out of the blend and the new sequence comes in fully. You should recognise now that this is another interpolation process between pairs of matrices, and we use the result to render our vertices.
It’s not a perfect system of course, we still need to be selective in our choice of sequence, a transition from a RUN to a GETUPFROMFLOOR would make no sense if we saw it, but a transition from RUN to FALL, to GETUPFROMFLOOR to RUN would look fine and a transition from GETUPFROMFLOOR up to STAND may not require the IDLE animation to actually kick in until the stand is fully blended. These are animation choices you must make. But blending works well for most transitions of sequences and removes that sharp judder from one to another.
You can enable the BlendAnimation flag to see this in action in the demo, now instead of an immediate switch, the blending factor comes into play.
Having our models animating smoothly and ready to be moved under con‑ trol is a big step, but without some form of ground to walk on it’s little more than a 3D sprite floating around in an empty universe. If we plan to have a character animating and doing things in a world, we must take another big step and finally get to the main topic of this chapter, environments.

Explaining Environments
Although not all games need a big world, there does need to be some virtual space concept where the objects you control/avoid/interact with, can exist and your camera can view.
The type of environment you choose will vary according to your game. Most 3D free‑moving games will require a world of some kind and in turn that world will probably have a data structure behind it, depending on its size, it might use an opti‑ misation system like a partition tree to access it for logic and sometimes rendering.
I’m once again going to have to explain a few things before we can leap into our next 3D game. Sorry, I know these long explanations can get a bit boring, but they might help you to understand the route we are taking despite you having possible knowledge of better routes. The road to coding enlightenment is often paved with some dumb ideas we thought were clever at the time and experience eventually shows us how stupid we can be.
3D Space was easy because our expectations of what happens in space are not fixed in our brains not too many of us have been in space! But environments that we allow things to interact with, in more or less real‑world style, require a whole host of considerations we must try to work out and make as realistic as possible. So, let’s try 1st to outline some of the technical things we have to incorporate.

The Ground, the Place Where You Will Fall To!
In 2D games, it’s very easy to think of the ground as just being a solid tile or area that our sprite falls to and cannot fall through, gravity acted as we expected


Recognisable Environments	361
until we hit something that we considered solid and stopped gravity. But in 3D we must think of the ground a little differently. For our basic purposes, we can consider any flat plane to be ground and have it be level with the up/down Y axis at 0, so if our object wants to move around on this plane we just make sure his Y coordinate is 0, or returns to 0 if he jumps or gets blown off his feet.
That will be a perfectly acceptable way to do things, if the world was made up of perfectly flat play fields. True, we can arrange to have arenas or playing fields that are flat, but it’s a very dull view. We need to consider the undulations and changes in height of a typical terrain. Whether it’s a grassy field, or a city with roads and kerbs and steps to buildings we have a considerable challenge to know what we are stepping on and how it will change our height above that base 0 level.
There are also matters of visualisation. To work well, a ground plane will need to extend some distance ahead of us, we will need to see where we are going, and it needs to look good.
We’ll examine the issues in turn to see what we can do to overcome them. But let’s first have a play around with a very simple single quad ground plane and get a character walking around on it. Download the GroundPlaneDemo from the site and get it running.
You will see a simple character on a very large single quad ground plane. But one quad, on its side stretching one texture to such extremes isn’t giving us much sense of ground; it’s also stretching the individual pixels of the texture so much that it ends up looking like a horrible mosaic when close up.
We could try a higher‑res texture, but our texture limit is around 2048´2048 pixels, and our plane width is far in excess of that, so stretching is inevitable, but we will still get the same issue over time if we have a large ground plane.
Though strictly speaking good texture design, usage and placement in a mesh is the job of an artist, we at least need to be aware of how to make the best use of our textures to make the artists job a bit easier. Also, we can make use of some cool concepts of maths to improve the visuals.

A Simple Ground Plane
The type of ground you use will of course make a lot of differences to your type of game. But if we plan to have a flat point to jump, run, drive, take off from and splat into from a height, a ground plane of some kind is needed! We can visualise and create a flat ground pretty easily as a simple quad, or very large pair of tri‑ angles, and if we display it in monochrome with a basic shader, that will be fine. That’s not uncommon for debugging.
But for all but the simplest games, a ground plane should be made up of a number of triangles; it’s easier for the GPU to cull the unwanted parts allowing you to create quite a complex ground plane. The extra resolution of more triangles per unit square also has the extra welcome effect of allowing us to manipulate the component triangles of the ground plane, especially the Y component of the ver‑ tices to give height and undulation to our plane. The more detail you have in your plane the smoother these height effects can be. Mapping your terrain to another data source to provide texture data and object placement can also then allow a lot more variation for not much data. Unlike a very large OBJ with multiple added OBJs or subshapes to provide detail like trees, grass and rocks. This is the begin‑ ning of procedural generation of terrains and is quite an exciting subject.
For  now,  I’ve  included  a  very  simple  Ground  Plane  class  in  your GroundPlaneDemo. It works by generating two initial triangles, to represent


362	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
the outer corners of a square terrain, then recursively splitting that into two and generating as many triangles as you need by setting a maximum level of recursion.
So, you start with two base triangles, it works out the mid‑point of each tri‑ angle, splits it into two and then splits those two into two and so on. The result is as many or as few triangles making up your ground plane as you need. Don’t set the MAX_TRIS level too high though, recursion like this is based on powers of 2 doubled, so you very quickly go from 2 triangles to 8192 in only 11 steps. As with all things you must find a balance, more triangles give a greater detail but increased GPU usage, it’s a variable rate though, and you can add other variables to alter the detail later.
Simply add GroundPlane files cpp/h to your future projects if needed, create an instance of a GroundPlane in your game initialisation, set your MAX_TRIS value and then call Init() to load and create textures, and Generate() to create a vector of the vertices. Which you can decide should go into a VBO or remain in the client RAM to test against. Update will place it flat, but rendering is then up to you depending on how you store it. I’ll use a VBO for now and my render system is based on that.
Sadly, after teasing you with a bit of procedural generation, we’re not going to go any further, but we can tinker with it a little bit. There are a few extra options added to the GroundPlaneDemo to alter the height of parts of the mesh making up the plane (when set to a MAX_TRIS level above 8) both randomly and using a heightmap. These are useful concepts to keep hold of, but not of much use to us currently. Study the code to see how they work, since we are not going to use them in our main demos, I won’t write them up, we still have a lot to cover before we can get back to coding something fun.

Level of Detail
The GroundPlane class touches on the idea of altering the level of detail, with its MAX_TRIS value, but what exactly does that mean? LOD methods basically take into account the fact that the further something is from our viewpoint the harder it is to make out detail, so a low‑detail object far away is not going to look much different from a high‑detail object equally far away. This can be apparent if we have a high poly model in our extreme view, and it may have a few thousand triangles but it’s probably only going to look like a few dozen are rendered. Our GPU is obligated to try to draw the thousand triangles even if it ends up looking like a blob due to the distance. If, however, we had a selection of models of lower level of detail, we could ask the GPU to render an appropriate model depending on the distance from our viewpoint (Figure 8.12).










Figure 8.12  The detail is in the clogs by Pim Bos.



Recognisable Environments	363
If done well with a good selection of LOD models, we would not really notice the change in mesh. But there is of course a downside to these LOD models, they will take up space. The compromise between performance and memory always comes into play. But performance nearly always wins the debate if memory is available, and we should try to make it so. Look at this example in the next screenshot. Here, we see 30 cars in rows. But the further cars are using a small 168 triangle mesh, the middle ones use 1338, and the ones at the front use 2146. If you look very closely you might see a few small differences, but really how close do you look at a model moving quickly on the screen? The large reduction in mesh size has a very direct impact on the amount of processing your GPU has to do, so making sure you add an LOD option to your draw options can be a big performance boost (Figure 8.13).
















Figure 8.13 As far as the eye can see.

This might be clearer to see if we render these models as GL_LINE_STRIPS rather than GL_TRIANGLES, a quick, cheap, but not very accurate way to repli‑ cate wireframe on GLES (Figure 8.14).
















Figure 8.14 Just the vertices.



364	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
But we’re not talking models yet, we’re talking about our ground, and our ground is currently one single variable‑sized mesh, so there’s no (simple) efi‑ cient way of operating an LOD system at least on an environment we design and is mappable, though procedural generation using systems like ROAM2  can be very effective. It would be fairly trivial to turn the GroundPlane class into a basic working ROAM system, calling the generate function each frame in the update with a few variable details, but that won’t really suit our current needs.
So, if we can’t easily reduce the mesh info, what else is open to us? Well, stick around, there are a few things we can do that are fairly simple and effective, and a few things that are very complex and perhaps not so effective on our targets. We should deal with some of the cheap easy things first.
Some free tools also allow you to procedurally produce environments in different levels of detail as you need. This avoids the problem of our limited resources being asked to do more than they can cope with and instead, makes things available for loading. L3DT from Bundysoft is especially useful for creat‑ ing large terrains and splitting them into usable sections, try it out, there is a free and low‑cost pro version, and how to use it will become more apparent with experimentation.
On the other side of the scale, if you need a massive sweeping terrain of mountains, lakes and fields of gold, there are a lot of massive number of plug‑ins for 3D software and even some specialised professional tools to create terrain con‑ tent, like Gaea which is free for non‑commercial use so you can at least try it out.
But if you want to get full features you will have to pay a reasonable amount, but it won’t break the bank. Equally World Creator, which also comes in under $200 for an indie license, is a good option but be warned these are professional packages that require a bit of effort to learn to use well.
Generally, if you are working only on a Pi these high‑level systems are mas‑ sive overkill, but how detailed and big you make your terrains is up to you, and if you can create good‑enough render systems you can handle quite a fair amount of terrain detail.

Mipmapping
This brings us to a concept called Mipmapping. Mipmapping is a means of taking a basic texture, halving its resolution by simple but precise scaling which reduces its level of detail, storing that in a way our GPU can easily access, then taking that and making a quarter‑sized version, then an 8th and so on (Figure 8.15).









2  ROAM – Real‑time Optimally Adapting Mesh is a level of detail algorithm that does in fact allow us to create variable detail in a terrain around the area that is visible from a camera. It works best with procedurally generated terrains and is not easily implemented in a user‑mapped environ‑ ment. But can be a great method of creating endless wilderness‑based terrains that can render in real time. Look it up, it’s very easy to code, but not quite suitable for the games we are trying to produce.



Recognisable Environments	365


















Figure 8.15  MipMapped texture. (Image from Wikipedia, used under the GNU Free Documentation License.)


The effective result is that you have one main texture and four or five pro‑ gressively half‑sized slightly reduced‑quality versions. The scaled versions clearly are lower res but represent a better texture to use if you are viewing the texture from a distance or using an LOD model, where the main image no longer needs to be scaled by so much.
This can dramatically improve the overall quality of the texture sampling at different viewpoints but does come at the relatively high cost of around 50% more texture memory needed for the mipmaps. However, we also get a speed boost, since the GPU is not scaling things so much to fit in smaller spaces. How much of a boost will vary but it’s more than worth the effort.
Implementing mipmaps is especially easy as GPUs are very much in favour of using them and OpenGLES has the ability to automatically generate and use mip‑ map textures if it’s told about them. Even better it can automatically make them using the  glGenerateMipmap(GL_TEXTURE_2D) function already in place in OpenGLES. You do need to ensure you have applied the appropriate fil‑ ters when you create the texture and load in its image. The ModelManager class has appropriate filters in place around line 150ish, uncomment these lines and also a few lines down, look for these commands:

glGenerateMipmap(GL_TEXTURE_2D);

and:

glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER, GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_LINEAR); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MAG_FILTER, GL_NEAREST_MIPMAP_NEAREST);





366	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
And you will now be automatically using MipMaps every time you load an OBJ with TinyOBJLoader. Given the power we have now on Raspberry 4/5 you prob‑ ably won’t notice the speed increase, but it’s there. Any speed increase is well worth a bit of effort, but take care, not to fill all your GPU RAM. You should also consider making the Mipmapping optional on models you are not likely to scale or show moving away from the camera.
Mipmapping also highlights the fact that the GPU really prefers to use smaller textures when it’s rendering the pixels, ideally as close to a one‑to‑one relationship as possible. Even without Mipmaps, using the smallest possible tex‑ ture can give a big speed improvement. It’s again down to balance, the more we can fit in a texture the easier it is to add detail to a model, but the bigger the tex‑ ture is, the more work the GPU has to do to scale the texture to fit the resolution of the pixels it’s trying to draw.

Filtering
We can’t talk about Mipmapping though without talking about filtering, which relies on our understanding that when in GPU memory, our texture pixels as we see them are not actually pixels as the GPU sees them, in GPU memory they are called texels, and they essentially act as the data we are going to store in the pixels of our framebuffer which will be displayed.
Normally in an ideal one‑to‑one scale situation 1 texel will equal 1 pixel, but if you operate any kind of scaling you have to use more or less texels to make your pixel?
If you scale by 4 for example then your 1 pixel now has to occupy 16 pixels (4´4) but there is only 1 texel in the GPU that corresponds to the pixel, so that texel gets repeated in all 16 pixels that’s an example of magnification.
At the other extreme is when you scale down, called minification, where you have a situation where you may have more texels going into 1 pixel, and some‑ thing has to be done to merge the different texels into 1 pixel.
Both magnification and minification have downsides, they can create sharp blocky graphics on magnify and of course you lose detail in minification.
The blockiness is caused by the simple repetition of texels to fill the space, known as Nearest Neighbour interpolation. It’s a perfectly viable system and you may want to have blocky scaling, but most of the time we want to smooth it out. To reduce the blockiness, you can use other interpolation methods built into the GPU, often referred to as anti‑aliasing, though technically it’s known as bilinear interpolation. Instead of throwing 1 texel into all 16 possible pixels, it looks at the colour of adjacent texels and smooths or blends the colours outward. Creating a much smoother image which is more pleasing to the eye.
Though best results are seen in magnification, minification can also benefit from bilinear interpolation but the loss of detail is a fact of life. The smooth‑ ing effect of the interpolation can make that less harsh on the eye if the image is static, but movement at a distance will create a lot of noise in the image as it will use a different set of texels each update to create the pixels, so how do we overcome this? Our mipmaps will contain scaled texture samples that allow us or rather the GPU to use a texture that is a more appropriate size for the scale that







Recognisable Environments	367
is being minified; this will greatly reduce the noise as there won’t be a massive difference in the texels used to produce the pixels.
Finally, there is Trilinear Filtering which helps us to avoid a jump effect when the GPU switches from one mipmap to another. It does this from taking samples from the next and previous textures in the scale providing a kind of in‑between set of values from one texture image to another.
I could put in a lot of diagrams to explain this, but really, it’s quite a simple thing to see on screen. On the support site, you will find a short demo called FilterDemo, fire it up and have a look.
Our demo is pretty simple, and we start with a simple quad, clearly over‑ stretched. Pressing 1 will introduce Mipmapping for an immediate improvement in visuals and maybe even a bit more speed.
Pressing 2 gives us Bilinear filtering, and we can now see a lot less blockiness and a smoother though perhaps slightly blurry image. The blurriness is really down to the crazy choice of spreading one texture so thin. Press 3, to use a repeat‑ ing system.
Pressing 4 will demonstrate the Trilinear filters. Not bad, eh?
There is a computational expense to doing filtering, but at most it will only cost a few FPS and most GPUs are highly optimised to cope with at least a 4x1 texel filter (set in the EGL attributes in Graphics.h), though it is sometimes pos‑ sible to increase that range the performance drop becomes marked and not many SBCs are actually capable of doing more. Filtering in and using MipMapping can make the combination of the two an effective way to handle graphics and reduce noise or scaling issues.

We Don’t All Live in the Netherlands
So visually, we have made our simple ground plane appear nicer, but really all we have is a massive flat stamp, it may be mathematically a ground plane but it’s just dull to look at and will be quite dull to play. Unless you enclose it in walls and make it into an arena, it’s a very limited playfield.
We can use various procedural techniques to alter the height of our tri‑ angles which make up the meshes, and in so doing, create variation in the maps. We can overlay different textures, again based on height, or some other data structure that maps to our mesh. But in general, all this comes into the realm of procedural methods for making gamefields look moderately interest‑ ing, and as with so many other things is easier to get someone else to do it. That’s not to say that procedural generation isn’t a great option, it is indeed a pinnacle of coding skill, but like many things our little SBCs are going to struggle to do justice to the modern methods of creating detailed landscapes and simply don’t have the space/power/graphic capacity to do it well, so for now I’m going to abandon attempts at procedural worlds. The older methods our systems can handle don’t really cut it, so let’s not waste our time. Our art‑ ists and terrain creators can do a better job, so let them have the glory, we’ll use their results. But if you want to try it out, do some research on procedural terrains, especially heightmaps.







368	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Using an OBJ File – The Simple Solution
We can of course make use of a simple OBJ as our terrain, so long as it’s not too big and does not contain billions of triangles; this can be very effective for games with small playfields and like 2D tiles, it’s possible to stitch lots of them together to make larger worlds (Figure 8.16).















Figure 8.16 A simple scene.

How Far Above Sea Level Are We?
There is one obvious thing about a terrain, at least a good one that we need to address; it’s bumpy, it has little highs and lows. If we want our characters to walk ON that ground, as it rises and falls, they need some sense of height for them to make full and logical contact with the ground our gravity is pulling them onto.
This is totally simple if we have a totally flat plane, we just test for a known value, but our procedurally generated or loaded OBJ terrains are totally variable, almost every point in the mesh is going to have a different height value.
A heightmap if used can give us some sense of height a section has but it’s going to be an average of a few different sample faces, and we’re not likely to have a heightmap that has a one‑to‑one pixel resolution. It was always an approxima‑ tion of the height of an area with some smoothing to make it look smooth.
So, it’s time to think about our ground as part of our collision systems, at least as a special part. For any object/character we put in the world, if it’s going to have gravity it needs to know where the ground is directly under it and that requires us to test a lot of polys in a mesh.
Now the principle of this is pretty straightforward, the practice however is a bit more complex, there is some magic maths that will allow us to look directly below our feet/talon/pogo springs as the character needs and get the height. But that maths is actually pretty simple and breaks down into a series of code sec‑ tions to test for interactions between a “ray” and different types of mathematical object, planes, cubes, spheres, etc.
What is this ray concept? It is for all intents and purposes a simple vector, starting at a point in space, like for example the middle of your Model, and head‑ ing out into space in any given direction, like for example, straight down!
We know our terrain is made up of triangles, and I just told you we can test for ray intersecting with triangles, so we should now be able to make a pretty cool leap to realise that triangles can be used for more than just rendering.


Recognisable Environments	369
Interacting With Our World Collision Maps
If we look at any terrain geometry we plan to use as our ground, strip away its tex‑ tures we see it is clearly made up of polys, usually a mix of triangles. These tend to be optimised for drawing, but they can also be used as noted as possible collision sys‑ tems. A collision map or mesh is a stored reference map of all the triangles we use to draw, in essence as well as using meshes to render we can use them to detect collision. One thing we know about the ground is that it’s below us, we stand on it, grav‑
ity, drags us down to its level and forces us to rest at the highest point under our feet. Because gravity is uniform in its straight down nature, we can simulate the concept of gravity using a ray or a line pointing straight down. Defined from the
central reference point of our model, and a direction going straight down.
A Ray to Triangle calculation is actually pretty simple even though it has a horrible messy big maths formula. It is a basic concept that will be used a lot so it really is something we are going to need to understand, but explaining how it works in maths terms is, as usual a bit beyond me but I’ll try, with a bit of code I wrote what seems like 50 years or so ago when I was able to understand these things, and which I still use.

//Ray equation // Q = P + t*Dir //
// Plane equation
// Ax + By + Cz + D = 0 //
// normal.Q + D = 0
// normal.(P + t*Dir) + D = 0
// normal.P + t*normal.Dir + D = 0 //
// t = ‑(D + normal.P) //	‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑‑ //        normal.Dir //
// For any plane, D is the distance from the origin to the plane. // By definition, D can be computed by calculating the negative // of the normal dotted with any vertex on the plane.
// D = ‑normal.vertex0 //

Ok, it made sense to me at the time, when broken down into code it’s pretty straightforward and can be coded in less than 25 lines of moderately sane code which you can find in the RayIntersectTriangle method I put in the GroundPlane class. It’s functional but I’m unlikely to use it as we will have better methods coming along, but it is an interesting example of how to take a maths formula you don’t understand and break it down into code that might be easier to understand. If the collision mesh itself is kept in some kind of reasonable data structure
like a Quad/Oct Tree, finding out if we hit a particular triangle is a pretty simple case of running a Ray to Triangle intersection system on a subset of the mesh to check for a hit.
The upshot of this maths stuff is that we have a means to test if there is some‑ thing under us, and as a bonus there’s also a means to return the actual coordi‑ nate of where we hit that triangle. This is wonderfully useful so long as we’ve made reasonable efforts to not have to test for every single triangle in the mesh. In fact, that’s a very important point, any decent mesh for a terrain is going to consist of


370	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
1000’s of triangles, and we are probably only standing on one or two. We need to do something to avoid doing hundreds of pointless tests, which requires us to be a little smart about how we keep track of the data and use a collision map.
Our collision map can be anything from a simple grid‑based system, where we keep an array containing vectors, or a Quad Tree where we delve into the quad until we have a small number of objects to test.
As long as our terrain has only one level of play area, both these methods are perfectly acceptable. If, however, we have different levels for example in a build‑ ing with floors where you allow travel freely up and down stairs, you will need to use something more complex.
A collision map can be precalculated and loaded, or just as easily you can parse relevant meshes to make your map. Depending on the size, they are not massively complex to set up but of course should only be done at an init. I’ve pro‑ vided a means to pull the mesh data from OBJ files which I’ll talk about a bit later.

Render Culling!
It’s a pain that two different concepts share the same name but in one sense they are doing pretty much the same thing only for different reasons. You’ve already seen the massive benefits of sending all the data to the GPU and letting it do the drawing, It’s so much faster than CPU style drawing, but now that we have environments, you must have noticed we’re sending EVERYTHING to the GPU and letting the view systems decide what does and does not get drawn, but internally you should realise that every single vertex and associated fragments are being tested to see if they need to be drawn. But sending everything is really rather wasteful, we may be filling our GPU memory with hundreds, maybe even thousands of vertices that we’ll never see. We know our targets have quite limited GPU power and memory, they are not going to be able to keep up as our environments get bigger and bigger with more and more mod‑ els and more and more vertices being sent to the limited memory we have, even the biggest PC video cards have limits, we have much stricter limits, so so much stricter! So, we need to bring our CPU back into play again and allow it to make some general decisions about what it does and does not send to the GPU for processing. The concept of render culling is simple and rather obvious; we’ve done it already with our 2D systems and with our collision systems. Just as collision cull‑ ing prevents us doing collision checks we don’t need, render culling prevents us
trying to render models we can’t see.
There’s just no point sending a model with 800+ faces to the GPU if you know it’s never going to actually draw them. We also know those 800+ faces are contained in an object/model that occupies a certain amount of volume in our scene. It’s therefore quite easy for us to do a basic check on whether an object that contains those 800+faces is going to be visible. It’s worth wasting a little bit of CPU time to avoid wasting a lot more GPU time sending and processing those vertices when they are not going to be seen.
There are really two types of culling test, similar to the way we do collisions in 3D; we can do a broad‑phase and a narrow‑phase cull. Essentially, this breaks down into deciding on which models are sent to the GPU and for narrow phase, on which actual vertices get sent. Though on games the scale we are doing at the moment,3 narrow phase really isn’t worth the effort, and we ironically need a lot more GPU power to carry that off. So, we’ll stick to broad‑phase systems.

3  I’m really hoping by the end of this book you will start to push your boundaries and find ways to really optimise your rendering, but the scale of the projects here just won’t challenge you to do that.


Recognisable Environments	371
We don’t need to do anything too fancy, broad phase by definition is a rough check, unlike an internal GPU frustum cull which goes down to the triangle/ver‑ tex/pixel level, we can just make a rough check if our models/meshes are indeed inside or outside of the area our frustum is looking at. Even something as simple as testing if it’s behind us or not can eliminate a good number of our unseen models. A more effective but quick scan though a few hundred models in a simple game scene will probably reveal that as much as 80% of the models are not going to be drawn. That saving in time both for the CPU sending the vertices or the draw call and the GPU’s own saving of time in not drawing unseen models will make a marked difference to performance.
If we again consider what our models represent to us, we can reimagine this image as a guide to deciding what to draw and not draw… (Figure 8.17).


















Figure 8.17 The fustrum by Pim Bos.

We will look at our model’s position, and a rough encapsulation system like we did with collision, either a box or a sphere, and check to see if that capsule is totally outside the view frustum. If it is outside, there’s no need for us to send it to the GPU.
If it’s partially or wholly inside the view frustum, then we can send it! We can let the GPU handle the straddling models. This simple concept is slightly confused by the fact that our frustum is an odd shape, but it’s only an odd shape because of the projection matrix we used to create it, we can also use a matrix to twist our calculations back again.

Adding the Functionality
Add the Frustum.h and .cpp files to your project and add an instance to your Camera class. Ensure that when you update your camera you update the frustum as it needs to use the Camera’s matrices to get some of its data.
You could also incorporate most of these functions into your camera class itself, the choice is yours, I am only keeping it separate for clarity.
The frustum class works by calculating the six different faces or planes of the frustum and keeping a record of them.


372	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
After which if a model wants to test if it’s in the frustums view and needs to be drawn, it can test with a simple position point of its centre and radius of its encapsulation sphere, well I say simple, it’s maths again, but reasonably simple maths using the concept of an intersection with a line and a plane. Yeah… I know <jazz hands> Maths, there’s an equation N·P(r) + D=0. But I’m none the wiser, the code itself is easier to work out though and revolves around the basic idea that we can define the six planes that make up the sides of our frustum and with that decide if a ray, starting at the centre point of the model, and pointing in the direction of the plane, has intersected with that plane, and if so, is the dis‑ tance from the model centre, to the point of intersection less than the radius of a sphere or other test parameter.
I’ve given you a Sphere test as it’s the simplest but feel free to add Rectangles, Points or Convex Hulls if you think there is a need. When you add this to your model draw systems, you can check each model to see if it returns false on this test, if so, do not try to draw it!
That’s a lot of processing saved. You may even have a valid reason to not update it at all, depending on the game’s needs. Setting up and testing a frustum cull is not without some CPU cost, but if it stops you sending even a few dozen vertices to the GPU you will make a net saving on time, helping to keep your frame rate up.
Of course, no amount of culling can compensate for situations where your game design has every model in its camera view, in such situations frustum cull‑ ing will provide no benefit, and indeed even in a free‑roaming environment it can be hard to ensure you don’t overload your view frustum. That is something your AI may need to give some thought to, making the frustum test potentially part of your AI to prevent spawning in, or travelling into the frustum view when stressed.
To test all this out, add the Asteroid.h and Asteroid.cpp files to your Space Cadet project, and in the game init, create a good number of randomly placed asteroids.

for (int i = 0 ;i <  20; i++) {
Pos = glm::vec3(100‑Rand(200) , 10 ‑ Rand(20) , 100‑Rand(200) ); Asteroid* T = new Asteroid(&this‑>Handler, (char*)"../
Resources/Models/A2.obj", &this‑>MainModelManager); T‑>Scales = glm::vec3(MScale, MScale, MScale); T‑>SetPosition(Pos);
MyObjects.push_back(T); T‑>StoreGraphicClass(MyGraphics); MyGraphics‑>Init(T); T‑>programObject = lightsh; T‑>MyLightShader = T‑>programObject; T‑>TheGame = this;
}


Build and run, you should now be able to see a few of these new Asteroids not too far from your start point. Our space game was running at a reasonable number of frames depending on your target, since there were only four or five ships active at any one time. My Pi3 was doing 50+ FPS quite happily. But the addition of these rather large asteroids has caused the frame rate to plummet. Assuming you have added the Frustum class and correctly updated in the camera class, uncomment‑ ing the test at the start of the Asteroids Draw function and building again will see that frame rate go up again.



Recognisable Environments	373
Our 20 asteroids are still there spinning around doing nothing but looking pretty, but we’re just not drawing the ones we can’t see. This is a principle we can now add to any model we plan to draw, with the exception of the SkyBox, or a single mesh terrain, since they are (nearly always) going to be in our view, so test‑ ing them is worthless.
Why not try to add a bit more gameplay to your Space Cadet game by shoot‑ ing the Asteroids and giving them some collision properties so they can bounce off each other?


Physics, More Scary Maths Stuff? …More Long Winded Explanations!
All this talk of motion and collision means we are engaging in the world of phys‑ ics programming. So far, we’ve avoided using much clear physics in our demos, or have we? We have gravity, motion, collision and collision response, which as it just so happens are staple parts of creating some form of physical realism in our games and are the foundation of most physics’ libraries. But we’ve only scratched the surface and provided the most basic of motion and testing, with only a small degree of response. Sadly, like many of the big topics here, Physics is just too big and yes, a bit scary, to go into in a chapter or two, so I’ll point you to some of the basic things you need to consider if you want to have your games behave like the real thing.
Our main concern for things that are basically moving around in a world we create is to somehow emulate Newton’s three laws of motion, which are:

1.  Newton’s 1st law sometimes called the law of Inertia; an object at rest will remain at rest unless acted on by an unbalanced force. An object in motion continues in motion with the same speed and in the same direc‑ tion unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

2.  Newton’s 2nd law sometimes called the law of Acceleration; acceleration of an object is proportional to the force acting on the mass of that object.

3.  Newton’s 3rd law, this is the one everyone knows; <translatedfromLatin> To every action, there is always opposed an equal reaction: or the mutual actions of two bodies upon each other are always equal, and directed to contrary parts.

Or as most of us know it, every action has an opposite and equal reaction.
On a small level, emulating these laws is pretty simple, as long as we are not too picky about realism, we did this with our 2D gravity and 3D motion, but as the complexity of our world builds, the interactions between objects in that world start to grow and we know how dangerous that can be to our frame rates.
If you’re good at maths and physics you may well want to build up your own physics library or include basic systems in game, but if like me, you suck at maths or just want to save yourself a LOT of effort, there are many alterna‑ tives. One of the simplest for SBCs is to use an off the shelf physics library. Yes, it’s time for another dip into the public domain to avoid doing the hard work ourselves.




374	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Introducing Bullet Physics
There are quite a few different physics systems out there, and like maths libs, they are all well established and do what is expected of them, but unlike maths libs, they involve much more complex concepts and as such, coders have taken different approaches to the challenges that representing a Newtonian world pres‑ ents. These different approaches sometimes need extra hardware to function, or individual reserved cores, and even special types of instructions. They nearly all use some common approaches, but may alter precision, use slightly different methods and in some genuine cases use random values to decide in cases where values are too close to call. This means that there is a lot of variation in the results different physics engines can return. Claims of improved accuracy from different engine supporters are irrelevant, since almost all physics engines are approxi‑ mating a lot of things according to algorithms or heuristics that give a best fit for a test case, you are not writing test cases! For the most part though, they will all provide a result which on the surface at least appears to be realistic enough for us. So long as that apple falls from the tree, we should be ok.
Given that physics is always going to require a heavy amount of processing, we need to limit our search to a light but effective physics system, and one that is available off the shelf for free. I chose the Bullet Physics Library as it gives a good balance of performance over result and it’s 100% free. It’s installed on your target using

sudo apt  install libbullet‑dev4

That will take a few minutes but once done you will have installed the header files and library files you need to include and run Bullet. You then need to add the four core libraries to our list of library names, BulletCollision, BulletSoftBody, BulletDynamics and LinearMath in our external library list! These are .so libraries, so you will have to be careful if you distribute your project, to ensure the user installs this also, or that you also supply them in your distribution. Also remember to add to your list of include directories.

/usr/include/bullet

At the time of writing this install gives us version 3.24 on Pi Bullseye OS. There is a 3.25 version available, but you may have to build it from source and I’m not sure we really need to worry about that, the later 3.xx versions don’t contain any significant differences, and we’re not going that deep into them. So, stick with the current installable version for now and update when you feel a bit more confident and only if you feel there is a new feature/fix you need.
You may also consider downloading the PC version 3.25, or later, from the PyBullet.org website and running its demos on your PC, to aid your understand‑ ing. There’s a basic explanation on how to download and install on PC on the support site for you to get going.
When I last tried it, almost all the demos in there will run, but a few are not so friendly. It’s still a confusing mess the first time you try to use it, but once you load and retarget your solution to your current SDK, set App_BulletExampleBrowser as your start‑up project and run that, you can see what it does. It isn’t the most intuitive of systems, but it does help to visualise what each aspect of the demos are doing, and can provide you a lightbulb moment, as it has for me on a few

4  We’ve said it often enough, but setup.sh already installed this for you on Pi


Recognisable Environments	375
occasions. PyBullet.org also has a forum for noobs like us to ask silly questions which mostly get ignored, but it’s been active for so long that the answers to most questions are usually buried in there somewhere.
Despite differences in methods and terminology, most of the principles of physics engines are pretty much standard, so we should be able to dip our toe in the water with Bullet and get the things we want to work. Even if not always the best way, as with our coding, we can examine those better ways once we have something up and running and ready to optimise.

How It Works, and Finally Quaternions
Bullet works by creating a physics‑based world, populated with objects that rep‑ resent your game objects and providing a set of base classes which you can attach to your own objects and keep track of their position and orientation so that you can render them. And in doing so it provides physical property concepts of veloc‑ ity, mass, orientation, shape and other real‑world properties you can expect a 300m long spacecraft to have, as well as a 5¢2² overweight but surprisingly ath‑ letic plumber. Once these representative physical objects are set up, Bullet then updates their motion and forces acting on or against them and keeps track of all the positional and orientation information in its own class members, allowing you to use them in your own position and rendering systems.
In the simplest terms, Bullet has all these objects in this unseen phys‑ ics world and then works out what happens to them when they all get thrown up into the air bouncing off each other and then landing and bouncing on the ground. Rotational effects, friction, material properties, momentum retention and absorption as well as many other things are taken care of for us.
In order for this magic to work, we must allow Bullet to create and maintain this world which will mainly contain mathematical concepts of our terrain and of any moving or static objects we deem important enough to be part of that dynamic world. The objects are added to the world, given some physical proper‑ ties, and a suitable “solver” function is then called which then does the work to move them around. With a few optimisations added to the mix for eficiency and speed, such as a choice of method for a broad‑phase collision to reduce unwanted tests.
We can have quite some variety of basic object shapes which are based on our previous ideas of primitive collision types, and of course every object can have its own physical properties, and different solvers can be used for different kinds of motion and interaction.
By using Bullet methods to implement motion, we effectively can let it take care of all the movement, collision, response and reactions we should expect our objects to have. That lets us focus more on logic and decision‑making. Bullet will create events we can detect and objects we can test, when something important happens, like a collision, allowing us to make the right kind of sound, or destruc‑ tion of a relevant object, and then Bullet can be asked to continue to work on the motion of the interacting objects according to standard rules of how we think the universe should work.
We should bear in mind though it’s not exact, physics in the real universe works on a quantum level, everything checked and tested at all times. Computers work on a sequential, one at a time, at a given point in time, level. But it does work quite effectively, at the cost of quite a lot of processing!
We’ll include Bullet in most of our projects from now, to demonstrate the use, but going into a lot of detail about what it can do, how to set it up and so on,


376	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
will eat up pages, and there are some reasonable tutorials and a knowledgeable if slow to respond, online community available at the Bullet website http:/bul‑ letphysics.org/wordpress/. I’ll let the source code of the demos explain things in more detail, what and how things are being set up and why.
