How do we deal with a transparent polygon? What if we have a half‑height wall and can see over it, or maybe wooden oak beams where we can see over and under but not through?
Render systems can be optimised by quite massive margins by working out only the polygons you want to draw and avoiding sending things behind things. We’ve touched on that with our partition tree storage methods, but when using standard models and meshes, we need a more viable real‑time method. But, and it’s a big but, there’s a cost to that, in a scene with many 10’s of thousands of polygons but only 100 or so to draw, we still have to check all of them to work out which 100 we want. Balance as always between the times it takes against the gain we make is vital.
The simplest solution is to use data systems like partition trees, if that’s not possible try to maintain manageable‑sized meshes that are not going to have end‑ less numbers of unseen polys. Accept that a certain % of them will be unseen, even quite a high %, and do your best to ensure that unseen dynamic objects are not drawn if not seen. Frustum culls will remove the majority of your dynamic objects, those still in “view” can be tested with simple ray casts from their edges to your viewpoint should allow you to tell if you have a wall or other obscuring objects in the way.
Keep the sections of your world modular, and switch between them, as we did with the race tiles. Remember all culling is about doing a small test to get a big gain, try to do that at all times.
Sadly, I just don’t have a big enough environment model to really get you to work on improved rendering systems using occlusion‑based rendering, and if I did, I probably would make it into a BSP (Binary Space Partition) or similar system. So, you’re really not likely to get to a point where such fun but technical things as occlusion‑based rendering are needed. Perhaps that’s wise since you should not really have such large scenes in a machine with this level of power. But we’ll do our best to test and occlude our dynamic objects and keep our FPS up to the standard needed.
Let’s get on to using a map where we can practice a little of our occlusion concepts on a model rather than per poly basis (Figure 9.2).








408	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming




































Figure 9.2 Clambering around by Pim Bos.


Exploring the Maze
Ok, it’s not the most exciting game concept ever, but I have limited resources, and I just want to show you stuff that you can go away and do better. We’re going to move into the first person viewpoint now, with occasional 3rd and God modes, and explore an environment that has a lot of walls. I could also have ceilings, but I wanted to keep a god view to let you change your camera view to visualise the play area. This will help us to see how our characters are moving around and making good or bad choices.





Let’s Go Indoors	409
Download and compile the demo, 3DMazeHunt. It’s more complete than our other projects because we really don’t need too much handholding now. We will add some extra features though. Our primary interest here is discussing what we can do to make our render and movement systems better and for our AI, such as it is, to be more aware of how to behave when there are obstacles in the way. Clearly, the model works, we can move around, and our frame rate is fine, even with God camera mode forcing our GPU to draw all the triangles of the mesh we can do it pretty well. By modern game standards, it’s really quite a low poly maze, but for the Pi4 it might prove a bit of a challenge. I grabbed some screenshots from the FBX build supplied, but I’ve converted them to OBJ (Figure 9.3).























Figure 9.3  A better environment.

We can also view it as a mesh, since we’re going to really focus on the mesh for collision and other purposes. It’s important not to get too hung up on how something looks but consider how it’s made up. This is a capture from the Autodesk FBX viewer and it’s quite a useful viewer to have but it can’t provide a lot of information though does provide a lot of view options. I much prefer to use Assimp’s model viewer and exporter which was able to load the same FBX but reported a lot less polys (Figures 9.4 and 9.5).











410	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming





















Figure 9.4 Just a big mesh.
























Figure 9.5 Breaking down the details.

And as an OBJ, viewed on Assimp’s model viewer/exporter, we might notice different viewers and types report different values for polygons/faces and a lot less vertices. It can be hard to know which. But generally, the OBJ version is


Let’s Go Indoors	411
accurate as the FBX version discounts repeated images due to its inbuilt ability to store instance content. Something we should consider when deciding on a final model format. I will use OBJ this time, just because it’s easier to work with here and explain when we create physics meshes. But do consider the benefits of less polys and faster renders (Figure 9.6).






















Figure 9.6  Compare and evaluate.

Thanks to my Breda University of Applied Sciences colleague and old friend from our Manchester days, Martin Walker, who quickly put this together from some free assets we downloaded from Sketchfab.com. The theme is from a famous console game, which we use here for educational purposes only. In parts of his map, there are a lot of walls, and when we are in the enclosed spaces, we know there are going to be characters behind the walls in front of us, so it’s a good example to use for attempting some first generation sneaking around, using rays to detect walls and practising occlusion with frustum and Line of Sight occlusion ideas.
This would be wise to investigate, after all the total level is over 80K of polys, probably 2–3 times that in vertices, so even a Pi4 is going to have a little trouble rendering this at times. I had a much simpler model for the previous edition, but we needed something with more meat on its bones this time.
As an aside, Martin later produced an optimised version of the level map for me, it’s on the support site, try using it after using the quick basic one and see what a difference it makes to your overall frame rate. Proof that a good artist given a bit more time can save a programmer a lot of headaches.
Getting back to the demo code, take a moment to notice the way I move the player and by extension other objects around. I am not using forces or impulses and indeed I’m overriding most of the main motion systems by resetting angular velocities to 0. Why would I do that?
It’s to do with the nature and expectation of the game, and some conve‑ nience. It’s quite viable to move players and objects around in this 3D maze by


412	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
supplying force‑based movement, and if we balance everything well it will work nicely and feel very realistic, but we’re not trying to emulate a real‑world environ‑ ment, there’s nothing remotely real world about a first‑person shooter and that balancing can take quite some time to get right.
We need more direct control of our motion, so rather than apply torque or direction impulses, I am adding±angles to a model’s orientation to change it. I’m also applying forward linear speed, not force, to move him forward and back. That gives me a more immediate sense of movement and control in a game like this while still allowing friction to stop me and collision detection to prevent me moving through walls, though not, due to the override, providing any nice bounce‑off the walls. It’s a small price to pay, or I could even create my own bounce‑off using a collision response system.
Bullet still provides us with gravity, because that’s useful for controlling jumps and falls, the collision of course is still very useful, and even without force‑based movement it gives us speed/movement and orientation control. Other benefits include the rather nice btRaycast systems we can use for shooting detection and Line of Sight tests. These features really do help us and keep the use of the processor‑expensive physics engine relevant and useful to us. We can still have normal physics‑based crates and things in the game if we find a need. But players and enemies need simpler motion systems.
I’ve also up a nice camera that can switch from FPS to Third Person (a short distance behind), which will replace the overhead god camera and create a Game init loop to instantiate all the objects you will find in the Enemies.h file. It’s a simple array structure so you should be able to follow it ok.
There are two very simple enemy classes, for a PatrolBaddie and a StaticBaddie, they do very little at the moment beyond animate and acknowledge when you are in their sphere of interest. You can add more content, or better yet create new classes derived from these two basic types. Feel free to make them do some patrol‑ ling, there are some coordinates available for you in the Enemies.h file.
Why not add a SkyBox? After all there’s no ceiling, and then maybe a McGufin of some kind you have to pick up and run back to a base point with? No? Oh well I’ll do that on the support site.
Ok good, so this works, we can wander around, we can attack and be attacked. It’s all working fine. But we might be finding our frame rate* is drop‑ ping quite alarmingly due to the number of baddies being processed. In fact, it’s not the processing that’s causing us hassle but the drawing, even with a frustum cull. There are a couple of reasons for that, to start with our level is one large mesh, and as such we will always try to draw it all, but only see the vertices that are in our view, and we have quite some number of reasonably detailed characters to draw that we don’t always see, a good example of occlusion issues, but we’ll talk about that soon, first let’s get these characters doing their things.

Making a Level Physical
Whenever we load large levels or maps, we need to make it physical, since unlike an outdoor ground we have far more than simple undulations and hills to con‑ tend with. We may indeed have stairs and multiple levels of walkway, roofs and doors. Look at this complex Sketchfab level we can download for free (Figure 9.7).


* This really will be less obvious on a Raspberry 4/5, but was very apparent on the Raspberry 3, but adding more enemies to the scene will indeed start to create some frame drops to experiment with.


Let’s Go Indoors	413


















Figure 9.7  Go large.

70K+ vertices, ok that’s a lot, but it’s manageable, if we are careful when we render. But there are at least two main floors here and a couple of mezzanines. We can’t realis‑ tically place ground planes in there and still have all the crates, and other details there.
And when we look at it with textures enabled you can see some of that detail, with walls, stairs and even some static obstacles, we must not only render these but interact with them in some way, perhaps to make the doors open when we get to them or have windows we can see through but retain their solid status.
But we must be careful not to be overwhelmed by the details. It is after all just a group of meshes and we can indeed load those meshes into Bullet (Figure 9.8).




















Figure 9.8  Looking clean.


414	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Lucky for us the systems we used in our previous race game work just as well for complex levels like this as they did for tiles, we just need to make sure all the meshes we care about, which for a level is usually all of them, get turned into a btBvhTriangleMeshShape, and we can then move around inside it just as easily as we can move on top of our tiles… It’s a big shape, but we can handle it, as seen in this example with some suitable sneaky moody lighting changes, and debug lines enabled, from one of my first year students, Sebastian Himmer (Figure 9.9).






















Figure 9.9 Debug view.


This is a lot of polys, being kept in Bullets Dynamic World, but it does keep that data in a very eficient and effective data structure. Even so, there are limits and it would be wise to find ways to keep the data size and processing down to a minimum.
If we were really clever, we could even get our tame artist to break it up into sections for us, so that we only need to have one or two physical meshes in place to represent one area and swap it in and out as needed.

Moving the Baddies Around, Things!
In some ways, moving isn’t so different from the 2D versions we had already mastered. In a level‑based game where you are on open ground, you simply need to work out where the X and Z point you want to go to are and home in on them the same way we did with the 2D sprite based games. Using normalised vectors to step to it, or to provide a direction of force or velocity to apply. Getting from A to B across a terrain is pretty simple, just ignore the extra dimension!
Sadly, an indoor area isn’t so easy to get from A to B, there are walls, and corners, maybe stairs. These are not easy things to negotiate, in fact they can be a pain to even detect. Fortunately, Bullet takes care of detection for us, and in



Let’s Go Indoors	415
doing so gives us an easy way to determine if we have line of sight on the point we want to get to with its ray systems.
Our ability to move around, especially using AI, has become a little more complex, sure we can let the physics control our collision with these walls and stairs, but that’s not exactly going to impress our player when he sees the baddies pummelling themselves off the closest wall over and over again as they try to get to the spot on the other side.
What’s needed is a means to either limit their travel, the simple solution, by giving them pre‑defined bounding areas. Or if we do want them to move away from points, give them the logic to hunt you, or to move to another place.
Flow maps won’t work here, because flow maps assume a fixed direction and purpose of travel. If we want to set up patrols going around our area, they will work fine, but if we want to go from an arbitrary point A to arbitrary point B, due to a condition that gets triggered in game (for example, you trip an alarm), the best the flow map will do is similar to a travelator, get on and wait till your stop comes up. Too bad if you got on one stop after the point you want to go to, you’re going to go the long way around.
This is a complex problem, but we’ve done lots of simple solutions before, with good reason, so let’s try to also find simple solutions here. Basically, we need some kind of pathfinding. If an enemy decides to go hunt you, or travel, he needs to be able to move from point A to point B, regardless of walls, then maybe C, D and infinity and beyond.
So, Pathfinding…that’s a fun topic, one of the stalwarts of AI, and it’s a kind of catch all term for several methods of finding your way around. Whenever I ask my Dutch University students about pathfinding they usually respond. “A*”, maybe a few will know Dykstra, since he was Dutch. But while these are well‑established and useful systems, there’s more to it than that.
The basic idea of a pathfinder is interesting, essentially what you want to do is find the shortest way from A to B, negotiating around any walls, swamps, fire pits, dog poo and deep impassable trenches that are in the way. For most of us in the real world, that’s pretty easy, we use our primary sense of sight, we look around, take stock of the area, map it out in our brain and turn to the general direction we want to move to and head for it, cleverly and nimbly avoiding the fire pits and going around the swaps, leaping over the trenches though sometimes forgetting about the dog poo!
Easy right? Ok now do it blindfolded after being kidnapped, drugged, driven through the night and dropped into a strange house without even being offered a cup of tea to welcome you! Suddenly it’s not so easy, is it?
This is the problem our computer has to deal with, it has no vision, our code can only deduce it wants to go… thataway… in a direction suggested by some other factors in the code, and head thataway, through mountains of dog poop, swamps and fire pits, before mercifully lurching into a bottomless pit!
Well, if we can’t “see” our way around, what else is open to us? We have other senses and so does our computer, kinda!
The way our brain maps out a possible route from our start position is the key to making a pathfinder work. Whatever happens we’ll have a start point, and with sight we have the ability to see points ahead of us that should lead to where we want to go, but without it, we need to feel our way around, I’m deeply regret‑ ting the dog poo idea now.
But feeling around is as good a method as any for a computer. It just needs to know what to “feel” for. The method of building our brain map is far less


416	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
important than the fact we actually are producing a brain map. It really does not matter if we see, feel, smell, touch or taste…yeah let’s take the dog poo out; our way around, so long as we can build up some kind of mental map of our sur‑ roundings, and with that, work out how to get to where we want to go.
The points we are looking for represent different things, maybe restart points, a safe point from the start point to “here”, where we know we can feel ahead and not bump into anything. Every point that gets us closer to our goal becomes a new safe point to be remembered.
What these points are, is debatable and dependent on the game, but essen‑ tially, it’s a signpost. It can tell you either the location of the next safe signpost, or more usefully, the direction of the next signpost on the way to where you want to go to?
But of course there’s no way for the signpost to know where you want to go to…unless you have cleverly placed on in your map somehow, maybe before you started the game, or when the game fired up, part of its initialisation was to work out all the paths from one signpost to another and assume you pre‑loaded the signposts with info on where the next one to get to is located.
We use signposts all the time, go to any city centre you will see signposts directing you to where you want to go to, but consider that whoever put those signposts there had to know which way the signposts had to point.
The more proper term for these signposts is nodes. A node is an odd concept but it’s basically a data structure that maintains a link with other data structures, allowing you to traverse from one to the next. Nodes are a common feature in a lot of coding tools but especially for AI and pathfinding.
Once we have a signpost telling where us to go in “Thataway” direction to get to the next signpost, we can move “Thataway” and when we do get to the next signpost, it will then tell us to head ThatNewWay, to take us to the path we want to go to! We most likely will use a vec3 to indicate the points to travel to, but we probably are going to ignore the y component, even if our target is upstairs, all you need to have is a sign at the top and bottom of the stairs, pointing to each other, the movement systems will take care of the rest.
It’s a simple system, mainly because it relies on us to fill in the directions manually, using our good old Mark1 eyeball and brain. It would, however, be very cool if you could get the computer to fill it in on Game init, try doing a bit of research on Pathfinding methods to work out how to do this, or indeed put in a better system.
Our Signposts, though usually invisible, are really nothing more than a list of pointers to other nodes. They have a little bit of logic, which looks around the currently active objects to see who’s there. If it finds an enemy, it implants it’s address in the Enemies closest Signpost stored value. And the Enemy is then able to move to that signpost knowing that moving in that direction, once there it’s free to head to its target node. I’ve made the signposts in the 3DMazeHunt visible, so you can see the connection to them. But they have no physical properties so can’t be collided with. When you have a place to go to, from here to the signpost, you have a standard A to B system just like we had in 2D.
Standard A to B systems might not be effective though if for example there is a wall in the way, the signpost concept might well allow us to put a signpost at every possible junction but really it’s better if we try to put them at key points and allow our AI to have a little bit of simple logic.
That’s where our ray cast concepts come in useful, if we know we want to get from point A, to point B, we can have a look to see if there is a clear path, by


Let’s Go Indoors	417
simply casting a ray towards our target. If the path is clear, we move directly to it in a straight line, if not, if a wall is in the way for example, we can make decisions to move in the general direction and use rays ahead and to the side of us, to find corridors that will allow the player to move closer to a point where a free path is available. A scattering of signposts will provide ways to update our target direc‑ tion and that should allow us to move around reasonably well.
That will deal with the general direction of our motion, but we still need to try to avoid bumping into things. Lots of enemies rushing from point to point means lots of characters getting in our way and sometimes walls that need a swift change of direction. Of course, we need to be careful where we place the signs. They ideally need to create a clear line of sight matrix, so that you can be sure of moving along a free space from one to another, but as I said we don’t want to drown the map in signposts.
Avoiding bumping into other enemies is best done with other systems, I quite like using proximity boxes or antenna rays (see Figure 9.10), which test the areas immediately in front of us, and if it finds another moving character, both obey aviation law and move to the left, to avoid collisions. Or is it right? Actually, as long as they both do the same, it’s fine. Equally such systems can be used to “see” how close we are to a wall by checking to the relevant left and right, if they find a wall, or other obstacle, they either stop or deflect us away. All of which mimics how a fairly well‑ordered colony of ants might negotiate around a maze.
The screenshot in Figure 9.10, for an old Pi3 demo, shows the antenna ray in front looking for an object to avoid and the ray to the right test finding a wall to avoid. We could add any number of such antenna’s searching ahead and around to make decisions based on contact with things about to hit us but generally ahead and to the side will give us enough info on what our character can “see”.


















Figure 9.10  Feeling around.

Of course it’s not a perfect system, but actually that’s part of its joy, if enemies are running too fast, their change in direction is not going to be smooth/quick enough sometimes, and they will bump into walls and each other, just like you see in every blooper show on TV show with that Storm Trooper hitting his head!



418	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Review the MoveToPoint(x,y,z) code in the provided EnemyLogic class, you can now add to the project. This will give you methods to move your enemy characters around to move around and also detect your presence and respond to alerts in a simple way. As always feel free to add new behaviours.
You’ll find a “finished” but un‑optimised version of 3DMazeHunt on the support site, where I have made everything work for you, don’t cut and paste! Try to write your own versions, then compare with mine. If you must cut and paste, try to find some better ways to achieve what I did, there are several.

Trigger Points and Colliders
Signposts of any kind also need some kind of detection. One of the nice things about having a physics system is the collision concept; currently, we use it to do something when things bump into things, but we are not just limited to that. We can also do some very specific events or game requirements. Imagine Star Trekwar (careful of the copyright) doors, opening when your character gets close to the door itself, or a hidden enemy at the end of a corridor sets off to attack you when you enter the corridor. Yes, you can do these things with range and ray tests, but range and ray tests need to always be running in our main game code that can be clunky and cumbersome. Collisions with invisible collision objects, “Colliders”, are handled totally by Bullet and we can set and forget them.
You can also have colliders which don’t relate to any game models. They can just be areas or shapes that you place in the scene, and when your character moves into them. Their own collision response system will trigger your code, to make that door to open, or signal that monster (and a few of his friends) to come towards you.
A collider then is just a shape that has some physical properties for collision purposes, but isn’t really part of your game, isn’t usually rendered, and has code that only gets called when you detect it has collided with another object, which then causes an event to occur.
Bullet has a neat collision shape type called a btGhostObject, which we can set to any shape, use its user index values to indicate what it is or does and even set it to only collide with a certain group and mask of other physical objects, i.e. our player, here’s an abbreviated example;

btGhostObject* Collider =  new btGhostObject();
btSphereShape* Sphere =  new btSphereShape(0.2f); // can be any shape/size
Collider‑>setCollisionShape(Sphere); Collider‑>setUserIndex(3); //it’s a door trigger type 3 Collider‑>setUserIndex2(1); //door 1 or use the Userpointer to point to something
DynamicPhysicsWorld‑>addCollisionObject(Colliders, 1, 2);// player is mask 2 also

It’s a bit of a chore doing these one at a time, but we can probably extract such information we need from a scene, or map file to indicate where the doors are and place colliders, what matters is that this btGhostObject will exist and can therefore trigger a response in our collision callbacks.
GhostObjects can have a multitude of different uses, but they don’t have mass, so don’t move, this makes them super useful for static, low maintenance colliders. But, if you attach them to dynamic things you need to update them with the thing they are attached to, there are some cases that can be useful, like



Let’s Go Indoors	419
setting up a proximity area around a ship, and detecting a point of incursion without causing a full collision response.
Colliders play a big role in most game engines allowing a game’s storyline or events to progress and allowing new content to be developed in the game. From our viewpoint, they are free gives that Bullet gives us to use as we see fit.

What Do We Draw?
Accepting for now, that we have to draw the entire maze, since we don’t have a decent render method that would make it better (you can research a few though). We need to focus our efforts on the enemies and other details we’ve placed in the world.
Which is where we get to our model occlusion. Using Bullet every model has an AABB bounding volume we can access, using Bullets inbuilt function.

MyPhysObj‑>GetRigidBody()‑>getAabb(aabbMin, aabbMax);

This gives us two possible corner points which we can extrapolate to a full eight for the cube, we can use these to cast a btRay to each corner from our camera view. If any of these rays goes from the eye point to the corner without hitting a wall we can see it, or at least be very close to seeing it, as the AABB is often larger than the model. But if all eight are obscured by a wall, we can’t. Eight (though we could actually reduce that to four) simple tests could save us from sending a model to the GPU and the associated time is gained.
It is sometimes valid to allow a model to hide another model behind it, but usually our models do have a lot of empty space, which we can see through. Basic occlusion systems work on bounding volumes which regard that empty space as a valid area of occlusion, so it’s probably best to focus only on avoiding a draw on an object obscured by an environmental feature.
I’ve added an unoptimised occlusion test method to the ObjectModel class, it returns a simple bool for true, it’s visible, or false, it’s behind a wall, for you to try it out, it’s totally self‑contained, all you need to do is add it to your draw sys‑ tems, and if the draw is not needed abort the draw.
Your fun task now is to optimise that method and get it even faster and of course if you don’t want to use Bullet, you need to use a different ray cast and have a different way to access the bounding volume.
One small point I should make you aware of as, it caught me out when I was writing it. In an FPS, the Camera is located at the models head, and because our player character is himself a physical object, even if not rendered, he is also detectable by a ray‑cast system, and it is therefore entirely possible, that the ray test can return him as the resultant hit of a ray cast. This makes sense when you think about it, we are encased in a collision shell, and firing a ray from inside that shell we will always hit the inside of it. But because all collision tests are done as pairs, and dynamically sorted with some small elements of unpredictability, it may be that sometimes you get the wall and sometimes you get the player, such random returns can play havoc with your logic. So, take care when using ray casts from objects not to start the ray from inside the collision container. I add an offset slightly larger than the radius of the character multiplied by a normalised direction to avoid that. If your camera is not a physical object, you won’t need that normally or you must take care to ignore any ray cast result that returns your player.



420	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
What’s the Point?
Oh yeah, we don’t really have a game here, do we? Just a couple of things to dem‑ onstrate some points. Well, add a game! That’s what you’re reading this book for isn’t it?
I’ve made the baddies shoot at you if they see you, and you can kill them, if you installed the Signposts correctly then they can move around pretty indepen‑ dently to whichever position you are, and you can change your camera views to allow you to get an idea of what the enemies are doing. That’s a decent set of basic abilities, and of course you can add more.
Try making a stealth game where instead of shooting them automatically, you need to avoid being seen by patrolling guards. If you are spotted, the guard radios in and surrounding guards will stream to that location. If you can destroy the reporting guards before the reinforcements arrive, you have a chance to remain stealthy again, and the guards will return to their patrol paths.
Your ultimate aim is to simply capture a flag at the end of the level and make it out alive. You might even want to add an option of a hunt and destroy if you do manage to get the flag, forcing you to fight your way out.
You’ve got all the means you need to do this, I’ll post my version on the site, and you can compare. Do yours first though, no peeking!
I know I said previously I don’t have a big enough level, but since the first edition there has been a lot more content that has become available online and you could now indeed try a really big level, this one (https://skfb.ly/6tCxy) is especially nice, you can download and use it.
It will create a few challenges for you and even though the Pi4 is more than capable of displaying it, it offers many options to experiment with occlusions. Sketchpad also has a lot of cool soldiers/guards/lab techs and random people you can arrange to have walking around, so you could easily make a game out of them for free. There’s some effort in finding models, exporting them in a format you can use and loading them into Mixamo and then your project but…that’s why we keep tame artists in the cellar.
“Level5 Zone1 (Level Design 2002)” (https://skfb.ly/6tCxy) by hansolocambo is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution (http://creativecommons.org/ licenses/by/4.0/).

Ray Cast and Picking
From time to time in a game, we will need to use our mouse as a controller for our viewpoint or as a controller to access objects in our game, also perhaps to access icons on a 2D GUI.
How and when we switch the method of control is entirely up to the design of the game, but we will need to know how to use it in its different modes. Selecting 3D objects in our 3D world using a 2D pointer presents some fun problems.
Our mouse obviously works on a 2D screen, it only has X and Y coordinates, and the Z is probably going to remain at 0 or near that. But our objects are living in a 3D world and only appear to be 2D on our screen. To pick a 3D object, we need somehow to get our mouse to go inside the world and “pick” the object/item we are interested in.
Obviously, we can’t put our mouse into the screen, so we need somehow to work out what we are looking at with our eye and pointing at with our mouse. This gives us a clue!



Let’s Go Indoors	421
If we draw a line from our eye viewpoint to the pointer on screen, we are effectively creating a line, or as we more accurately now call it, a ray. A ray is a vector… and we know a few things about vectors. For one, they can be any length but still point in the same direction. Normally we’ve shrunk vectors to make them unit size. But what happens if we extend the line from our viewpoint to the mouse pointer, into the screen… we get something like this (Figure 9.11).

















Figure 9.11  Picking by Pim Bos

We get a line going from our eye to the mouse pointer into the screen. But how does that help us? Well the screen is effectively our near plane of our cam‑ era, and our eye, for all intents and purposes is the start of the view frustum, so we can trace our point of visual origin back to get a line from our eye, to the xy point and then using the projection of our camera send a ray off in that direction in space. Yes, it’s maths again…hold on, it’s not too bumpy! Once we have the ray, we know there are nice equations that we can use, to test if an object which we have surrounded with a sphere for collision purposes has been intersected by that ray.
So, if we do a quick scan through all our known objects in the scene, non‑ visible ones culled preferably. Do a picking ray test, and we can tell which objects the ray is interacting way. I say objects plural of course because it’s a ray and will keep going forever but we can use the far plane as a limit. It may intersect a couple of objects, so we’ll just focus on the closest to us on the valid assumption that we’re clicking on the one with the highest draw priority. Using one of the rather nice Ray→Shapetype systems available to us, we can tell if we have a hit. If we’re using Bullet‑based objects, they are already encased in their collision shapes, if not we should at least enclose them in spheres.
Lucky for us, we are using Bullet Physics, which we know has its own ray cast code, though it does not have a specific pick method, we have to supply our own. You should decide from the start if you are going to use a Bullet‑based ray‑ cast or write your own. Since I plan to use Bullet in all 3D demos in some form it’s probably best to demonstrate a Bullet version. We’ve already used a simple glm
ray cast for our Death Rays; the process is exactly the same. The process is simple though there are two clear steps.



422	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Step 1, create the directional Picking ray, this uses <jazz hands> Maths, but basically takes the x and y coordinates of our mouse, jiggles it about a bit with the camera view and projection and produces a ray as a vector3. This is where having access to the camera’s variable data finally becomes useful, and I hope you added access systems. For now, I will just grab them from the camera itself.

Step 2, cast the ray, using the internal Bullet systems, which returns the RigidBody and other info “IF” there’s a hit.

I won’t detail the maths, and the code is in the Rays class in the next RayPick demo for you to look at. All we need to know is we now have a very nice Pick function which if we have a mouse in use will tell us what we are clicking on and allow our code to take whatever action is needed for that.

Are We Going to Do Any AI?
Oh, Heyaall No!! AI is another of those massive topics where I’d have to write another 20‑chapter book on the subject. But I have given you a lot of basics in practical use, like point to point, homing, state management system, steer‑ ing systems, linked nodes, stealth, triggered and determined responses, and randomness as triggers and seeds, and a few other things that were specific to the games we produced.
There is also the fact that AI, now in 2025, broadly means something else in computing terms as we see more and more AI systems, using learning algorithms to improve performance in different fields. In fact, what we are doing here, isn’t AI, it’s AB, Artificial Behaviour, usually of NPCs in a game, so please consider that when in this book I say AI, I really mean the Artificial Behaviour of things.
AI/AB is such a big subject because it tries very hard to be generic, which is probably impossible. Every game has its own little needs, and while we can recog‑ nise some of the similarities, we need to tweak things to suit the situation we are in. All the games we’ve done feature some elements of “AI/AB” and you are free to expand or replace those elements.
A great many AI/AB concepts are also available in various forms as libs or as simple cut and paste solutions to issues, A* Pathfinding is a great example of a heuristic solution which people have produced multiple very impressive ver‑ sions, which will work better in some circumstances than in others, but there are significant issues over how you define the nodes it needs to operate.
If you really want to get into AI/AB, and I suggest you do, my old friend Mat Buckland wrote a couple of the best introductions to AI/AB when it was still called just AI, you can find, and he needed 500 pages or so to do it. Check out his books if you can and get some of the basics of AI/AB in place and move on to more modern behaviour and perhaps actual AI/Machine Learning systems when you have made a few games and become unhappy with the NPC’s behaviour.










Let’s Go Indoors	423


10


Graphics Need a Boost















That Cat’s Been in the Bag Long Enough
We’ve pretty much got basic concepts of different types of game up and running, but our graphics are still a little on the light side; ideally, we want to delve deeper into the technical side of graphics programming, but that’s a lot of maths to get your head around, and again, a 20‑chapter book, most of which isn’t really going to be a lot of use on our itty bitty GPUs. But one big graphic boost you can add and probably get away with is shadows. I hope you remember we put them in a bag for later, well now it’s time! (Figure 10.1.)

















Figure 10.1 Cast a giant shadow by Pim Bos.


424	DOI: 10.1201/9781003527695-11
I mentioned before about shadows and their reliance on shaders which we were just beginning with, so it was a bit of a complex topic to get our heads around at the start of our shader journey, but we should now be comfortable enough with shaders to start doing some complex stuff. We now have suitable models and terrains which will be able to demonstrate them properly, so now is the right time for shadows, this topic is a bit technical though, despite being a fairly simple premise, shadows, used to be hard on OpenGLES 2.0, but are fairly easy on OpenGLES 3.x. The method we’ll use here is simple as long as our play area isn’t too huge, and it’s a kick‑off point to other options that you can tinker with when you are comfortable.
Before we start though, you need to know why I’ve left it this long to discuss, it’s simple, shadows will kill your frame rate, because we’re going to have to run two passes of our graphics through the GPU, at least using this method, though some refinements are now possible, but shadows are a computationally expensive process on even the best GPUs, our little GPUs might be able to run the code, but you have to consider this feature only when you really need it and not in a game with dozens of shadow‑casting objects. You can develop tricks and methods to improve performance, and that’s definitely something you need to do, but any form of shadow other than a pre‑baked built‑in model shadow is going to kill performance.
All that said, let’s try out the most useful system and get it working, the Raspberry 3B struggled a bit with this, but it did manage it, so later Pis and of course PCs will be more than capable.

Frame Buffer Objects
Before we get started with shadows, we need to understand a basic concept, which for the most part has been hiding from us, Frame Buffer Objects (FBOs). These are simply the graphical buffers we get our shaders to output our data to. All frame buffers have one Render Buffer Object of some kind, usually for display, as a colour RGBA buffer, but they also tend to have depth buffer, maybe even a sten‑ cil buffer, so that internally it can tell whether to draw a pixel that is closer to the view point than the previously drawn one at that location and is not masked out by a stencil, in the image buffer. Depth and stencil are usually combined though. The depth/stencil map is optional, but doing 3D without it wouldn’t make sense. So, for drawing purposes, we usually set up a colour buffer, called GL_ COLOR_ATTACHMENT0and a depth buffer GL_DEPTH_ATTACHMENT, when an FBO is created. Now these only describe the type of content, but not the
actual make up or data format of them.
Now, surprisingly we have been working with an FBO. It’s our standard default display output which we swap out to the screen for display when we have finished updating. So generally, we don’t give it a lot of thought. It was set up when we initialised OpenGL and EGL.
OpenGLES 2.0 was pretty happy with that; it only actually allowed us to write to the one colour buffer. That changes with OpenGLES 3.x though as it allows us to define multiple render targets (MRTs) and the reason we have GL_ COLOR_ATTACHMENT0 makes a bit more sense because we can have GL_ COLOR_ATTACHMENT1 and GL_COLOR_ATTACHMENT2 and up to our max (usually a total of eight buffers). This could now start to be useful.
Now back to that type of data. Our shaders only write out to a graphic format, which is defined as one of the texture formats, usually something like GL_RGBA


Graphics Need a Boost	425
there’s actually only a couple of types in GLES2.0, given that its intended purpose is only to produce screen visible data that’s understandable. But we are allowed to create our own FBOs and rather than render to the screen we can choose another render object, so we have a bit of control over the format of the data we render. And now… let’s talk about how we can use this feature to get some cool shadows.

Shadow Mapping
The usual first step in our journey into shadow mapping is a well‑established method of gathering the relevant data in a height map and then using that height map in lighting calculations for the actual render. Simple shadow mapping there‑ fore consists of two passes, Gathering and rendering.
I think I’m right in saying Simple Shadow Mapping (let’s call it, SSM, my own three letter acronym) is still one of the best current methods used for cre‑ ating simple dynamic shadows. They have been around a few years now and work pretty well. But like all shadow systems, they do come at a bit of a cost, as they are a result of multiple passes through our GPU to set up that frame buffer which will “render” a scene from the lights point of view rather than the camera. Rather than render pixels it will store depths from the point where the light is placed to the point of the pixel being lit. The resulting frame buffer will contain depth information on the pixels the “light positioned camera” draws, but only as distance from the light source.
Think of it as a light directly overhead a model of a character holding an umbrella we want to render, the light is shining down on the umbrella which clearly gets lit but stops the light at that point, everything under the umbrella will be in shade and lit only by the ambient light in the scene. So that means we have a concept of height, where the umbrella is! Anything we draw under that level is darker, i.e. unlit. Changing the angle of the light does not make a massive difference, remember matrices allow us to twist and turn points and directions in space, which is all a light is from our viewpoint, the distance the light travels until it hits an obstacle, can be comfortably worked out and then used to decide if pixels on the same line of light are in front of or behind the shape absorbing the light. Rain and umbrella’s work the same way really (Figure 10.2).

















Figure 10.2 Keep dry by Pim Bos.



426	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
That in effect means we have access to a concept of things that the light hits and how deep into the draw plane it was hit…which we can twist around into a value of above and below things that light is going to hit. Things above the barrier can be drawn as lit normally, things below can be in shade or totally dark as we decide.
Okay, that sounds simple, doing the calculations might be trivial but there are a lot of them and they need to be easy to access.
This is where we need the power of our GPU and its shaders. OpenGLES has so far been perfectly happy calculating and drawing our RGBA pixels into a big render target buffer which then becomes the screen, technically that’s our Colour Buffer, which we know now is part of our default FBO.
Now, we should be comfortable with understanding that our shaders update our colour buffers using the data we send out in a shader. But those colour vec4’s are just numbers we calculated, supposing we do calculations other than colour? For example…drum roll please. A height buffer! But, cut that drum for a minute, on OpenGLES 2.0, we can’t make a pure numerical height buffer, only a colour buffer of types such as RGBA, but we can pack RGBA with any numbers… so we can encode height into a vec4 intended as RGBA.
Yup, OpenGLES 2.0 onward already gives the tools we need to do shadows, but we still need to get the shaders up and running and we (usually) must do two draw calls to our GPU, one to create the height buffer, stored in our colour buffer, which we will now call a shadow map, and one to create the actual image which uses that shadow map.
This is where understanding our FBO becomes useful because while the default FBO quite rightly outputs to a screen buffer, we can create our own FBO which outputs its data to…..do we still have that drumroll…? A texture. And… once the render to the shader is done that texture can be used like any other texture in a shader. But this time rather than being colour data, it’s useful height information for the pixel we are working on, which tells us if it’s above or below a point of light occlusion. We call this a gather pass, since the main loop isn’t trying to render any objects but build a data structure in a texture that we use later in the render with shadows pass.
This is a form of procedural generation, we effectively create a brand‑new shadow texture every game loop, and the texture that is created is used as part of the loops draw system.
Let’s set things up, creating a simple shadow buffer/texture looks like this, we make an FBO, generate (or better still reuse one we made earlier) a special texture, just the same way we do already for model textures.

GLuint Graphics::CreateShadowBuffer(int width, int height) {

GLuint FBO, RenderBuffer; glActiveTexture(GL_TEXTURE1);  // use an unused texture

// make a texture the size of the screen glGenTextures(1, &ShadowTexture);
glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, ShadowTexture); // it’s bound to texture 1

glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0, GL_RGBA, width, height, 0, GL_RGBA, GL_UNSIGNED_BYTE, NULL); // was RGBA, probably does not need the A might save time/space



Graphics Need a Boost	427
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_S, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE);
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_T, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE);
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER, GL_LINEAR);
glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MAG_FILTER, GL_LINEAR);
// once the texture is set up, we can turn to the Framebuffer

glGenFramebuffers(1, &FBO); // allocate a FrameBuffer glGenRenderbuffers(1, &RenderBuffer); // allocate a render
buffer
glBindRenderbuffer(GL_RENDERBUFFER, RenderBuffer); // bind it glRenderbufferStorage(GL_RENDERBUFFER, GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT16,
width,height); //give some parameters for it glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, ShadowTexture); // bind the texture glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, FBO); // and the frame buffer

glFramebufferRenderbuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, GL_DEPTH_ ATTACHMENT, GL_RENDERBUFFER, RenderBuffer); // they are now attached
glFramebufferTexture2D(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, GL_COLOR_ATTACHMENT0, GL_TEXTURE_2D, ShadowTexture, 0); // and so is the texture that will be created

if (glCheckFramebufferStatus(GL_FRAMEBUFFER) != GL_FRAMEBUFFER_COMPLETE)
printf(“oh bugger framebuffer not properly set up”); glActiveTexture(GL_TEXTURE0); // careful to not damage this return FBO;
}

Our  shader  only  needs  to  ensure  it  outputs  the  gl_Position  relative  to  a LightViewProjection matrix, it gets pass to an out;

void main() {
gl_Position = u_lightMVP * a_position;
v_Coords = gl_Position; // pass this to the fragment which can’t access gl_Position
}

Our Fragment Shader can then pack the data into one or more of the RGBA floats too great a texture that contains height information rather than colour.

void main() {
float value = 10.0 ‑ v_texCoords.z; // 10 is the most usual range
float num = floor(value);
float f = value ‑ num;   // get the floating point value float vn = num * 0.1;
DepthTexture = vec4(vn, f, 0.0, 1.0); }

Small note: I was told it was also possible to create a texture using only the depth buffer generated by the Vertex Shader, as a texture and not have a Fragment Shader, but I was unable to achieve this on a Pi, so I simply reverted to my old tried and tested and clearly working Fragment Shader pack/unpack method… I may explore this on my support site. If I do get it to work with only a depth attachment it would represent a speed increase.



428	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
This is all rather neat, and it allows us to set up an FBO which only gets used for the gather pass, and we revert to the normal render using this captured depth texture as shadow texture.
Our gather and render pass might look like this:

glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, ShadowFBO); // set our shadow buffer
glViewport(0, 0, ShadowBufferSize, ShadowBufferSize); // set to buffer size
glClear(GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT); // clear the shadow buffer, there is no colour

// run through our objects, and if they have shadow draws let’s call them, don’t worry too much about objects with no shadow, they will simply return

for (int i = 0; i < MyObjects.size(); i++) {
MyObjects[i]‑>DrawShadow(); // most objects will do a dummy call

}  // when this ends our texture has height data for any shadow casters

// reset back to screen values
// release the shadow framebuffer
// reset the viewport to screen size glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0); // render to the
screen FBO(0) again
glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT); // clear main screen
glViewport(0, 0, ScreenWidth, ScreenHeight);

// go draw things

for (int i = 0; i < MyObjects.size(); i++) {
MyObjects[i]‑>Update(); MyObjects[i]‑>Draw();
}

Simple enough? As always, a full look at the code will make this clear and run‑ ning the demos will show it all working, and perhaps highlight a few weaknesses of this system.

And We Can Do More Now?
OpenGLES 3.x can of course also do this two‑pass system, but it can do more because it has MRTs, we’re no longer limited to having only one usable RBO attached to our FBO, though we still can only have one true depth/stencil buffer. We can now have several colour buffers and one of those can be configured as

glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0, GL_R32F, width, height, 0, GL_RED, GL_FLOAT, NULL);

It should be noted that GL_R32F is an extension format, but quite common; how‑ ever, if you can’t get it on your single‑board computer (SBC), you may have to downgrade to GL_R8 or pack your content into a float vector.




Graphics Need a Boost	429
Now we have a texture available that can be written by the Fragment Shader with data we pass it from the Vertex Shader. And we can load the texture when we do the next pass. gl_Position is still the data we want, but we can’t use the depth buffer anymore, so we send a copy of it, then proceed as normal to work out our position depending on the render we want.

// Vertex shader snippit #version 310 es
in vec4 position;
uniform mat4 lightViewProjection; // Pass depth to fragment shader out float v_depth;

void main() {
vec4 lightSpacePos = lightViewProjection * position; v_depth = lightSpacePos.z / lightSpacePos.w;
// do whatever normal vertex shader stuff you need. }

// Fragment shader snippet. #version 310 es
precision highp float;
in float v_depth;  // this was the gl_position we lost layout(location = 0) out vec4 outColor; // normal colour RBO0
layout(location = 1) out float outDepth; // 2nd RBO for depth in gl_R

void main()
{ // Convert normalized depth to uint
outDepth = v_depth ; // as long as it was GL_R32F we write outColor = vec4(1.0); // Your normal colour output goes here }

This allows us the possibility to create a shadow texture as we are rendering, while at the same time, rendering our model. I call this a laggy shadow, since it’s always going to use a texture from the last created shadow map. You do need to keep two shadow textures, and alternate between them, after all you can’t really use the one you are currently rendering so it’s back to double buffering again. But it can give you a bit of a performance boost due to only one pass, if you are able to handle the very slight lag on the shadow… if not… Go back to the simple two‑pass system.

Are You Feeling Lucky Punk?
There is also an option, sometimes, on some versions of OpenGL and GLES3.x to not use a second FBO and instead attach a new different Depth Buffer as a render object to your default FBO. That way you can always run just the one combined gather and render and use the depth buffer you generated last time as your texture for depths.
Now… this does seem to work, at least on my Pi and Mini PC, but… it’s not always guaranteed to work on every single SBC out there or even on updates to the drivers, it may or may not even be possible to delete the default attach‑ ments, it very much depends on the OpenGLES 3.x drivers. So, while this may be a tempting option, now that we are getting our head around FBOs it’s generally not advised to tamper with your default buffers, even if your code seems to allow it. Best to be safe.



430	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
The Rest of the Pack
There are other shadow methods, which need a lot more technical and maths skills than I can cover here, but this SSM using either gather method is a good starting point…
Different shadow systems may be more appropriate in different settings, especially for larger view systems. Perhaps, you can consider Cascade Shadow Maps, where you generate more than one shadow map for the view area in your scene. This is an expansion of the SSM system, allowing you to cast shadows over a larger area with less distortion.
For small scenes, a method known as Screen Space Contact Shadows mixed with a bit of SSM can give great results.
Another example, common in more modern games, which I tend to avoid though, is Distance Field Shadows. These can look great but have a high mem‑ ory overhead and require a lot of pre‑computation to work well. I’m not so sure smaller SBCs which are our main target here would be able to make them work well. Which is my coded way of saying, I’ve never tried it on a Raspberry, but I might do a demo on the support site.
There really are a lot of options, time to get your Google fingers working, but there’s a common theme, they are all FPS eaters, some much more than others, so you have to try it and see, balance the cost of speed and memory against the result. That’s another game programmer’s skill you need to add to the tool bag.
One more thing to Google is deferred rendering, which makes extensive use of MRTs to pre‑calculate and then use data that can produce a final complex ren‑ der scene much faster than single forward shaders. There are some great tutorials online that can really allow you to do a lot with a math‑intensive scene, but it’s really hard to get any kind of performance on a Pi, even an overlocked Pi5, so it’s not something I will go into here. But may cover it in my forums if there’s some interest, or the Pi6 has a big power boost on its shaders.

Deeper Dives
FBOs, using textures as data sources, and MRTs also open us up to some won‑ derful concepts in render processes, such as Deferred Rendering, Physically Based Rendering (PBR), Bump Mapping, etc., but we’re stuck with one unavoidable fact. The Pi (all versions), which is our target system, is painfully, almost irredeemably slow at accessing memory from a shader. So, while we can indeed load a pixel from a texture, and save a pixel to a texture, the overall time cost of doing so is absolutely incredible. That puts us in a situation here where any demos I produce will ulti‑ mately look great but will be painfully slow. We could do them on PC, but I wanted this book to target the limitations of systems and exceeding them beyond known boundaries is not something I think we can cover here. So, I will ofload this to some online resources and allow readers of this book to contribute to such ideas. There are great articles online that cover PBR and Deferred Rendering, and we do a bit of multi‑texture mapping for our models, but that’s as much as I want to do here.

Recap the Processes
A lot of good stuff has now been covered; we can roam around free space. We can fly and crash under the influence of forces and ram our enemies. We can drive cars and let them spin off and roll depending on their mass and how bad our driving is, hitting mountains of cardboard boxes with abandon.


Graphics Need a Boost	431
We also can roam around a plane which we can generate ourselves, and for good measures we can take shelter and explore in any nearby buildings we happen to find in our worlds.
We can let our objects that we’ve given some intelligence, find their way from point A to B and then C, as well as provide simple signposting systems for path or track‑based motion.
We can view almost any real‑world environment we are able to model and properly avoid drawing things we don’t need to draw allowing our GPU to focus on its task of putting our nice visuals on screen.
As with our 2D recap, we have come a long way, but we’re not Jedi’s yet.
All we have done so far is make that scratch on the surface of what is possible a tiny bit deeper, there’s a hundred different ways to do most of these things, and more than one flame war has been started on which is the correct way. But hopefully by now you are also starting to see some ways to make things work better.
How effective a method is, is going to depend a great deal on what kind of project you are writing. Some methods will work brilliantly in some games, much more than others. Always remember the lesson we learned with the 2D double buffer. Yes, it worked, and worked well, but it was slow, it was ungainly, it had limits when drawing transparency and it was CPU heavy. You need to con‑ sider what the best option is every time you write code.
But one thing I really hope you have learned it’s that 2D or 3D the basic con‑ cepts are all the same, Space Invaders to Halo and everything in between pretty much all operate on the same principles, move something around under human control, avoiding things that can cause it harm, while getting the CPU to control something that is obstructing you in some way.
Games really are not massively different project to project. The main dif‑ ferences come from technical needs. Do we need 1000 models on screen with physics, or do we need our GPU to do part of the physics for us, are we going to use a detailed path tracing system for photo‑realistic graphics? Technical content can be a money pit in terms of your learning, and in some ways it will never end, because tech always changes, and eventually it will slip away from even the most die‑hard tech nerd. We’ve had to do some tech stuff, because well, we needed to have at least the basics in place to allow us to see things moving around. But game programming is a multi‑faceted skill set, and tech is only one part of it which you are more than welcome to dive into and find even more pleasure in some of the more modern hardware. Unless you are working directly for a console or GPU maker, you’ll always be slightly behind the curve.
But focus on gameplay understand the principles of gameplay and how games are put together and you can stay ahead of the curve because gameplay really does not change as rapidly as tech.
We’ll move into the home straight by working with a few of the technical things we need to know, mainly because if we ignore them our resources will vanish. Taking what we know so far and refining it, will allow us to make the games a bit faster, smoother or just a bit more shiny.
Hopefully, your confidence levels are high enough now for us to tackle pretty much anything, and even if you don’t quite understand something your ability to research and learn online will be improving.





432	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
Some Extras from OpenGLES 3.1 New Shaders
Now, one thing we can have a little talk about is the fact we have a couple of new types of shaders in OpenGLES 3.1 but… One is quite cool, and one is less useful. I’m again not going to go into a lot of details since our games don’t lend them‑ selves too much to having them in place, this is where we do finally start to see some serious limitations on a Pi compared to a full‑fat OpenGL PC/Mac.
Oh, and be careful, these shaders may not work directly as typed on a PC, due to them being very Raspberry OpenGLES 3.1 specific but they are very easy to correct, if you have been studying shaders so far. You have been studying shad‑ ers, haven’t you? Oh well, the support site will advise.
There is one other type of modern shader, called Tessellation Shaders (TS), which is sometimes available on OpenGLES 3.1/3.2 as an extension. But it’s not available on the current Pi4/5’s and it would probably not be available if it was ever updated to OpenGLES 3.2, since it is quite an intensive process that lower‑powered GPUs struggle with. So, we won’t cover those here even though our PC code could handle it, consider it a homework assignment you can do alone and boast about on the forums.

Compute Shaders
We know now that our GPU can do complex maths and store the results in buf‑ fers that we reload as textures. This is perfectly nice for graphic data, but compute shaders allow for the GPU to do some other types of multi‑threaded job tasks and take advantage of its very much more eficient floating‑point processing.
Essentially, we can use the GPU as a co‑processing system that we can ofload tasks to if the tasks are able to take advantage of parallel processing. And there is the first problem, we don’t really have anything in the samples we are doing that would really benefit from this but there’s a bigger problem. A Pi’s GPU has far less processing cores than a PC’s video card, and… its clocked far slower than our CPU. So ermm… it’s all a bit pointless on a Pi, even though it’s there, can be fun to play with, it’s not actually going to provide any advantage over a standard multi‑core CPU job manager on a Pi.
Setting up compute shaders and understanding what they do, is quite com‑ plex, and given that I really don’t think they will add anything to our current level of ability, I am not going to discuss them here. There’s a LOT of cool online content you can try out, but only your PC builds will benefit.
So, we’ll cheat and ofload content using compute shaders to the support site, if there’s any reader interest in it.

Geometry Shaders
After the disappointment of Tessellation and Compute Shaders, we do have a new shader we can use! Though this is an OpenGL 3.2 feature, we do have extensions on almost all versions of OpenGLES 3.1 and indeed the Pi4/5 also supports them. Geometry Shaders (GS) add another step into the shader pipeline but it’s a pretty simple step and works only on the collection of vertices that form a primitive to add extra levels of geometry rather than additional vertices and fragment points. And we do have a few situations in our current games where we can play with these. GSs as the name suggests allow us to modify our geometry, specifically by modifying the data input we provide as vertices. Normally, we don’t really get to do a lot with our vertices, our VS inputs them and then some maths gets done to work out position data, and maybe a few frag coord concepts and data is sent

Graphics Need a Boost	433
out to the FS. But the vertex is for the most part a single constant value we don’t get to mess with or even ignore. We don’t even get to know too much about other vertices it may be connected to that make up a triangle.
The GS, however, allows us to make some decisions on those vertices, drop them, change them, or even cooler, add more vertices to create new primitives or expand on our shapes.
GSs are an optional concept; they sit between our normal Vertex Shaders and work on the output data given to them. This means if we are drawing trian‑ gles, the Vertex Shaders must do their job three times to produce three points for the next shader to work with. A GS always gets those three points to work with before it has to pass modified versions to the Fragment Shader.
GSs do have lots of uses in games, we can do cool effects like Billboards, more GPU‑based particles, debug lines and some very cool explosion effects, to name but 4 (yeah, ok, I only really do those 4 so… that’s all I know, there are oth‑ ers but let’s just admit my ignorance here and move on, ok?).
A Vertex Shader normally sends its data to a Fragment Shader by sending gl_Position or using an out for a value it calculates. If, however, we have a GS in place, we should make a different type of out, called an interface block, where we group the outputs, like a C++ struct. It’s not compulsory, but it is advisable, I think it also has some internal speed implications.
Here’s a simple example of some shader code, where we take a cube or ball (something simple) and make it “breathe” over a period.

// Vertex Shader
#version 310 es // extension needed for Pi precision highp float;
#extension GL_EXT_shader_io_blocks : require // allows us to use extension blocks

// Attributes are now in for GLES3.2 in vec3 position;
in vec3 normal; in vec2 texCoord;

// We only need the model matrix here. uniform mat4 modelMatrix;

// Outputs to geometry shader, wrapped in a tidy interface block, grouping our outs
out VS_OUT {
vec3 position; vec3 normal; vec2 texCoord;
} vs_out;

void main() {
// Simply transform to world space and pass to geometry shader vs_out.position = (modelMatrix * vec4(position, 1.0)).xyz; vs_out.normal = mat3(modelMatrix) * normal;
vs_out.texCoord = texCoord; }

/* Geometry Shader, is new, and takes the interface block, as well as the triangle
That the last 3 vertex shaders produces*/

#version 310 es precision highp float;
#extension GL_GEOMETRY_SHADER_OES : require // we need both of these to use GS

434	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
#extension GL_EXT_shader_io_blocks : require // and here the IO block

layout(triangles) in;
layout(triangle_strip, max_vertices = 3) out; // were going to output a strip

// Input from vertex shader in our block in VS_OUT {
vec3 position; vec3 normal; vec2 texCoord;
} gs_in[]; // notice this is an array, its actually the 3(triangle) vertices

// Output to fragment shader in a block but the same concepts we have from vertex
out GS_OUT {
vec3 position; vec3 normal; vec2 texCoord;
} gs_out;
// Now we’re going to do a bit more work here so need these

uniform mat4 viewMatrix; uniform mat4 projectionMatrix; uniform float time;

vec3 calculateBreathing(vec3 position, vec3 normal, vec3 triangleCenter) {
// Primary breathing along normals float primaryBreathSpeed = 2.0; float primaryBreathAmount = 0.1;
float primaryDisplacement = primaryBreathAmount * sin(time * primaryBreathSpeed);

// Secondary pulsing effect float pulseSpeed = 3.0; float pulseAmount = 0.05;
float pulseScale = 1.0 + pulseAmount * cos(time * pulseSpeed);

// Calculate radial breathing from triangle center vec3 toVertex = position ‑ triangleCenter;
float distanceFromCenter = length(toVertex); float radialBreathSpeed = 4.0;
float radialBreathAmount = 0.03;
float radialFactor = 1.0 + radialBreathAmount * sin(time * radialBreathSpeed + distanceFromCenter);

// Combine all effects
vec3 normalOffset = normal * primaryDisplacement;
vec3 scaledPosition = triangleCenter + toVertex * pulseScale; vec3 radialPosition = triangleCenter + (scaledPosition ‑
triangleCenter) * radialFactor;

return radialPosition + normalOffset; }

void main() {
// Calculate triangle centerpoint vec3 triangleCenter =
(gs_in[0].position+gs_in[1].position+gs_in[2].position)/3.0;

// Calculate average normal for coherent breathing, gives us a line to move along


Graphics Need a Boost	435
vec3 avgNormal = normalize(gs_in[0].normal + gs_in[1].normal + gs_in[2].normal);

for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
// Apply breathing transformations, in/out along a normal line vec3 finalPosition = calculateBreathing(
gs_in[i].position, avgNormal, triangleCenter
);

// Transform to clip space
gl_Position = projectionMatrix * viewMatrix * vec4 (finalPosition, 1.0);

// Pass through interpolated attributes gs_out.texCoord = gs_in[i].texCoord; gs_out.normal = gs_in[i].normal; gs_out.position = finalPosition;

EmitVertex(); //The cool bit, we send a new vertex in gs_out[i] to FS
}
EndPrimitive(); // and this ends the sending of new vertices }


// Fragment Shader, we keep it simple most of the maths was done in GS. #version 310 es
precision highp float;
#extension GL_EXT_shader_io_blocks : require

// Input from geometry shader in GS_OUT {
vec3 position; vec3 normal; vec2 texCoord;
} fs_in;

// Uniforms
uniform sampler2D diffuseTexture; uniform vec3 lightPosition; uniform vec3 viewPosition;

// Output is simple fragColor out vec4 fragColor;

void main() {
// Normalize interpolated normal
vec3 normal = normalize(fs_in.normal);

// Calculate lighting direction
vec3 lightDir = normalize(lightPosition ‑ fs_in.position);

// Calculate view direction
vec3 viewDir = normalize(viewPosition ‑ fs_in.position);

// lets do full lighting here // Ambient lighting
float ambientStrength = 0.2;
vec3 ambient = ambientStrength * vec3(1.0);

// Diffuse lighting
float diff = max(dot(normal, lightDir), 0.0);

436	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
vec3 diffuse = diff * vec3(1.0);

// Specular lighting
float specularStrength = 0.5;
vec3 reflectDir = reflect(‑lightDir, normal);
float spec = pow(max(dot(viewDir, reflectDir), 0.0), 32.0); vec3 specular = specularStrength * spec * vec3(1.0);

// Combine lighting with texture
vec4 texColor = texture(diffuseTexture, fs_in.texCoord); vec3 lighting = ambient + diffuse + specular;

fragColor = vec4(texColor.rgb * lighting, texColor.a); }

The Vertex and Fragment Shaders are pretty much what we are doing already, but we did add that little #extension GL_EXT_shader_io_blocks : require to let us make use of the IO block structure, so they should be familiar, and yes, we could easily employ the same ideas in a Vertex Shader, and move the vertices as part of our calculations, but this adds to our flexibility and can give us even more options to work on a primitive collection instead of 1 vertex. Also, our GS is an optional extra.
There’s a demo on the support site to show how this is set up and working, but you can see this ability to mess with vertices in a different shader and then send them to our fragment is a huge bonus.
Now again hopefully you know all this can be done just with a Vertex Shader, pushing vertices back and forth along a face normal isn’t especially complex. Let’s pull back that thought we put on hold. We’re not really doing anything too extreme here and really, it’s hard to see what the benefit is. We stuck another shader into the pipeline and made it do more work, isn’t that going to be slow? Certainly, an extra step causes the GPU to take more time, so where’s the benefit? Well, consider that it’s possible to have our GS take in, and send out, different
types of data.
We can take in several types of data, OpenGLES 3.1 defines these are our main types of draw formats as defined in our OpenGLES 3.1 header;

#define GL_POINTS	0x0000 #define GL_LINES	0x0001 #define GL_LINE_LOOP	0x0002 #define GL_LINE_STRIP	0x0003 #define GL_TRIANGLES	0x0004 #define GL_TRIANGLE_STRIP	0x0005 #define GL_TRIANGLE_FAN	0x0006

Our  GS  can  happily  accept  these  formats…  but  it  can  only  output,  point lists(strips), line strips and triangle strips. Did you notice the word strips?
Also notice that the GS specifies what it is going to output and…dramatic pause… How many! We limit to 3 at the moment because we just want a triangle, but we can actually have quite a few, the exact number depends on the drivers, but 256 is the normal default.

layout(triangle_strip, max_vertices =  3) out; // we output 3.. but we can do more

This means, we can define small shapes with just a single point, and output a triangle strip to make a quad, or a cube, or anything else we can define simply by emitting each vertex. But quads and small polygons are enough to be going on with. Generating quads or other shapes like this in the shader is very fast on the


Graphics Need a Boost	437
Pi, because remember the Pi has painfully slow memory access, so when loading attributes to a Vertex Shader, if we can arrange to only send one vertex, to repre‑ sent a corner point, and then have the GS itself work out the other three points of a quad. We are going to (almost1) avoid three painfully slow memory transfers.
Quads are quite a poor example as it’s possible to use textured points to rep‑ resent quads as well, and those are pretty fast, but small regular shapes, hexagons for example to make snowflakes, but also 3D shapes like pyramids or cubes are quite possible before we start to overdo things.
And that… is why Geometry Shaders are useful on a Pi, as long as you don’t go mad and try to build a torus for every point, you can make particles, sprites and billboards, just by having a VBO of points. We save so much time by not hav‑ ing to load from the memory, and we can build up shapes from simple primitives.

Classic Uses of Geometry Shaders
There are two really cool things you can do with Geometry Shaders that we must talk about: Visualising Normals and Explosions.
Both are possible because a Geometry Shader has access to all the vertices of their primitive at the same time, so for a triangle, we get all three points acces‑ sible by the shader. When we have all three points, we can do some maths and calculate the normal of the face.
Three points allow us to work out the edges and centre of the triangle and our ability to generate points mean we can emit a vertex at that point, and another at a point somewhere along the normal and we draw a line to visualise the normal. This is usually a classic debug tool, to check that your faces are correct, but it can also be used for various other effects, even if you want to take it to extremes. A face on a cube is made of two triangles, and each one has a centre point to allow us to draw a normal from there, on a simple cube we get a nice example of the Geometry Shader being used to generate and visualise the normal line (Figure 10.3).



















Figure 10.3  All faces have normals.

1  We must, however, accept that those emitted points are stored somewhere in the GPUs memory which is slow.


438	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
And explosions can be done with the same basic idea; a known centre point normal line allows us to move the entire triangle along that normal line over time, to create some extremely nice explosion effect, which works on any mesh you care to feed it. You may have the normal already from the VBO passed to the Vertex Shader, but this works on the simple fact you get all the triangle vertices from any primitive in the mesh. It’s the same principle as the breathing demo (Figure 10.4).
























Figure 10.4 A simple expansion.


Of course, you can mix this up by adding some concept of limited varia‑ tion along the line to slightly alter the direction of the normal, since explosions generally are not so rigid and mechanically accurate to cleanly follow a normal path. More variation and more faces along with some inserted animated fire and smoke textures can create a very effective scattering of a model’s faces to simulate explosions (Figures 10.5 and 10.6).














Graphics Need a Boost	439





















Figure 10.5  Putting a twist on it.
























Figure 10.6  Putting a twist on it +0.2seconds.






440	The Fundamentals of C/C++ Game Programming
I won’t put any code in here you can see it all in the demo on the support site.

Caution
Now these new types of shaders are very cool to play with and when used care‑ fully can give you a big performance boost, even Tessellation when it becomes available, though you’d be hard pressed to find any situation where you’d get a benefit from a compute shader on a Pi4/5 (brilliant on a PlayStation 4/5 though). I suppose if you have all four cores on your Pi screaming through a job manager at 100% you could spare some setup time to get a couple of noise texture setup in three frames time… but! CS, TS and GS are the very epitome of great power coming with great responsibility. Used sparingly and in correct situations you can gain a lot from them. Using them just for the sake of using them, will drop frame rates all over the place, though you might get a pretty movie render, which isn’t a real‑time application.






































Graphics Need a Boost	441


11


Populating the World














Keeping Track of the Assets
We’ve come a long way and in doing so we’ve started to notice the size and complexity of our content has increased dramatically. We can create a lot of different playfields to put our game into and we’ve discussed some of the standard physics and AI systems used in the majority of games.
But all these things come at a cost…Memory. Our target has a limited amount of onboard memory, and if we plan to target all possible variants, we must therefore work with the lowest spec system. In our case, a 2–8 GB Raspberry Pi4 Model B. Though who knows, we may want to use the Pi3 Model A with 256 MB. (Programmers traditionally count a high percentage of mas‑ ochists in their number).
