Appendix I















Working with Older Pi Models
During the first edition of this book, I worked mainly on the older Raspberry Pi systems, like the Raspberry 2b and 3b. These were nice machines but a lit‑ tle unusual in the way their graphic libraries worked and some of the memory arrangement. This meant we set up the OpenGLES2.0 render system a little dif‑ ferently from other SBCs) and standard Linux machines. The main code itself though runs just as well on any system, it’s only when trying to work on different hardware you get to have problems.
The Pi4 range and later are much more generic Linux systems and don’t really have any exceptions to standard to get things up and running.
Older Pis can still run most of the early code in this edition, but they can’t do any of the OpenGLES3.x content. They  also needs a slightly different setup to get a display surface. That might mean, some things are just not viable. Where possible and when it makes sense, I have provided some alternative OpenGLES2 versions of the shaders to allow the simpler projects to work, but mostly this book is about OpenGLES3.x and that’s just not that easy to emulate on an older Pi.
Since I have written most of my projects here on the Raspberry Pi4 series of machines, there were not so many things that were very specific to the Raspberry range. Older Raspberry models used in the first edition made extensive use of Broadcom’s graphic libraries, which enhance the standard EGL systems. In this edition, we’ve kept them in only for older Raspberry when the code is compatible. Raspberry OS, and older versions known as Raspbian, can often be run on other target SBCs, but it’s usually a badly ported and/or incomplete copy. It’s normally far better to find a clean version of Debian or Ubuntu, which was spe‑
cifically built for your target, it will tend to be a lot more stable.


463
It also means that some of the key libraries we need to build our project are not going to be where I’ve described them in our Raspberry Pi‑based tutorials. They may not even be part of the OS as it is supplied meaning you have to install them yourself.
I’ve covered quite a number of different SBCs available, on the support site, so feel free to check out the other SBCs section. If your board or OS is not cov‑ ered, leave a note on the forum, and hopefully, you will get a response, but once you know the basics of getting a target setup, the process isn’t markedly different on other systems.












































464	Appendix I





Appendix II















Updating vs New SD
When you burn an SD with any maker’s distro of an OS, you are basically creat‑ ing a brand new clean formatted SD card with the very latest version of the OS and any drivers needed to make your particular board run. This is of course a good thing. But as you spend a few weeks creating projects and using your target board for any number of fun things, you will find you add your own apps, librar‑ ies and useful assets to your SD card. These are lost when you burn a new SD.
Losing them each time a new OS becomes available and is burned to your SD, is annoying! Yes, you can save them, or re‑download them, but it’s really quite a tedious process, time should be spent coding not re‑installing apps and data. (Backups… remember to use backups for all personal data).
Unless the distro from the maker actually fixes a known issue with the core use of your target machine, try not to continually burn new SD cards. Rather, focus on an update and upgrade cycle every so often to make sure you have the latest version of your apps and libraries.
Linux systems keep track of the current version numbers of all your apps, and using the following will make sure your system is updated with any latest apps and libs. You should also always do this immediately after you have burned and re‑partitioned a new SD.

sudo apt‑get update && sudo apt‑get upgrade

This will keep your board public drivers and all your onboard apps up‑to‑date and functioning. Do this every so often just to make sure you have the latest versions of things. I don’t however recommend this in the middle of a project



465
development session. Sometimes, changes to libraries can be quite major, causing new ways to access features, and your code itself might then need to be altered in ways that are not always obvious.
It’s also a very good idea to keep a backup of your SD on your PC from time to time, with all its current updates and the tools and libraries we install. It’s not at all unknown for coders to damage their storage medium, especially when doing some form of media access systems that forget to close files or accidentally format things. Having a backup of your SD, available in that unhappy event, will save you a lot of time updating and reinstalling your working tools.












































466	Appendix II





Appendix III















A Word or Two About Source Control
I’m hoping you have come here from early in the book, where I thought about putting this section, but was concerned with scaring you off or overloading a beginner’s enthusiasm. So, let’s assume when you go through this, you’re still at a finding your feet stage and this is something you want to find out about, but the choice of what and when to use are still open to you.
Source control or revision control is a form of backup system all profession‑ als use, there are many different types, the most popular currently are SVN, Git and for professionals, Perforce.
Like most situations when there are two or more options. People tend to fall into one of the camps and defend it while putting down the others. It’s not for me to suggest a choice, I tend to use all three in different development environments, though I have a soft spot for SVN, even though it’s starting to be overtaken in popularity by Git.
Perforce is popular in commercial situations but it can be complex to use, for simple home coding I think it’s overkill but feel free to seek out single‑user free demo versions.
Whatever you choose you just need to remember one thing…use it!
How does it help us? Good question, I am glad you asked. As we go through this book, we are going to be building up a framework and creating many genera‑ tions of buildable files, we’re also going to make some almighty cock ups, typos, missed lines, attempts to do something different, etc., which are going to create bugs that we may not be able to immediately solve and will create considerable annoyance as we are trying to learn new things. Earlier in the book, I demon‑ strated how easy it is to make mistakes with our early memory management, and


467
those were deliberate! More mistakes are sure to follow, programmers are human and make mistakes; it’s going to be frustrating.
Frustration is the biggest drawback to learning I can think of, it’s important to build your confidence and sense of ease in coding, and bug hunting for seem‑ ingly impossible bugs is never anything else but frustration.
A source/revision control system will provide us with a means to step back to the last stable build we had before we made the error and start again. This is often the fastest way to deal with complex bugs, which are avoiding your bug‑squash‑ ing debug hammers.
This works best if you have regular backups of your code, in general if you write any small module of code, and it is running fine, back up and commit it to your online repository. Do this as many times as you want in each session, don’t leave it to the end of your session. EVERY time you write some decent sized chunk of code that works…submit your code to your server…then if you screw something up, you are at worst only going to lose the time it took to enter the faulty code, and you can be more careful when trying again to add the code to the last working version you had from 30 minutes ago.
Whatever system you use, you’re going to need a client side app. SVN and Git both have a very usable interface, and you can download SVN from here https:/ tortoisesvn.net/ and Git from here https:/tortoisegit.org/.
You will also need to locate an SVN or Git hosting server where your projects can be uploaded. These range in price from free to very much not free. But I won’t specifically recommend any. Simply Google for free SVN (or Git) hosting, and you’ll find something.
If you are network‑minded and have your own server, you can even host your repository yourself.
Perforce is a different kind of system, and in my view much more complex, especially for simple projects, but if you already are using it, you may want to con‑ tinue, it’s available from https://www.perforce.com/. It also needs a host server and again there are commercial ones available including some free ones.
Take some time to read the instructions on the sites and pick one. So long as you remember the golden rule of committing often, you will save yourself a lot of time and effort, especially when things go wrong.
If all this sounds confusing and scary at the moment, don’t worry, it will become clearer, for now, just work through the book carefully and just don’t cre‑ ate any bugs! No bugs at all, just, don’t!

















468	Appendix III





Appendix IV















Bits Bytes and Nibbles Make You Hungry!
I briefly touched on the topic of bits when we were discussing the colours we used to make the pixels. It’s a bit of a false view these days that computers only work in binary, 1’s and 0’s. It’s true they use binary a lot, but these days on modern com‑ puters not as much as they used to, except in one or two areas, such as Graphic and audio data.
I don’t honestly know the reasons why the basic units of computers have some connection to snacks, but these are the following basic units.
Bit, Nibble, Byte and Word, which traditionally were 1 binary digit (8 to a byte), 4 binary digits (2 to a byte), 8 binary digits and 16 binary digits (2 bytes combined). These were the limits when I started with 8‑bit machines. Over time as the sizes of units got larger as CPUs were able to process more new units such as longwords, 32, Doubles 64 etc, came to be used, memory size grew as did the sizes of registers, but the basic units still exist.
A bit is the smallest possible digit a computer can recognise, the traditional 1 or 0. But your targets memory is not laid out as a large number of 1’s and 0’s, which allows you to target any one at any time. It’s laid out in various sizes of chunks that the CPU or GPU grabs in 1 mouthful. When we talk of a 32‑bit or a 64‑bit computer, what we are actually saying is, the CPU grabs 32bits at a time and holds those 32bits as a number in its registers to manipulate any way it sees fit. 64bit machines grab 64bits. This wider address size has a side effect in that the code will grow in size; most variables will now be 64bits long rather than 32, pointers need to be 64bit and many of the machine’s instructions are also now 64bit increasing the actual size of our code. That’s something we need to think about, we generally still have the same amount of RAM available, and our code


469
will increase in size. It won’t double, but it will be more. But 64‑bit machines are usually much faster for various reasons and that is well worth the increased size. We also tend to consider that the term “word” now, is a 32‑bit value, as the old 16‑bit value has more or less been left behind as computers have grown in size and power.
Now most of the time we don’t really need to worry too much about the bit patterns of our 32‑bit words, but we do when it comes to graphics. Since a 32‑bit word contains 4 bytes of data, with 1 byte each containing information on the Red, Green, Blue and Alpha components of our pixels. It’s also contained in a specific part of our word. A red pixel for example might look like this in binary

11111111000000000000000011111111b

Oh, and to make it even more fun, it might be;

11111111000000000000000011111111b

Yeah…the same, the difference here is that the Alpha value is at the start, it’s a wonderful way to confuse a PC coder, make them look at ARM binary values. Oh, and let’s not forget that in C/C++ component colours and alpha values are floats between 0 and 1.0f.
All this depends on whether your CPU prefers to read data in a Big or Little endian format, ARM is actually capable of both for even more head‑exploding madness.
It can be hard to get our heads around the size of things, for example, is int 16,32 or 64 bits. The answer is yes, to all 3, but it totally depends on what machine you are running on. C++ has a very useful sizeof(x) function that returns the byte (8bit) size of any basic type and helps you to overcome some of the problems of how much space you need to allocate from time to time.


























470	Appendix IV





Appendix V















The Libs We Used
Despite not wanting to, we did use a few different libraries, some of which needed to be installed on our systems in order for us to work. Should you find you need to format your storage system and reinstall your OS, these libs will be lost and need to be re‑installed. So, here’s a short run down, in one place, of the libs you need to re‑install if you do have a fresh OS.
Always do an update/upgrade whenever you make a fresh install

sudo apt‑get update && sudo apt‑get upgrade

Then, the following libraries can be installed.

sudo apt‑get install pulseaudio sudo apt‑get install libopenal‑dev sudo apt‑get install libalut‑dev sudo apt‑get install libBullet‑dev sudo apt‑get install libogg‑dev

If you cannot find graphic drivers on your system, these may allow you to create the projects, but at possible sub‑optimal frame rates, and only if your system has access to the GPU. However, don’t include this if your SBC already comes with OpenGLES drivers, unless you are sure they are emulated, in which case it might update the system to a full or partial hardware system!

sudo apt‑get install libgles2‑mesa‑dev

In spite of being named libgles2, it will also include OpenGLES3.0+, but it can’t actually tell if your machine is capable of using them until you interrogate the GPU.


471
If you have a multi‑core CPU, you might want to try using OpenMP to han‑ dle some of your multi‑core work. It should already have OpenMP’s libs installed as it’s pretty much standard in multi‑core systems, but you can check by trying to install anyway, it will inform you that the current version is the latest (I’m actually pretty sure all multi‑core systems will have this as standard, but there’s always one). All single core system will still work and compile but you won’t see any benefit and indeed you might see a slowdown, as threads are being constantly set up and the CPU is time slicing rather than processing on idle cores.

sudo apt‑get install libgomp1

We also downloaded a couple of header‑style libraries which we then compiled to be part of the finished executable, so they will not be affected by a new stor‑ age setup. In this edition of the book, they were installed onto the target systems using git clone, to known directories to stay accessible to all projects. But in the previous edition, I also suggested installing them as source on your development system and including them in the build. Either approach is valid and I’ll let you decide, I’ve tended to move away from the project included version which can be found here;

SOIL,  or  more  correctly  the  STB  file  loading  was  downloaded  from https://github.com/nothings/stb

GLM The Maths Lib was downloaded from (but check for the latest versions) http://glm.g‑truc.net/0.9.9/index.html

Since edition 1, I’m now more comfortable with cloning the repositories directly to my Raspberry or other Target system with git clone, in your home/pi directory.

git clone https://github.com/nothings/stb.git git clone https://github.com/g‑truc/glm.git

I mainly do this because I have so many projects now, and with each having its own copy of STB and/or GLM it was taking up a lot of space with repeating con‑ tent, basically choking my source control back up with the same data 30+ times. It also speeds up the initial transfer of files since there’s no need to send them down on the 1st build of your project.
TinyObjLoader though is still best downloaded and included to be compiled in your project. It doesn’t update often but it does update often enough to keep your eyes open for changes and new features
https://github.com/syoyo/tinyobjloader.
Also adding to our arsenal of model loaders is Assimp, which in some cases is a direct replacement for TinyObjLoader but since it’s a much bigger library you may decide which of the libs is best suited to your needs. Generally, if you only need OBJ files, just use TinyObjLoader, for almost any other model format. Assimp is the way to go.
Assimp is available for download or git clone from https://github.com/ assimp/assimp

git clone https://github.com/assimp/assimp.git

Or if you are feeling brave you can find the source code to contribute to the proj‑ ect at http://www.assimp.org/index.php



472	Appendix V





Appendix VI















Writing My Own Games?
Oh boy, this is a biggie… I get asked so often by people, how do I write my own games, I’ve got a great idea? Well technically the answers are in here, everything you need to know is here and can be built on, except for that one crucial thing.
A vision!
There’s no real way to get past this, but most programmers don’t have that vision, we see the project as a series of logical and technical problems, which must be overcome in order to do… what?
Without vision, the clear and full picture of what the game is going to be, starting out on a technical path of super‑fast fancy wizardry, is going to be wasted and frustrating.
I’ve tried to simulate vision by using game genres we should all be familiar with, so you can do even a basic bit of research to understand what the games are hoping to turn out like, but if I were to suggest to you, we do a game about Doggies Piloting Atomic Power Balloons, In Space! You might quite rightly scratch your head in confusion.
This is why we have designers, those happy smiling people who want you to deliver the moon on a stick and convince you that making it 30% more fun is somehow possible.
The days when we could take a cool programming effect and turn it into a game are largely, gone, despite the occasional exception (I’m looking at you Flappy Bird)! Games need to have a clear outline of what they plan to be.
So, if you have an idea for a game, the 1st thing you should be doing is writ‑ ing it down on paper, planning it out. Identify the goals and aims of the game. What will the player be asked to achieve, how will that be done?


473
What are the aims of the AI, what obstacles will be put in the way, what will be the progression of the game? There are a great many design questions you need to answer before you touch a coding keyboard in anger. Be fully aware of what are the tasks you need to tackle, the scale of them, and most importantly, the feasibil‑ ity of them. Some things are just not possible on the hardware you choose, are there alternative means to do them?
Once you have planned your game, consider its core makeup. I’ve put for‑ ward the notion that all games are basically variations on Space Invaders, but of course, some games bend that notion to near breaking point. At the core of the game, technically it will still have a main loop, taking input, processing and rendering, so you can take some comfort in that. The issue is how do you create a play world, what will be in it, and how will they all interact.
Focus on those points, be clear about what it will look like, how the objects move or are moved, what influences will the user have in the game, and what random factors will it have.
Once you have that vision, get a designer to check it out for you…they’ll tear it apart for sure, but that’s good. You need to be sure that what you want is going to be fun, playable and possible.



































474	Appendix VI





Appendix VII















Release Builds vs Debug Builds
This one is a biggie, so take care with this. If you plan to “release” your games into the wild, you need to make sure you send people a Release Build, not a Debug Build.
There are two reasons for this:

1.  Debug builds, as you might expect are designed to be easy to debug and have quite a lot of code plugged in from standard libs to allow you to inter‑ cept functions/methods and even send and interpret variable data so you can display it on your debugger. The breakpoints match the code, and you can step through the code line by line at the equivalent memory address the build produced. The compiler also puts spaces in the code that do nothing but provide small gaps to plug in debug code, etc. Do you want your end users to be able to do something similar, and possibly insert code that hack‑ ers can use to gain access to a user’s machine? I’m going to guess, no?

2.  Debug builds are not optimised, they can’t really be, since the code needs to be laid out clearly and easily for the debugger and the coder to access it. If the compiler has made choices that code can be faster by unwrap‑ ping loops or avoiding intermediate variables, or removing a function whose return value wasn’t actually used but it was called. Then, the fin‑ ished code isn’t going to match the code you wrote, and debugging is thereafter, more guesswork than science.

The biggest point though is that optimisation. Release builds can be set to compile in such a way that the compiler is trusted to fully scan the code and make choices


475
that make the code faster and/or more compact. If the code has been written well, then a Release Build is something to be welcomed and indeed you may get quite an impressive boost to speed in your final build, and compactness can be assured if all unused code or data is trimmed out of your final builds. Compiling the code though is often very slow, sometimes a matter of two or three cups of tea, slow.
So, once you have a finished game, it makes perfect sense to create a release build, the main downside is that such builds tend to be very slow, which isn’t such a big deal if we’re only doing it once.
But once built it will just run faster, won’t it? Won’t it?
Won’t it?
Why am I stretching this out for comedic effect?
Well yes, most of the time it will indeed run faster often 10%–30% faster depending on how many little tweaks the compiler can find, but… A Debug Build sets up lots of things for you on the assumption you are going to put them right later. For example, Warnings on a Debug Build can be largely ignored, the com‑ piler will include some boot code, that will clear out memory and set up a blank space for you to run your code. A Release Build does not have that debug boot code. It loads and runs in whatever state it is in. So all memory is unallocated and raw, leaving you at risk of random memory values when you normally assumed a nullptr or 0. Unclear typedefs or memory, that a debugger might warn you about now become a Release builds nightmare as it will make no assumptions that your code is properly formatted, it just runs what it finds and if it crashes… it crashes… And here’s the bad bit... It has no way to tell you why it crashed because you have no debugger code in there to catch such errors.
So, this is pretty terrifying, but, as long as you keep to a few simple rules you should not have too much of a problem. The biggest point is never  assume the memory is blank, always declare the default value of variables, always… Got that? Never set your warnings to ignore, and fix them as soon as you see them, if you wait till the end of your project to switch warnings back on. You’re going to find
a few extra weeks of work correcting them all when you thought you were done. While still producing debug builds, as soon as your project is usable, TEST
your project, TEST it again, then get someone else to TEST it… Fully testing a Debug Build allows you to be fairly confident your code is working so that when/if the Release Build fails you can try to isolate a technical rather than a coding error. Once you have that Debug Build up and running confidently, make a regular release build to check for any issues and try to fix them rather than accumulate them at the end…1 or 2 fixes a week, is far easier than 200 at the end. Remember the slow compile makes the turnaround much more labour intensive. Do it at the end of a day, or week depending on the project. And again, TEST it. Don’t let
Release Build bugs accumulate.
Debugging a release build is the most annoying thing ever and you have to make some best guesses to where the cause is by sometimes adding little plug in, “I got here before it crashed” type output hooks to find where it’s crashing.
Visual Studio Debugger will still “work”, but at only a fraction of its effec‑ tiveness on a Release Build. Mainly you can look at memory and breakpoint at the start of a function, but there’s no longer a direct tie to the code on screen you wrote and most of the console code is debug content that will be optimised away as no longer used. So, your little output hooks are best sent to your screen, you can’t rely on the console output to still be present. We’ve done this on a couple of our later projects.


476	Appendix VII
By far the most important thing is NEVER send a Debug Build into the wild. Your code can be easily disassembled, added to and some of the copyright con‑ tent you have, libraries, assets, etc., may become vulnerable to extraction.

Thanks
There are a lot of people to thank for this book, but top of the list as always for me is my daughter, Danielle. Who has somewhat reluctantly featured in the credit list of every game I’ve written since she was born. Giving her the dubious distinc‑ tion of numerous mentions, on several game credit sites, without ever having any interest in playing or making computer games.
The addition in December 2015 of her son Harvey, my 1st Grandchild, and in March 2019, Eva, her daughter and my second Grandchild gives me even more cause to consider her the greatest achievement of my life, games being a very dis‑ tant second, or probably third as I am quite proud of my guitar collection, though not my actual playing!
Thanks to my friend Professor Penny De Byl for helping me to find a means to publish this nonsense, and her help with checking my concept and invaluable advice and encouragement on what to add and take away from the original con‑ cept to keep it fun and interesting.
Thanks also to my friend and former colleague, Jamie Stewart for taking the time to go through the book at different times and comment on any mistakes I made, deliberate or otherwise.
Thanks to Grumpy old Git developers (Facebook group, not an insult) Gareth Lewis, Rob Wilmot and Paul Carter, Paul for helping me find and convert some low poly Car models for producing some nice LOD versions of the cars. Gareth, for his timely help with a Raspbian compatible key reading routine when I was on the point of throwing the Raspberry Pi out the window, and also to my former student Petar Dimitrov, who came up with a neat keyboard scan system to determine which keyboard event was actually active, which was much tidier than the one I had, so I shamelessly stole it, with his consent. Sadly I have since replaced those key systems, but they still made the 1st edition possible.
Thanks also to old friends Shaun McClure and Ocean Legend Simon Butler for their pixel‑pushing prowess on the 2D art you can find on the site and use, and Colin Morrison for his 3D racetrack tiles and a few other models I wasn’t able to fit in, but you can find on the site.
I have to give a huge shout‑out to the incredibly talented Pim Bos, one of our families of BUas graduates who studied Visual Arts when he was with us. He did the cartoons that illustrate the book. His fun take on complex concepts is inspir‑ ing and made me chuckle every time he sent one in.
A special shout out to the small band of unsuspecting volunteers who ran through both books for me, finding multiple spelling errors and more than a few issues with my coding, especially to colleague David Jones who I now owe free drinks for life for his proofreading and eye for detail.
Finally, my thanks to the management, staff and all students past and pres‑ ent at the International Games Architecture and Design (IGAD) programme of Breda University of Applied Sciences in Breda, The Netherlands, which has been my home for the last 17 years. They changed the name to CMGT, but it’s always going to be IGAD to me. I’ve learned much from them and I hope I’ve given a little bit back at times.
